Sergio Costa: do we have BGP/anycast anomalies? (I know our anycast is limited, but DNS steer might’ve changed pathing.)

Victor Alvarez: DNS TTL drop went out yesterday (per the terraform PR). could be more clients re-resolving and landing on different paths. but this is probes (not clients), so less likely.

Emily Zhao: FYI I checked AWS “Global Network” dashboard (the internal one). it shows “increased latency” between EU and US around that time but no incident ID. screenshot in #eng-sre already? I can post here if useful.

Irene Choi: please post.

Emily Zhao: uploading now… (screenshot: EU-WEST->US-EAST latency band elevated, no red status).

Rafael Mendes: that’s helpful. suggests provider-level symptom without formal incident.

Vanessa Ortiz: from capacity side, us-east had ~28% headroom at time of trigger; eu-west had ~35%. so not a capacity exhaustion artifact.

Keiko Yamamoto: could still be local endpoint overload causing handshake delays (SYN backlog). do we see SYN cookies / listen queue overruns on the ALB/NLB?

Peter Holtz: NLB metrics: `TCP_Target_Reset_Count` modest bump but not huge. `NewFlowCount` stable. feels network-y.

Victor Alvarez: agreed. also mtr shows loss before hitting our edge.

Miles Thornton: action item: in game day checklist, add “snapshot provider network view” + “capture mtr from canary nodes” as required evidence when reachability flips. helps tune corroboration without hand-waving.

Allison Grant: +1. also, our reachability alert currently pages at 2% failed probes over 5m. might want a longer hold-down / require sustained p95 handshake > X ms (not just failures).

Sergio Costa: careful: longer windows slow detection for real blackholes. maybe do dual-threshold: fast trip at high failure (>=15%), slower corroboration at low failure + high latency.

Victor Alvarez: makes sense. for today’s event, it was “low fail + high latency” category.

Irene Choi: also, we should tag these as “provider symptom suspected” in audit events? at least a freeform note field. enterprise asks “what happened” and we need something better than “reachability bad”.

Rafael Mendes: we can add a runbook step: “check AWS status/PHD + internal global network dashboard” and link it in the incident-bot summary.

Emily Zhao: fwiw, symptoms look resolved now. re-running mtr from euw1 is clean: 0% loss, avg ~92ms, no timeouts.

Victor Alvarez: great. that validates reachability signal wasn’t totally bogus. it caught a transient transit issue.

Peter Holtz: gateway connect timeouts back to baseline as well.

Miles Thornton: can we drop this into the post-gameday notes? especially the asymmetry + no-PHD-event detail.

Victor Alvarez: yep, I’ll paste summary into the retro doc + link this thread.

Irene Choi: thanks all. :thumbsup: (reaction :thumbsup: from Irene Choi)

Allison Grant: I’ll open a small follow-up for alert tuning + evidence capture fields (Sergio/Miles I’ll loop you).

source.content:
Irene Choi: Kicking off a thread on Dedicated drain automation approach for game day + longer-term. Goal: get a safe, repeatable sequence for draining a Dedicated GPU pool in a region when we’re failing over routing. Want to avoid customer-visible stalls + avoid accidental overload in target region.

Rafael Mendes: +1. From SRE POV we need: (a) deterministic steps, (b) hard safety gates, (c) ability to stop/rollback quickly. Today it’s too manual and people do steps in different orders.

Miles Thornton: Proposed principle: routing decision first, drain second. But “routing first” can mean “stop sending new traffic” not “move all traffic immediately.” I think we need a two-phase: quarantine + drain.

Mina Haddad: On routing side we can implement a per-tenant/per-pool “no-new-assignments” state. Existing connections can continue until timeout, but new requests won’t land there.

Ravi Menon: Yep. Think of it as policy engine predicate `pool_state != DRAINING`. If DRAINING, route away unless tenant is pinned and no healthy alternative (then fail closed w/ explicit 503 + retry-after).

Vanessa Ortiz: Capacity guardrail is the big one. If we route away before target region has headroom, we create a thundering herd on target pools. Can we gate the start of quarantine on “target headroom >= X for Y minutes”?

Keiko Yamamoto: Autoscaling is not instantaneous; “headroom >= X” needs to include pending capacity + warm-up time. Suggest we use two gates: (1) current headroom gate, (2) projected headroom gate from scaler (e.g., within 10 min). If projected fails, automation should stop and ask for human.

Benji Okafor: I want explicit safety limits:
- max drain operations per hour per tenant
- max % of total dedicated capacity we can put into DRAINING globally
- require corroboration signals if this is triggered automatically (region health + error burn)
Also: always support dry-run output of what we *would* drain and why.

Peter Holtz: From gateway perspective, if pool is DRAINING, we should return `503` + `Retry-After` rather than allowing long timeouts. Otherwise clients retry aggressively and we amplify load. This should be part of the drain workflow.

Tomáš Novák: + add a runbook step: verify circuit breaker config and retry budgets before pressing “execute drain.” We had this bite us in staging.

Ethan Park: Control plane angle: we need an idempotent “drain pool” endpoint with a job token, and status transitions that are monotonic:
ACTIVE -> QUARANTINED -> DRAINING -> DRAINED
and a separate “CANCEL_DRAIN” that can go QUARANTINED/DRAINING back to ACTIVE if safety gates fail.

Victor Alvarez: Network note: when we route away, some clients will keep hitting the region due to DNS caching / Anycast weirdness. So “no-new-assignments” needs to be enforced server-side too, not just via routing policy. Assume some % of traffic will still land.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. For Dedicated, we can’t rely on client behavior. Need enforcement at the ingress + scheduler.

Miles Thornton: OK, here’s a concrete sequence proposal. Feedback welcome.

Miles Thornton: Draft sequence (automation controller):
1) Pre-flight checks (hard fail if any fail):
   - confirm target region for tenant/pool is HEALTHY from region-health-service
   - confirm `target_headroom_pct >= 25%` for 5m OR `projected_headroom_pct >= 25%` within 10m (Vanessa/Keiko to tune)
   - confirm no active incident for target region (manual override allowed)
   - confirm policy engine config checksum is current (avoid stale rules)
2) Enter QUARANTINED:
   - routing: set `no_new_assignments=true` for the source pool
   - gateway: enable retry-after for this pool’s routes (Peter)
   - emit audit event: reason, evidence signals, operator (or automation)
3) Stabilize window (hold-down):
   - wait 2–3 minutes and verify:
     - new request rate to source pool drops below threshold
     - error rate does not spike > budget
     - target region p95 latency stays < threshold
4) Start DRAINING:
   - control plane marks pool DRAINING
   - scheduler stops launching new replicas in pool
   - autoscaler: freeze scale-up in source (optional), allow scale-down only
   - begin eviction of long-lived workers w/ max drain rate (e.g., N nodes/min)
5) Validation:
   - wait until in-flight req count < M and active workers < K
   - verify dedicated SLO burn rate stays below critical
6) Mark DRAINED:
   - pool is out of rotation entirely
   - post summary to incident-bot thread w/ links to dashboards
7) Rollback conditions (any time):
   - target region p99 latency > threshold for 3m
   - target 5xx burn > fast-burn threshold
   - drain progress stalls (no decrease in workers for 10m)
   - safety limit exceeded (Benji’s caps)

Vanessa Ortiz: Looks good but I’d prefer to compute headroom in “tokens/sec” not % of GPUs. Some tenants run heavier models.

Mina Haddad: If we key headroom by model profile, routing can choose the right target pool. We can do a “compatibility set” + “capacity score.” But for v1 of drain automation maybe keep it simple and only allow drain if tenant has a pre-provisioned secondary pool in target region.

Ravi Menon: +1. For Dedicated, I’d like to avoid “best-effort” cross-pool routing in emergencies unless we’re very confident in capacity. Otherwise we violate isolation expectations.

Rafael Mendes: On SRE side: I want the sequence to require an explicit “tenant allowlist” for automated drain in prod initially. Default is manual approval.

Benji Okafor: Yep. Feature flag: `dedicated_drain_automation_enabled` by tenant_tier + tenant_id. And separate flag for “auto-execute” vs “recommend-only”.

Keiko Yamamoto: Drain rate needs to be conservative. If we evict too aggressively, we spike cold-starts in target. Suggest initial cap: 1 node/minute per pool, and global cap 5 nodes/minute across region.

Peter Holtz: On gateway behavior: when pool is QUARANTINED we should still serve *existing* streaming connections. When DRAINING, we should not accept new streams. Need to ensure server sets a clear error code (maybe `region_draining`) so SDKs can reconnect to new region.

Tomáš Novák: Can we add in the automation: “announce state change” with a Slack post + a dashboard annotation? On-call needs to see exactly when QUARANTINED started.

Devon Price: I can wire that into incident-bot. If controller emits an audit event, bot can post summary + auto-annotate Grafana via our webhook. Fields needed: tenant, pool, state, reason, operator, links.

Ethan Park: Implementation detail: the drain endpoint should support a “dry_run=true” that returns computed impacts: estimated drain time, target capacity check results, affected routes. That will help game day rehearsal.

Victor Alvarez: Another gotcha: cross-region egress constraints. When traffic shifts, we might saturate interconnect. We should include a network signal gate (packet loss / reachability probes) before starting drain.

Miles Thornton: Good call. We can use perf-canary cross-region reachability probes as corroboration. Maybe not a hard gate at first, but at least a warning + include in audit event.

Rafael Mendes: For game day: please include a “stop button” that returns pool to ACTIVE without waiting for drain completion. If we see target region degradation, we need to stop the drain and potentially re-open the source.

Mina Haddad: Routing can flip `no_new_assignments=false` quickly, but we should keep a cooldown timer to prevent flap. ADR-043 is proposing max flips/hour.

Benji Okafor: Exactly. Rollback should be “fast but not oscillatory.” Suggest: allow one immediate rollback if safety gate trips, then hold-down 15m before any new drain attempt.

Vanessa Ortiz: + also ensure rollback doesn’t leave us half-drained with insufficient capacity on source. If we evicted nodes already, we may need to freeze and stop further evictions, not necessarily bring capacity back instantly.

Keiko Yamamoto: We can implement rollback as: stop evictions + re-enable scheduling on remaining nodes. Scaling back up is separate (and might require warm-up). So rollback != “instant restore,” but at least stops making it worse.

Peter Holtz: From client impact: better to stop the churn quickly. Even partial restore can stabilize retries.

Tomáš Novák: Action items?

Miles Thornton: Proposed AIs:
- (Ethan) spec the drain endpoint state machine + idempotency + dry-run payload
- (Mina/Ravi) define routing behavior for QUARANTINED vs DRAINING, including failure mode when no target exists
- (Vanessa/Keiko) define headroom gates (current + projected) and drain rate caps
- (Peter) gateway behavior + error codes + retry-after semantics for QUARANTINED/DRAINING
- (Devon) audit event -> incident-bot + dashboard annotation plumbing
- (Rafael/Tomáš) runbook draft: pre-flight checks, go/no-go, rollback steps

Irene Choi: Sounds right. Please keep v1 conservative: allowlist + manual approval in prod. Once we have game day evidence + SLO outcomes, we can loosen. I’ll sync with Sean on expectations.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Let’s aim to have an end-to-end “recommend-only” run in staging by next week.

Devon Price: If someone can share the audit event schema draft, I’ll stub the bot formatting.

Ethan Park: I’ll post a doc link in #eng-platform once I have the endpoint contract written.

Victor Alvarez: I’ll send the network signal options (probe metrics + thresholds) later today.

Irene Choi: Thanks all. Keep updates in this thread as you have drafts/PRs.

source.body:
Victor Alvarez: heads up, we’re proposing DNS TTL reductions for regional failover (Hosted + some Dedicated endpoints). Want a quick risk assessment here before we merge the tf changes.

Victor Alvarez: current state (most public records): TTL=60s (a few are 300s still). Proposal: drop to 20s for the failover-weighted records, keep everything else at 60s.

Irene Choi: 20s is… aggressive but maybe fine. Can you clarify which records exactly? `api.redwoodinference.com` is behind the geo/latency policy right?

Victor Alvarez: yep. Specifically:
- `api.redwoodinference.com` (latency/weighted policy)
- `stream.api.redwoodinference.com`
- `dedicated.<region>.redwoodinference.com` NOT in scope (those are customer-specific and already pinned)
- a couple internal healthcheck CNAMEs used by probes

Miles Thornton: +1 to being careful. Remember TTL is only one lever; resolvers don’t always respect low TTL.

Emily Zhao: confirming: some enterprise customers terminate behind corporate resolvers that clamp to min TTL (I’ve seen 30s/60s). Also Java apps tend to cache DNS “forever” unless configured (security property). So lower TTL helps, but it’s not deterministic.

Rafael Mendes: from SRE side: we’re trying to get faster convergence during a region impairment, but we don’t want surprise traffic shifts during normal ops. If TTL change causes more query load or weird caching behavior, that’s a bad trade.

Victor Alvarez: risks I’m tracking:
1) Resolver TTL floors (min TTL) => won’t see benefit for some clients
2) Negative caching (NXDOMAIN) if we misconfigure or a provider returns transient NXDOMAIN
3) Increased QPS to authoritative (Route53) and potential throttling (unlikely but worth quantifying)
4) Partial propagation mismatches during change window => weird split-brain (some clients re-resolve, others don’t)

Allison Grant: observability angle: if we do this, we should annotate dashboards and watch:
- DNS query rate at Route53
- `5xx` + tail latency by region
- ingress RPS shifts (esp. eu-west)
If there’s a subtle traffic drift we need to detect it fast.

Peter Holtz: also watch retries. Lower TTL can cause more reconnect churn on clients that resolve per request, but main risk is *failback* when a region recovers. If the record flips back quickly, some clients will thrash. We should pair with hold-down timers in routing policy.

Miles Thornton: right, DNS TTL != policy flap protection. The controller has hold-down, but DNS-based steering has to respect that too (don’t “bounce” answers).

Irene Choi: provider caveats:
- Some recursive resolvers serve stale records longer under load/outage (serve-stale RFC). During an internet event, low TTL might not matter.
- Cloudflare / public resolvers sometimes prefetch and extend caching behavior.
- On AWS, Route53 health checks can lag; but we’re not relying solely on R53 health checks for failover anyway.

Victor Alvarez: yep, this TTL change is meant to reduce the *best-case* convergence time when routing weights are adjusted, not to be the only trigger.

Emily Zhao: question: are we changing TTL for apex/ALIAS? If it’s ALIAS to a load balancer, TTL semantics are a bit weird. Route53 sets TTL but ALIAS answers can behave differently than plain A/AAAA from a client POV.

Victor Alvarez: `api.*` is ALIAS to regional NLBs via latency-based routing. TTL should still apply, but yes: some resolvers treat ALIAS just like A records. We’ll test with `dig` against common resolvers.

Rafael Mendes: can we do staged rollout? like: lower TTL only for a canary hostname first, run a planned weight flip in staging, then do prod.

Victor Alvarez: rollout plan draft:
Phase 0 (today): add canary record `api-canary.redwoodinference.com` with TTL=20s, same routing policy as `api.*`.
Phase 1 (staging): run a controlled weight shift us-east -> eu-west and measure convergence. Record:
- time-to-traffic-shift at edge (ingress)
- DNS query rates
- client error/latency
Phase 2 (prod, low blast): change TTL to 30s first on `stream.api.*` only (lower traffic), observe 24h
Phase 3 (prod): change TTL to 20s on `api.*` during a weekday window w/ oncall present
Rollback: revert TTL to 60s + stop further changes if:
- Route53 qps spikes >2x baseline for >15m
- unexplained region traffic shift >10% sustained

Sean Gallagher: I like staged. But I’d prefer 30s as final unless we can prove 20s doesn’t create noise. The bigger win might be getting rid of the 300s outliers.

Irene Choi: +1. Removing 300s is low-risk / high value.

Victor Alvarez: fair. Alternative proposal:
- Standardize to 60s everywhere
- Drop only failover steering records to 30s (not 20s)
- Keep canary at 20s for measurement

Miles Thornton: works for me. Also check: some mobile clients use OS caching with min TTL (iOS) + captive networks. Don’t assume perfect.

Allison Grant: if we do canary, please ensure dashboards include: `dns_ttl_seconds` label or annotation so we can correlate.

Victor Alvarez: we can add an annotation in the failover dashboard + a runbook note. I can also emit a one-time “infra change” event to the timeline feed.

Peter Holtz: any interaction with anycast? we have anycast notes in the networking doc but our public endpoint is still DNS->regional NLB. Anycast is only for internal edge proxies in a couple POPs, right?

Victor Alvarez: correct. No anycast change here.

Emily Zhao: another caveat: if we lower TTL but keep connection reuse high (HTTP/2), many clients won’t re-resolve until reconnect. So the effective traffic shift time is bounded by connection lifetimes too.

Miles Thornton: which is why the gateway redirect + retry-after stuff matters. DNS is just one piece.

Rafael Mendes: action items:
- Victor: update tf PR to set TTL=30s for prod records, add canary at 20s
- Allison: prep dashboard annotation + quick query for Route53 QPS
- Miles/Victor: add note to game day plan re: DNS TTL expectations (not deterministic)

Terraform Bot: PR opened: redwood-terraform/pr-1442-dns-ttl-and-failover-routing-updates (diff includes TTL changes for `api.redwoodinference.com` + new `api-canary.redwoodinference.com`).

Irene Choi: can you also add a guardrail in the PR description: “no other routing policy changes included” to keep scope tight.

Victor Alvarez: yep, will do. Also adding a checklist:
- [ ] verify no 300s TTL remain on failover records
- [ ] verify healthcheck CNAMEs unchanged
- [ ] confirm Route53 limits + current qps baseline

Sean Gallagher: and include explicit “stop” conditions + who has rollback authority (infra oncall + SRE lead). We don’t want to debate mid-change.

Victor Alvarez: ack. Rollback authority: infra oncall (primary) with SRE lead consulted, Irene as final escalation.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: once PR is in, I’ll add the dashboard annotations & a temporary alert on Route53 qps anomalies.

Miles Thornton: one more thing: make sure we don’t reduce TTL on any records that are used for data residency pinning (some enterprise tenants depend on region-locked hostnames).

Victor Alvarez: confirmed out of scope. Only the global Hosted API hostnames.

Irene Choi: ok. +1 to proceed with the 30s plan + canary at 20s. Let’s schedule staging measurement before prod change.

Victor Alvarez: sounds good. I’ll update PR + drop a calendar hold for staging test with Emily + Allison.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. linking this thread in the reliability initiative doc so it doesn’t get lost.

Miles Thornton: right, DNS TTL != policy flap protection. The controller has hold-down, but DNS-based steering has to respect that too (don’t “bounce” answers).

Irene Choi: provider caveats:
- Some recursive resolvers serve stale records longer under load/outage (serve-stale RFC). During an internet event, low TTL might not matter.
- Cloudflare / public resolvers sometimes prefetch and extend caching behavior.
- On AWS, Route53 health checks can lag; but we’re not relying solely on R53 health checks for failover anyway.

Victor Alvarez: yep, this TTL change is meant to reduce the *best-case* convergence time when routing weights are adjusted, not to be the only trigger.

Emily Zhao: question: are we changing TTL for apex/ALIAS? If it’s ALIAS to a load balancer, TTL semantics are a bit weird. Route53 sets TTL but ALIAS answers can behave differently than plain A/AAAA from a client POV.

Victor Alvarez: `api.*` is ALIAS to regional NLBs via latency-based routing. TTL should still apply, but yes: some resolvers treat ALIAS just like A records. We’ll test with `dig` against common resolvers.

Rafael Mendes: can we do staged rollout? like: lower TTL only for a canary hostname first, run a planned weight flip in staging, then do prod.

Victor Alvarez: rollout plan draft:
Phase 0 (today): add canary record `api-canary.redwoodinference.com` with TTL=20s, same routing policy as `api.*`.
Phase 1 (staging): run a controlled weight shift us-east -> eu-west and measure convergence. Record:
- time-to-traffic-shift at edge (ingress)
- DNS query rates
- client error/latency
Phase 2 (prod, low blast): change TTL to 30s first on `stream.api.*` only (lower traffic), observe 24h
Phase 3 (prod): change TTL to 20s on `api.*` during a weekday window w/ oncall present
Rollback: revert TTL to 60s + stop further changes if:
- Route53 qps spikes >2x baseline for >15m
- unexplained region traffic shift >10% sustained

Sean Gallagher: I like staged. But I’d prefer 30s as final unless we can prove 20s doesn’t create noise. The bigger win might be getting rid of the 300s outliers.

Irene Choi: +1. Removing 300s is low-risk / high value.

Victor Alvarez: fair. Alternative proposal:
- Standardize to 60s everywhere
- Drop only failover steering records to 30s (not 20s)
- Keep canary at 20s for measurement

Miles Thornton: works for me. Also check: some mobile clients use OS caching with min TTL (iOS) + captive networks. Don’t assume perfect.

Allison Grant: if we do canary, please ensure dashboards include: `dns_ttl_seconds` label or annotation so we can correlate.

Victor Alvarez: we can add an annotation in the failover dashboard + a runbook note. I can also emit a one-time “infra change” event to the timeline feed.

Peter Holtz: any interaction with anycast? we have anycast notes in the networking doc but our public endpoint is still DNS->regional NLB. Anycast is only for internal edge proxies in a couple POPs, right?

Victor Alvarez: correct. No anycast change here.

Emily Zhao: another caveat: if we lower TTL but keep connection reuse high (HTTP/2), many clients won’t re-resolve until reconnect. So the effective traffic shift time is bounded by connection lifetimes too.

Miles Thornton: which is why the gateway redirect + retry-after stuff matters. DNS is just one piece.

Rafael Mendes: action items:
- Victor: update tf PR to set TTL=30s for prod records, add canary at 20s
- Allison: prep dashboard annotation + quick query for Route53 QPS
- Miles/Victor: add note to game day plan re: DNS TTL expectations (not deterministic)

Terraform Bot: PR opened: redwood-terraform/pr-1442-dns-ttl-and-failover-routing-updates (diff includes TTL changes for `api.redwoodinference.com` + new `api-canary.redwoodinference.com`).

Irene Choi: can you also add a guardrail in the PR description: “no other routing policy changes included” to keep scope tight.

Victor Alvarez: yep, will do. Also adding a checklist:
- [ ] verify no 300s TTL remain on failover records
- [ ] verify healthcheck CNAMEs unchanged
- [ ] confirm Route53 limits + current qps baseline

Sean Gallagher: and include explicit “stop” conditions + who has rollback authority (infra oncall + SRE lead). We don’t want to debate mid-change.

Victor Alvarez: ack. Rollback authority: infra oncall (primary) with SRE lead consulted, Irene as final escalation.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: once PR is in, I’ll add the dashboard annotations & a temporary alert on Route53 qps anomalies.

Miles Thornton: one more thing: make sure we don’t reduce TTL on any records that are used for data residency pinning (some enterprise tenants depend on region-locked hostnames).

Victor Alvarez: confirmed out of scope. Only the global Hosted API hostnames.

Irene Choi: ok. +1 to proceed with the 30s plan + canary at 20s. Let’s schedule staging measurement before prod change.

Victor Alvarez: sounds good. I’ll update PR + drop a calendar hold for staging test with Emily + Allison.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. linking this thread in the reliability initiative doc so it doesn’t get lost.

source.messages:
Ava Chen: quick ask — can we add a heatmap that highlights GPU slot sparsity across racks and regions? seeing weird cold pockets in eu-west.
Tom Briggs: yup, we should do a heatmap + a sparklines row. want it to surface both utilization and "sparsity" (slots idle > 20% for > 30m)
Ava Chen: exactly. aim is to correlate sparsity with thermal deltas and cost (warm-slotting wasted cycles)
Priya Nair: FYI we also need to treat eu-west with a different SLO — smaller fleet and tighter latency reqs. alert thresholds can't be same as us-west.
Jorge Ruiz: re: query — here's a starting promql I used locally:
```
sum by (rack, region) (rate(gpu_utilization_seconds_total[5m])) / sum by (rack, region) (machine_cpu_cores)
```
but better to use node-level allocatable vram and nonzero allocations: 
```
(sum by(rack,region)(gpu_allocated_vram_bytes) / sum by(rack,region)(gpu_total_vram_bytes))
```
Tom Briggs: for sparsity detection I prefer a count of slots with <5% utilization over 30m:
```
count_over_time((gpu_slot_utilization < 0.05)[30m:30s])
```
then divide by count of slots per rack for a ratio.
grafana-bot: snapshot created: https://grafana.internal/snapshot/abcd1234 (expires in 7d)
Ava Chen: nice. can we add an annotation stream for scheduled maintenance and autoscaler events? those explain sudden cold pockets.
Tom Briggs: yes — add prom label from k8s_events or autoscaler events. panel thresholds:
- yellow: sparsity_ratio > 0.2
- red: sparsity_ratio > 0.4
Priya Nair: also add thermal-delta overlay: rack_inlet_temp 90p - 10p > 5C => indicates uneven cooling causing hot spots
Jorge Ruiz: for SLO tuning: different alert rules per region, e.g., eu-west p95 latency alert at 250ms, us-west at 300ms. attach region label to alertmanager route.
Ava Chen: cost note — if warm-slotting reduces cold pockets but increases reserved hours, we need a cost dashboard; link to billing query?
Tom Briggs: add a cost panel using `sum(cost_per_gpu_hour * gpu_hours_by_region)` and correlate with sparsity_ratio.
Priya Nair: propose a recording rule for sparsity_ratio per rack to reduce query load:
```
- record: infra:gpu_sparsity_ratio:avg
  expr: sum(count_over_time((gpu_slot_utilization < 0.05)[30m:1m])) by (rack,region) / sum(count_over_time((gpu_slot_utilization >= 0)[30m:1m])) by (rack,region)
```
Jorge Ruiz: agree. also debounce alerts by 15m to avoid flapping during deploys.
Ava Chen: what's the rollout plan for the new panels? small canary to prod grafana org?
Tom Briggs: create new folder `Ops/GPU Heatmaps` -> start with 2 view panels (sparsity heatmap, thermal delta overlay) in canary org. PR the dashboard json to infra/grafana repo.
grafana-bot: grafana PR created: https://git.internal/infra/grafana/pulls/432
Priya Nair: tagging on-call: @tom @ava — can we get a follow-up runbook entry for interpreting sparsity vs preemption?
Tom Briggs: will draft runbook. action items:
- Tom: PR dashboard + recording rule (due EOD Thu)
- Jorge: verify alertmanager route and debounce (due Fri)
- Priya: add cost panel wiring and billing query (due Fri)
- Ava: annotate maintenance windows for last 30d (due Tue)
Ava Chen: thanks all :thumbsup:
Tom Briggs: +1
grafana-bot: deployment of dashboard to canary completed at 1765123620

source.content:
Vanessa Ortiz: Need quick alignment on Private customers + Helm. We keep getting “how do I override the hardware tuning profile?” asks. We need a supported path that doesn’t let folks wedge the runtime w/ random knobs.

Benji Okafor: +1. If we expose `runtime.config` as a free-form map in values, Support will hate us. We should constrain to a small set of overrides + validate.

Diego Santos: Context: in Private, customers may run mixed SKUs (H100 + L40S in diff node pools) and want to pin a profile per pool. Our override model needs to map cleanly to “deployment/pool” not “per request”.

Ethan Park: From schema side: the profile pack already has guardrails (compat matrix, allowed keys, safe ranges). But if Helm allows arbitrary runtime flags we bypass all of that.

Vanessa Ortiz: What are we thinking for values.yaml? Something like:
- select pack version
- select profile name OR allow “auto”
- allow limited parameter overrides (only a few)??

Benji Okafor: Proposed contract (supportable):
1) Only allow selecting a *profile* (or turning profiles off) via Helm.
2) Overrides happen by referencing a named override doc (small yaml fragment) that we can validate with same JSON schema.
3) For “break glass” we can allow `extraArgs` but only in internal installs (feature-gated).

Diego Santos: Re: node pools: Helm is deployed per runtime instance anyway (one runtime per pool in most Private setups). So “per pool” = separate release / values file.

Sean Gallagher: SRE asks: can oncall tell customers “set X to disable” and be confident it reverts to defaults? We need one obvious toggle.

Ethan Park: Toggle should be explicit:
- `hardwareProfiles.enabled: true|false`
- `hardwareProfiles.mode: auto|pinned`
- `hardwareProfiles.pinnedProfile: "h100-balanced-v1"` etc

Keiko Yamamoto: Also make `auto` not too clever. If detection is ambiguous (MIG, mixed GPU), it should pick “default-safe” or refuse and log loudly.

Irene Choi: Infra angle: GPU SKU strings are inconsistent across providers. Runtime selection logic normalizes, but Private customers will still ask “why did it pick X?” Need to expose selected profile in metrics/logs.

Benji Okafor: We already added profile selection metrics + debug endpoint in runtime (PR in flight). For Helm we can surface in NOTES.txt: “check /debug/config” or the CLI dump.

Kira Thompson: For docs/runbook: Support wants copy/paste snippets. Can we standardize the values keys and include an example for pinned profile + version pin?

Marissa Cole: Product/UX concern: if we allow “override a few knobs”, customers will expect it’s stable forever. I’d rather we say: choose a profile, optionally choose “balanced vs throughput” variant. Anything beyond that is a Support engagement.

Vanessa Ortiz: OK, so: profiles are the unit of customization. The exception is “disable profiles entirely”. No ad-hoc knob overrides.

Ethan Park: Works for me. The moment we allow `scheduler.maxBatchSize` etc in values we’re in bespoke-land.

Diego Santos: What about someone needing to reduce KV cache because they’re memory constrained? That’s the main “override” request we see.

Benji Okafor: That should be a separate *supported profile variant* (e.g., `l40s-memory-conservative`). Not a runtime config override. Otherwise the compatibility matrix becomes meaningless.

Irene Choi: +1. We can add variants later. For now keep the interface strict.

Sean Gallagher: On rollback semantics: we should make it so changing `pinnedProfile` is enough, no need to touch image tags.

Benji Okafor: Yup. Chart should support:
- `hardwareProfiles.pack.version` (defaults to “bundled with runtime image”)
- optional `hardwareProfiles.pack.uri` (external bundle) — but I’d prefer to keep it off for Private initially.

Ethan Park: If we do allow `pack.uri`, we need integrity checking (sha256) + an allowlist of versions. Otherwise customers can load random packs and blame us.

Vanessa Ortiz: I’d rather avoid `uri` for v1. Private customers are already managing the runtime image; that should be the artifact.

Diego Santos: Agree. For Dedicated/Hosted we can do remote pack updates later. Private should be “baked-in pack” so support can reproduce.

Keiko Yamamoto: Also: Helm should fail on unknown keys. I don’t want typos silently doing nothing.

Benji Okafor: We can do `values.schema.json` in the chart and validate on `helm install/upgrade`. Also runtime already validates effective config at startup.

Kira Thompson: Draft snippet for docs:

```
hardwareProfiles:
  enabled: true
  mode: pinned
  pinnedProfile: "h100-balanced-v1"
  requireCompatibilityCheck: true

# optional: turn off for troubleshooting
# hardwareProfiles:
#   enabled: false
```

And explicitly: “Do not set runtime env vars for scheduler knobs unless instructed by Redwood Support.”

Sean Gallagher: Add “rollback” snippet to runbook: set `enabled=false` OR switch back to `auto` and redeploy.

Irene Choi: + mention that when disabling, runtime logs should say: `profile_selected=none source=helm_disable` (or similar). Makes it obvious.

Ethan Park: Naming: we should log `override_source=helm_values` vs `fleet_default` etc.

Benji Okafor: Action items:
- I’ll update chart values keys to match the above and add schema validation (this is basically PR-612 scope).
- Ethan can sanity check the allowed enum values match the profile pack.
- Kira T can update the Private override section in the rollout/runbook docs.

Vanessa Ortiz: Works. One more question: do we allow “pinnedProfile per GPU sku” in Helm? Like map from sku->profile.

Diego Santos: That’s tempting but it’s a footgun if a cluster adds a new sku later. I’d keep it one pinned profile per deployment.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1. Simple.

Marissa Cole: Please keep the interface boring. If a customer needs mixed SKUs, we instruct them to run separate runtime deployments per node pool with different values files.

Vanessa Ortiz: Cool. I’ll reply back to Support ticket w/ “no ad-hoc overrides; supported: enable/disable + auto/pinned + pick a named profile; separate deployments for mixed SKUs”.

Benji Okafor: :thumbsup: I’ll drop a link in here once the Helm PR is up.

Ethan Park: :thumbsup: (and please ensure `requireCompatibilityCheck` defaults to true in Private)

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: and please ensure the disable toggle is documented as the first step in the oncall triage playbook.

Vanessa Ortiz: heads up for the profile-pack work — GPU SKU detection is messy across providers. We’re seeing different strings for “same” card, which breaks profile selection. Can we enumerate variants we’ve observed + decide normalization rules?

Irene Choi: yep. what are you using as source today? `nvidia-smi --query-gpu=name` or NVML?

Vanessa Ortiz: mix. Hosted uses NVML most places, but some runners / private deployments parse `nvidia-smi` output. Also K8s device plugin sometimes reports a different “product name”.

Nina Petrova: adding what I’ve seen from fleet inventory exports (cloud + bare metal). Not exhaustive but representative.

Nina Petrova: H100 variants:
- `NVIDIA H100 SXM`
- `NVIDIA H100 80GB HBM3`
- `NVIDIA H100 PCIe`
- `NVIDIA H100 NVL`
- `H100-SXM5-80GB`
- `NVIDIA HGX H100 8-GPU` (this shows up as node-level label sometimes)
- MIG: `NVIDIA H100 80GB HBM3 MIG 1g.10gb` (and similar)

Keiko Yamamoto: L40S variants I’ve seen:
- `NVIDIA L40S`
- `NVIDIA L40S 48GB`
- `NVIDIA RTX L40S`
- `NVIDIA L40S (AD102)` (lol)

Irene Choi: b200 is already starting to show up differently too.

Irene Choi: B200/GB200-ish strings we saw on the partner cluster:
- `NVIDIA B200`
- `NVIDIA B200 SXM`
- `B200-SXM6-180GB`
- `NVIDIA HGX B200 8-GPU`
- `GB200` (node label / marketing name from one vendor)

Yuki Sato: from runtime side, we mostly just need a stable “sku key” to map to `h100` / `l40s` / `b200` for profile selection. form factor can be useful (pcie vs sxm) but if it’s too brittle we should ignore for v1.

Benji Okafor: +1. loader can accept `detected_gpu_name_raw` and `detected_gpu_sku_normalized`. if normalized is unknown/ambiguous -> pick `generic-safe` profile and log loudly.

Ethan Park: schema has guardrails where a profile can declare `match` patterns. but I’d rather not ship a huge regex zoo in the pack itself. better to normalize in runtime/platform code and keep the pack clean.

Quentin Harris: compatibility matrix angle: if we normalize incorrectly we might apply B200 knobs on H100 which is…bad. prefer conservative mapping for anything unclear.

Sean Gallagher: SRE POV: need (1) metric to see what profile got applied and (2) ability to override quickly by pool when we find a weird string.

Vanessa Ortiz: agreed. proposal: normalization returns:
- `sku_family` in {`h100`,`l40s`,`b200`,`unknown`}
- optional `form_factor` in {`sxm`,`pcie`,`nvl`,`unknown`}
- `is_mig` bool
- `raw` string (for logs/metrics)

Vanessa Ortiz: but for now the profile selection key is just `sku_family` (and we only add form_factor later if we decide we need it for H100 PCIe vs SXM differences).

Irene Choi: ok. let’s define deterministic rules with a small set of regexes + normalizations:
1) lowercase
2) strip `nvidia`, `tesla`, `rtx`, extra whitespace
3) if contains `mig` => `is_mig=true` and strip the `mig ...` suffix before matching
4) match in order: b200, h100, l40s (to avoid “h100” matching inside some longer string? probably fine but order is safer)

Yuki Sato: watch out for `gh200` / `gb200` confusion. If someone passes `GB200` today, that might be a node label for “Grace Blackwell Superchip”, not the GPU chip name string.

Nina Petrova: yeah we saw `GB200` from a vendor’s inventory feed, but on the node `nvidia-smi` still says `NVIDIA B200`.

Benji Okafor: so rule: if raw contains `gb200` but not `b200`, treat as `unknown` and require explicit override? or treat as `b200` but mark `ambiguous=true`?

Quentin Harris: for safety, I’d do:
- if contains `b200` => b200
- else if contains `gb200` => unknown (and log “GB200 seen; set override if you know it’s B200”)
We can relax later once we have more data.

Irene Choi: +1 conservative. also `HGX H100` shouldn’t break us — node label might be `NVIDIA HGX H100 8-GPU` and should still normalize to h100.

Keiko Yamamoto: L40S has the annoying `AD102` suffix from some builds. we should just match `l40s` anywhere in the cleaned string.

Vanessa Ortiz: let me paste a quick mapping table we can agree on.

Vanessa Ortiz: Normalization mapping (v1):
- if cleaned contains `h100` => sku_family=`h100`
- if cleaned contains `l40s` => sku_family=`l40s`
- if cleaned contains `b200` => sku_family=`b200`
- else sku_family=`unknown`
Special cases:
- if contains `mig` => is_mig=true, but sku_family matches based on remaining text
- if contains `pcie` => form_factor=`pcie`
- if contains `sxm` => form_factor=`sxm`
- if contains `nvl` => form_factor=`nvl`
- if contains both `sxm` and `pcie` (lol) => form_factor=`unknown` and log
- if contains `gb200` and not `b200` => sku_family=`unknown` and log

Ethan Park: looks good. keep it simple.

Sean Gallagher: log needs to include pool/region and raw string. otherwise oncall will be guessing.

Benji Okafor: I’ll add startup log + metric labels:
- `runtime.gpu.detected_name_raw` (log only)
- `runtime.gpu.sku_family` (metric label)
- `runtime.gpu.form_factor` (metric label)
- `runtime.profile.selected` (already in PR 22027 but we can align naming)

Yuki Sato: also please normalize the raw strings we store (trim weird unicode / zero-width spaces). we’ve been bitten before.

Irene Choi: can we list per-provider examples to validate?

Nina Petrova: quick provider samples from my notes:
AWS (p5): `NVIDIA H100 80GB HBM3`
GCP (a3): `NVIDIA H100 80GB HBM3`
Azure (ND H100 v5): `NVIDIA H100 SXM`
CoreWeave: `H100-SXM5-80GB`
Lambda: `NVIDIA L40S`
On-prem: `NVIDIA RTX L40S`
Partner B200 cluster: `B200-SXM6-180GB`

Vanessa Ortiz: perfect. I’ll fold that into the infra side acceptance checks (unit tests with sample strings).

Quentin Harris: add a test for `NVIDIA H100 80GB HBM3 MIG 1g.10gb` => sku_family h100, is_mig true.

Benji Okafor: ack. I’ll add fixture cases + make unknown mapping explicit.

Keiko Yamamoto: one more edge case: sometimes device plugin reports `nvidia.com/gpu.product: NVIDIA-H100-80GB-HBM3` (dashes). should match after we replace non-alnum with spaces.

Irene Choi: good call. normalization should replace `[_-]` with spaces, collapse spaces.

Vanessa Ortiz: action items:
- Benji: implement normalization helper + tests w/ the sample strings above; add logs + metrics.
- Vanessa: update infra inventory checks + add alert if unknown sku_family spikes per pool.
- Irene: share any additional strings from B200 bringup once we have more fleet access.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: once metrics are in, I’ll add a dashboard slice “unknown sku_family by pool” so we catch this before rollout.

Benji Okafor: cool. I’ll thread the PR here when it’s up.

Yuki Sato: thanks. this will save us a lot of “why did it pick generic profile” debugging.

Vanessa Ortiz: lol yep. ok closing the loop when PR lands.

source.messages:
Irene Choi: heads up, for hot-route protection we need to settle which fleet signals are actually reliable for admission control triggers. seeing debate in reviews: GPU saturation vs queue depth vs scheduler lag. want to lock a recommendation this week + note region-specific thresholds.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. GPU % is the one everyone reaches for but it lies a lot in practice (esp in us-east). queue depth seems closer to “customer pain” but also depends on batching settings.

Rafael Mendes: from SRE view: the main thing is signals that correlate with p95/p99 latency BEFORE we’re on fire. GPU util tends to peg at 95% during healthy steady state for some pools.

Keiko Yamamoto: also autoscaler can mask things. gpu-util can be high while we’re scaling out, so if we shed based on util we’d shed too aggressively.

Jamal Pierce: i pulled a quick snapshot from last week burst (us-east) — GPU util was 92–97% for ~40 mins, but latency only spiked for chat when queue depth crossed a threshold.

Jamal Pierce: excerpt (from notes):
- us-east chat pool baseline gpu_util: ~88–93% at normal daytime
- eu-west baseline gpu_util: ~70–82%
- queue_depth_req (avg) baseline: ~0.2–0.8
- when queue_depth_req sustained > 6, p95 latency started climbing fast

Robert King: so maybe gpu util isn’t a trigger, but still useful as context / to validate we’re not just in a metric bug.

Elena Popov: routing needs something stable and low-cardinality. per-GPU metrics are expensive in decision path; aggregated per-pool / per-cluster is fine. queue depth per route-group would be ideal.

Julian Moore: runtime side: scheduler lag is probably the cleanest leading indicator. We now export `runtime.scheduler_lag_ms_p95` + `runtime.queue_depth_requests` (post PR-27435). Both are already aggregated by pool.

Logan Wright: careful on tags. we had the cardinality incident last month when route_id slipped into overload metrics :grimacing: if routing wants per-route-group, we can do `route_group` not per-tenant.

Aisha Bello: +1. The alerting side also prefers a composite: (queue depth high) AND (sched lag high) for N minutes.

Irene Choi: what about GPU saturation as a guardrail though? e.g. if queue depth low but GPU is pegged, that’s still a sign we’re out of headroom? or could just be long sequences / kernels.

Sofia Ivanova: queue depth low + gpu pegged is often long prompts with continuous batching. scheduler is “busy” but not necessarily behind. it only becomes a problem if lag grows (scheduler can’t keep up) or queue accumulates.

Vanessa Ortiz: also MIG fragmentation can make util look high with less effective throughput. we’ve seen util ~95% even when one slice is starved.

Peter Holtz: for circuit breakers we usually look at:
1) scheduler lag p95 (service time backlog)
2) pending queue depth (arrival > service)
3) 5xx spike (already failing)
GPU util is mostly a “capacity planning” indicator not “admission now” indicator.

Robert King: question: do we have consistent queue depth across regions? i remember eu-west uses a slightly different batching config.

Julian Moore: yes but magnitude differs. queue depth is “requests waiting to be admitted to scheduler” so it depends on front-door concurrency + batch size.

Jamal Pierce: that’s where region-specific thresholds come in. eu-west queue depth baseline is lower; using a global threshold of 6 might be too high (we’d react late). maybe use percentile over baseline per region/pool.

Aisha Bello: +100. absolute thresholds are brittle. can we do: trigger when `queue_depth` > max(absolute_min, baseline_p95 * factor)?

Keiko Yamamoto: baseline computation in routing sounds risky for data plane complexity. but we can precompute per-region thresholds in config (control plane) and ship them.

Elena Popov: yep, we can keep routing simple:
- read per-route-group budget
- read per-region “pressure thresholds” for pool
- evaluate with last N seconds aggregated metrics from fleet signals service
No dynamic baseline calc in routing.

Irene Choi: can infra own the per-region threshold table (at least initial values) and we can revisit after canary?

Vanessa Ortiz: yes. propose:
Signal A (primary): scheduler_lag_ms_p95
Signal B (secondary): queue_depth_requests_avg (or p95)
Signal C (context only): gpu_util_p95

Vanessa Ortiz: then define pressure states per region/pool:
- WARN: lag > X OR qdepth > Y for 2m
- HOT: lag > X2 AND qdepth > Y2 for 1m
- CRIT: lag > X3 AND qdepth > Y3 for 30s (activate strict admission)

Rafael Mendes: this maps nicely to “start shedding best-effort at HOT, protect enterprise budgets at CRIT”.

Logan Wright: for dashboards, please standardize names:
- `fleet.pressure_state{region,pool}` as a gauge (0/1/2/3)
- `overload.shed_rate{region,route_group,tier,reason}`
We can annotate when pressure_state flips.

Peter Holtz: note: pressure_state should be derived in control plane / fleet signals service, not computed differently by each component.

Robert King: do we have an agreed metric source? i’ve seen both prometheus and the fleet-signals gRPC.

Vanessa Ortiz: for this project: fleet-signals gRPC (aggregated) is the source of truth. prom is for debugging.

Jamal Pierce: region notes (tentative, based on last 14d):
- us-east (chat pool): WARN lag>120ms or qdepth>4; HOT lag>250ms + qdepth>8; CRIT lag>400ms + qdepth>12
- eu-west (chat pool): WARN lag>90ms or qdepth>3; HOT lag>200ms + qdepth>6; CRIT lag>320ms + qdepth>9
These need validation during game day.

Aisha Bello: add a “don’t flap” rule please. if we enter HOT/CRIT require 60s below WARN to exit.

Keiko Yamamoto: also check autoscaling interaction: when scale-out in progress, lag can oscillate. maybe incorporate `pending_scale_actions` as a dampener? (not a trigger)

Sofia Ivanova: be careful: when scale-out happens, new replicas warm KV cache etc. you still might want to shed briefly to protect tail.

Irene Choi: ok, decision direction:
- use scheduler lag + queue depth for triggers
- GPU util is contextual only
- thresholds per region/pool in config, owned by infra + tuned via canary/game day

Irene Choi: action items:
1) @Jamal Pierce share the threshold table draft in the rollout doc + include “how derived”
2) @Vanessa Ortiz + @Robert King confirm fleet-signals gRPC fields + update contract doc
3) @Elena Popov ensure routing consumes pressure_state + doesn’t query raw gpu util
4) @Logan Wright align dashboard queries + make sure tags are bounded (region/pool/route_group only)

Vanessa Ortiz: ack. i’ll post the gRPC fields here once i confirm final names.

Julian Moore: current names from runtime exporter (double-check):
- `runtime.scheduler_lag_ms_p95{region,pool}`
- `runtime.queue_depth_requests_avg{region,pool}`
- `runtime.gpu_utilization_p95{region,pool}`
Fleet-signals service already aggregates to `fleet.*` equivalents.

Rafael Mendes: once we have the table, we’ll run it in the game day and see if we start shedding at the right time (before enterprise p95 blows past SLO). :thumbsup:

Irene Choi: cool. please keep this thread updated with any “gotchas” discovered in eu-west; we’ve been burned by copying us-east thresholds before.

source.messages:
Sahana Iyer: heads up: the signature verification integration tests are now the slowest step in `private-installer` CI 😬

Sahana Iyer: `bundle-verify-integration` job went from ~6m to ~28-35m after we added tamper/replay/downgrade cases. We need to get this back under ~10m or we’re gonna start timing out in release train.

ci-bot: Buildkite build #118244 for redwood-private-installer / main failed
ci-bot: Step `bundle-verify-integration` exceeded soft timeout (30m). Log: https://buildkite.example.redwood/...

Devon Price: looking at the logs now. a lot of time is `sha256sum` over big fixtures + re-generating offline bundle each test case.

Ethan Park: yeah, each test currently:
1) builds bundle (tar + generate manifest)
2) signs manifest
3) unpacks
4) modifies artifact
5) runs preflight

Ethan Park: so we’re doing the expensive steps N times.

Jordan Lee: manifest canonicalization step is also re-running every time. not huge but it adds up.

Sahana Iyer: can we make a “golden” bundle fixture once per job and then derive tampered variants cheaply?

Devon Price: +1. also the fixture includes a big image tarball (we added it to simulate offline image import). hashing that is killing us.

Connor O'Brien: release side: we don’t actually need a 3GB image tar to validate signature semantics. we need to validate that we *would* hash the referenced artifact, but the artifact can be small in tests.

Marcus Lin: from security POV, as long as the test still exercises:
- content hash validation
- signature verification
- fail-closed behavior
- downgrade protection
we can shrink the bytes.

Sean Gallagher: also worth splitting “fast unit-ish verification tests” vs “true end-to-end on a full bundle”. run the full bundle one nightly?

Ethan Park: we already have unit tests for canonicalization + envelope parsing. the integration suite is meant to be “bundle on disk + preflight CLI”.

Ethan Park: but we could do:
- one e2e uses a “medium” bundle (still realistic layout)
- tamper variants derived from that bundle via file copy + single edit

Sahana Iyer: what’s the slowest operation by flame graph / rough breakdown?

Devon Price: rough from build logs:
- `create_bundle()` ~9-12m (mostly tar + sha256s)
- `verify_bundle()` ~30-60s each
- repeated 12 times = pain

Devon Price: also `docker save` in one fixture is ~4m. why are we doing docker save here?

Ethan Park: legacy from older offline install tests. for signing, we only need to test that the manifest lists artifacts + hashes and we verify them offline.

Jordan Lee: can we replace docker save fixture with just a dummy file named `images/redwood-runtime.tar` that is small?

Marcus Lin: yes, but keep the path + semantics. the test should ensure that *changing that file* triggers hash mismatch.

Sahana Iyer: ok. proposal:
1) replace heavy image fixture with small deterministic tar (like `tar --mtime=0 --sort=name ...`)
2) generate base bundle once, cache it across test cases
3) for each test: copy base bundle dir (hardlink?) and mutate only one file

Sahana Iyer: for (2) we can do `TestMain` / shared setup if in Go.

Ethan Park: suite is in Go. we can use `sync.Once` and write base bundle into `$TMPDIR/base_bundle`.

Devon Price: copying the bundle dir might still be expensive. can we do copy-on-write? linux hardlinks via `cp -al`?

Sahana Iyer: Buildkite agents are linux, so `cp -al` should work. but careful: mutating file should break hardlink (need to copy the file you mutate, not write in-place).

Jordan Lee: for tamper cases we usually modify manifest or one artifact. we can do:
- copy tree as hardlinks
- for the file to modify, `cp` it first to break link, then edit

Ethan Park: makes sense.

Connor O'Brien: also add `BUILDKITE_CACHE`/artifact caching? we could upload the base bundle as build artifact for the step and have other steps consume it.

Sahana Iyer: within a single step we can probably avoid cross-step caching. cross-build caching is tricky (branch isolation) but possible.

Devon Price: simplest win is: stop regenerating bundle per test. right now it’s literally in each test function.

Ethan Park: i can refactor to build once.

Sean Gallagher: do we have `-run TestBundleVerifyTamper.*` set to run serial? go tests default parallel at package level but not within suite.

Devon Price: we turned off `t.Parallel()` previously because tests were stomping on temp dirs.

Sahana Iyer: if we make temp dirs unique per test (and base bundle read-only), we can turn `t.Parallel()` back on for mutation/verify steps.

Ethan Park: verify step reads from disk + computes hashes; parallel might be ok. but CPU-bound hashing can blow up. if we shrink fixture, it’s fine.

Jordan Lee: also we can short-circuit hashing in tests by using smaller artifacts, as discussed.

Marcus Lin: just don’t accidentally create tests that pass even if verification skips hashing. we should keep a test that ensures hash mismatch is caught.

Ethan Park: yep.

Sahana Iyer: what about caching computed hashes? manifest already contains expected hashes. verification computes actual hashes. but for test runtime, we can keep artifact sizes small.

Devon Price: also: our “base bundle” generator currently re-hashes *every file* even when we only need a few artifacts for the integration tests.

Devon Price: could create a “test bundle builder” that emits minimal layout:
- manifest.json
- manifest.sig
- charts/ (tiny)
- binaries/ (tiny)
- sbom/ pointer file

Connor O'Brien: +1. our CI should validate semantics, not the full production payload.

Sahana Iyer: ok. action items:
- Ethan: refactor suite to build base bundle once per package; use hardlink copy per test + break link on mutated file
- Devon: swap heavy fixtures (docker save) -> deterministic tiny tar/file
- Sahana: adjust Buildkite step timeouts + add timing logs (per phase)

Ethan Park: i’ll take the refactor. estimate: today + tomorrow.

Devon Price: i’ll patch fixtures now. probably behind a flag so we don’t lose the “realistic” path names.

Marcus Lin: please add a comment in code about why fixtures are small (perf) + what security properties are still validated.

Sean Gallagher: once it’s down, can we set an SLO for this job? like <12m p95 on main.

Sahana Iyer: yep. will add to infra dashboard after we stabilize.

Devon Price: quick note: deterministic tar creation should use:
`tar --sort=name --mtime='UTC 2020-01-01' --owner=0 --group=0 --numeric-owner -cf ...`
so hashes are stable.

Jordan Lee: love it. also ensure json canonicalization for manifest is stable. our tests currently write json with stdlib which doesn’t guarantee key order if map-based.

Ethan Park: base bundle generator uses our canonical writer (sorted keys). good.

Connor O'Brien: ping me if we need to change the release gate thresholds. but ideally this doesn’t block RC builds anymore.

ci-bot: Buildkite build #118301 started for redwood-private-installer / branch `ethan/shared-bundle-fixture`

Sahana Iyer: thanks all. let’s regroup after we have numbers.

Ethan Park: update: refactor done locally. base bundle built once. tamper tests run in parallel with isolated dirs. runtime on my laptop: 4m -> 55s.

Devon Price: fixture patch landed too. docker save removed. the “image tar” is now 8KB deterministic file. :thumbsup:

Marcus Lin: reviewed the changes. looks fine. keep one test that ensures changing `images/redwood-runtime.tar` triggers `HASH_MISMATCH` and we’re good.

Sahana Iyer: awesome. once CI confirms, i’ll tighten timeout back down and add an alert if it regresses.

ci-bot: Buildkite build #118301 passed
ci-bot: Step timings:
- bundle-verify-integration: 6m12s (was 33m41s)

Sahana Iyer: ✅ perf is back. i’ll file a follow-up to add a nightly “full-size bundle smoke” so we still catch weird tar/unpack issues without slowing main.

Connor O'Brien: perfect. this unblocks the release train gate. thanks.

title: #eng-infra: 1760710033
preview:
Sahana Iyer: heads up: the signature verification integration tests are now the slowest step in `private-installer` CI 😬

Sahana Iyer: `bundle-verify-integration` job went from ~6m to ~28-35m after we added tamper/replay/downgrade cases. We need to get this back under ~10m or we’re gonna start timing out in release train.

ci-bot: Buildkite build #118244 for redwood-private-installer / main failed
ci-bot: Step `bundle-verify-integration` exceeded soft timeout (30m). Log: https://buildkite.example.redwood/...

Devon Price: looking at the logs now. a lot of time is `sha256sum` over big fixtures + re-generating offline bundle each test case.

Ethan Park: yeah, each test currently:
1) builds bundle (tar + generate manifest)
2) signs manifest
3) unpacks
4) modifies artifact
5) runs preflight

Ethan Park: so we’re doing the expensive steps N times.

Jordan Lee: manifest canonicalization step is also re-running every time. not huge but it adds up.

Sahana Iyer: can we make a “golden” bundle fixture once per job and then derive tampered variants cheaply?

Devon Price: +1. also the fixture includes a big image tarball (we added it to simulate offline image import). hashing that is killing us.

Connor O'Brien: release side: we don’t actually need a 3GB image tar to validate signature semantics. we need to validate that we *would* hash the referenced artifact, but the artifact can be small in tests.

Marcus Lin: from security POV, as long as the test still exercises:
- content hash validation
- signature verification
- fail-closed behavior
- downgrade protection
we can shrink the bytes.

Sean Gallagher: also worth splitting “fast unit-ish verification tests” vs “true end-to-end on a full bundle”. run the full bundle one nightly?

Ethan Park: we already have unit tests for canonicalization + envelope parsing. the integration suite is meant to be “bundle on disk + preflight CLI”.

Ethan Park: but we could do:
- one e2e uses a “medium” bundle (still realistic layout)
- tamper variants derived from that bundle via file copy + single edit

Sahana Iyer: what’s the slowest operation by flame graph / rough breakdown?

Devon Price: rough from build logs:
- `create_bundle()` ~9-12m (mostly tar + sha256s)
- `verify_bundle()` ~30-60s each
- repeated 12 times = pain

Devon Price: also `docker save` in one fixture is ~4m. why are we doing docker save here?

Ethan Park: legacy from older offline install tests. for signing, we only need to test that the manifest lists artifacts + hashes and we verify them offline.

Jordan Lee: can we replace docker save fixture with just a dummy file named `images/redwood-runtime.tar` that is small?

Marcus Lin: yes, but keep the path + semantics. the test should ensure that *changing that file* triggers hash mismatch.

Sahana Iyer: ok. proposal:
1) replace heavy image fixture with small deterministic tar (like `tar --mtime=0 --sort=name ...`)
2) generate base bundle once, cache it across test cases
3) for each test: copy base bundle dir (hardlink?) and mutate only one file

Sahana Iyer: for (2) we can do `TestMain` / shared setup if in Go.

Ethan Park: suite is in Go. we can use `sync.Once` and write base bundle into `$TMPDIR/base_bundle`.

Devon Price: copying the bundle dir might still be expensive. can we do copy-on-write? linux hardlinks via `cp -al`?

Sahana Iyer: Buildkite agents are linux, so `cp -al` should work. but careful: mutating file should break hardlink (need to copy the file you mutate, not write in-place).

Jordan Lee: for tamper cases we usually modify manifest or one artifact. we can do:
- copy tree as hardlinks
- for the file to modify, `cp` it first to break link, then edit

Ethan Park: makes sense.

Connor O'Brien: also add `BUILDKITE_CACHE`/artifact caching? we could upload the base bundle as build artifact for the step and have other steps consume it.

Sahana Iyer: within a single step we can probably avoid cross-step caching. cross-build caching is tricky (branch isolation) but possible.

Devon Price: simplest win is: stop regenerating bundle per test. right now it’s literally in each test function.

Ethan Park: i can refactor to build once.

Sean Gallagher: do we have `-run TestBundleVerifyTamper.*` set to run serial? go tests default parallel at package level but not within suite.

Devon Price: we turned off `t.Parallel()` previously because tests were stomping on temp dirs.

Sahana Iyer: if we make temp dirs unique per test (and base bundle read-only), we can turn `t.Parallel()` back on for mutation/verify steps.

Ethan Park: verify step reads from disk + computes hashes; parallel might be ok. but CPU-bound hashing can blow up. if we shrink fixture, it’s fine.

Jordan Lee: also we can short-circuit hashing in tests by using smaller artifacts, as discussed.

Marcus Lin: just don’t accidentally create tests that pass even if verification skips hashing. we should keep a test that ensures hash mismatch is caught.

Ethan Park: yep.

Sahana Iyer: what about caching computed hashes? manifest already contains expected hashes. verification computes actual hashes. but for test runtime, we can keep artifact sizes small.

Devon Price: also: our “base bundle” generator currently re-hashes *every file* even when we only need a few artifacts for the integration tests.

Devon Price: could create a “test bundle builder” that emits minimal layout:
- manifest.json
- manifest.sig
- charts/ (tiny)
- binaries/ (tiny)
- sbom/ pointer file

Connor O'Brien: +1. our CI should validate semantics, not the full production payload.

Sahana Iyer: ok. action items:
- Ethan: refactor suite to build base bundle once per package; use hardlink copy per test + break link on mutated file
- Devon: swap heavy fixtures (docker save) -> deterministic tiny tar/file
- Sahana: adjust Buildkite step timeouts + add timing logs (per phase)

Ethan Park: i’ll take the refactor. estimate: today + tomorrow.

Devon Price: i’ll patch fixtures now. probably behind a flag so we don’t lose the “realistic” path names.

Marcus Lin: please add a comment in code about why fixtures are small (perf) + what security properties are still validated.

Sean Gallagher: once it’s down, can we set an SLO for this job? like <12m p95 on main.

Sahana Iyer: yep. will add to infra dashboard after we stabilize.

Devon Price: quick note: deterministic tar creation should use:
`tar --sort=name --mtime='UTC 2020-01-01' --owner=0 --group=0 --numeric-owner -cf ...`
so hashes are stable.

Jordan Lee: love it. also ensure json canonicalization for manifest is stable. our tests currently write json with stdlib which doesn’t guarantee key order if map-based.

Ethan Park: base bundle generator uses our canonical writer (sorted keys). good.

Connor O'Brien: ping me if we need to change the release gate thresholds. but ideally this doesn’t block RC builds anymore.

Sahana Iyer: thanks all. let’s regroup after we have numbers.

Ethan Park: update: refactor done locally. base bundle built once. tamper tests run in parallel with isolated dirs. runtime on my laptop: 4m -> 55s.

Devon Price: fixture patch landed too. docker save removed. the “image tar” is now 8KB deterministic file. :thumbsup:

Marcus Lin: reviewed the changes. looks fine. keep one test that ensures changing `images/redwood-runtime.tar` triggers `HASH_MISMATCH` and we’re good.

Sahana Iyer: awesome. once CI confirms, i’ll tighten timeout back down and add an alert if it regresses.

ci-bot: Step timings:
- bundle-verify-integration: 6m12s (was 33m41s)

Sahana Iyer: ✅ perf is back. i’ll file a follow-up to add a nightly “full-size bundle smoke” so we still catch weird tar/unpack issues without slowing main.

Connor O'Brien: perfect. this unblocks the release train gate. thanks.
