source.content:
Sahana Iyer: seeing flakes in staging again on the new signing stage. build `private-installer #11872` failed in `sign_offline_bundle` with a timeout waiting on the HSM emulator. anyone else seeing this?

buildkite-bot: Build #11872 failed on step `sign_offline_bundle` (staging). <https://buildkite.com/redwood/private-installer-staging/builds/11872>

Sahana Iyer: logs snippet:
```
+ ./scripts/sign_bundle.sh out/bundle.tar.zst
[sign] starting softhsm2...
[sign] waiting for pkcs11 slot...
ERROR: pkcs11: no slots found (attempt 45/45)
ERROR: timed out waiting for HSM (900s)
```

Connor O'Brien: is this on the shared staging runners only or repro locally too?

Sahana Iyer: only on staging runners so far. local + dev box seems fine.

Hanae Suzuki: that “no slots found” usually means softhsm didn’t init the token dir (or got clobbered). are we running multiple signing tests in parallel on same workspace?

Sahana Iyer: staging pipeline runs integration tests + signing step in the same job container, but we do have `pytest -n auto` for some suites. signing step itself is separate command, but same workspace + volume mounts.

Devon Price: we also added the “tampered bundle” integration tests behind `SIGNING_TESTS=1` in staging last week. those spin up softhsm too.

Connor O'Brien: so potential race: two softhsm initializations fighting over `SOFTHSM2_CONF` / token path.

Hanae Suzuki: yup. softhsm uses a token dir; if two procs init simultaneously you can get empty slots / corrupted db.

Sean Gallagher: +1. looks like a classic shared state / parallelism flake. can we isolate signing to its own job w/ fresh workspace?

Sahana Iyer: we can, but it’ll add ~6–8 min. there’s also another failure mode we saw earlier:
```
PKCS11 error: CKR_DEVICE_ERROR
openssl: error:8000001D:system library::Device or resource busy
```

Neha Kapoor: if it’s staging-only, i’d prefer we fix the isolation rather than add long retries. but a short retry might be ok as mitigation.

Connor O'Brien: agreed. goal is: release train can’t be blocked by staging flakes.

Devon Price: what about marking signing ITs as non-parallel + moving them to a separate stage? (unit tests still parallel)

Sahana Iyer: proposal:
1) change signing tests to run with `pytest -n 0 -m signing`
2) set token dir under `$BUILDKITE_BUILD_ID` so each build is isolated
3) add a lightweight retry around “wait for slot” (2 tries) w/ backoff

Hanae Suzuki: for (2) make sure `SOFTHSM2_CONF` points to build-specific conf too. we currently write it to `./.ci/softhsm2.conf` which is shared.

Sahana Iyer: good catch. will template it.

Connor O'Brien: do we know why it takes 900s to fail? we should fail faster if slot not ready.

Sahana Iyer: we set `HSM_READY_TIMEOUT=900` because the emulator sometimes took ~2-3 min on overloaded runners. but 15 min is too much.

Sean Gallagher: can we do: wait up to 120s, then restart softhsm once, then fail? makes logs more actionable + stops burning runner time.

Hanae Suzuki: also add a preflight dump so we have evidence when it fails:
```
softhsm2-util --show-slots
ls -lah $TOKEN_DIR
cat $SOFTHSM2_CONF
```

Devon Price: + add `set -x` in the wrapper (or echo commands). right now it hides some env.

Sahana Iyer: ok. i’ll update `scripts/hsm_start.sh`:
- token dir: `$WORKSPACE/.hsm/$BUILDKITE_BUILD_ID`
- conf: `$WORKSPACE/.hsm/$BUILDKITE_BUILD_ID/softhsm2.conf`
- add `flock` around init just in case (in-job parallel)
- reduce timeout to 180s; on failure restart once

Connor O'Brien: can we also pin the container image? sometimes the pkcs11 libs mismatch if base image updates.

Sahana Iyer: staging uses `private-installer-ci:latest`. we can pin to a digest for staging until this stabilizes.

Neha Kapoor: please do. “latest” in CI is always a footgun.

Sahana Iyer: ack. i’ll pin + open a follow-up to move to weekly bump.

Hanae Suzuki: one more: softhsm sometimes blocks on entropy on small VMs. do we have `haveged` / `rngd` in the runner image?

Devon Price: i don’t think so. but would that show as “no slots”? usually it hangs on keygen.

Hanae Suzuki: fair. but if init is generating keys on first boot, entropy can be slow. maybe we should pre-create token + keypair once per build and reuse within job.

Sahana Iyer: we do `softhsm2-util --init-token` and then generate key inside the signing script every time. i can split so we init+gen once, then signing just calls `cosign`/`openssl` against existing key.

Connor O'Brien: that would reduce runtime too.

Sean Gallagher: action items?

Sahana Iyer: i’ll:
- push patch to staging pipeline + scripts today
- add a build annotation on failure with the preflight output
- open a ticket to separate signing ITs into their own step once we confirm the race is the root cause

Devon Price: can you link the ticket here? i’ll update the release checklist so we don’t block on staging while you roll this out.

Sahana Iyer: creating now: ENG-4921 “Isolate softhsm token dir + serialize signing tests in staging”

Connor O'Brien: thanks. also can you add a temporary retry in the pipeline itself (not just wait loop)? like rerun the step once if it fails w/ slot timeout. :pray:

Sahana Iyer: yeah, Buildkite has `retry: automatic`. i’ll set it to 1 retry for `sign_offline_bundle` but only on exit code we use for “HSM not ready” (not on signature mismatch etc).

Hanae Suzuki: pls ensure the script exits with distinct codes. right now everything is `exit 1`.

Sahana Iyer: will do. `21` for HSM readiness timeout, `22` for pkcs11 init failure, `1` for everything else.

Neha Kapoor: sounds good. post back once staging is green for a few runs.

Sahana Iyer: will do.

(buildkite-bot reaction annotations: Connor O'Brien added :thumbsup: to Sahana Iyer’s proposal; Hanae Suzuki added :eyes: to the log snippet)

Sahana Iyer: update: pinned CI image digest + pushed token-dir isolation + serialized signing tests. staging build `#11879` passed. running a couple more.

buildkite-bot: Build #11881 passed on step `sign_offline_bundle` (staging). <https://buildkite.com/redwood/private-installer-staging/builds/11881>

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: if we get 5/5, let’s remove the 900s timeout everywhere and keep the single-step retry.

Sahana Iyer: agreed. will follow up tomorrow w/ stats.

source.text:
Maria Gomez: FYI — noticed a ~28% bump in GPU spend for Feb compared to forecast. Most of it shows up as "untagged" in the billing export. Anyone else seeing this?
Kevin Li: can you paste the billing export link? we should run the usual BigQuery grouping.
Maria Gomez: https://console.cloud.google.com/billing/exports — I exported to bigquery this morning. top-line is GPUs in us-central1 and europe-west4 appearing under project:infra-shared.
Sachin Rao: infra-shared is our shared GPU pool for sandbox teams, right? sounds like workloads are spinning up without cost center tags.
Lina Park: yep, some onboarding scripts default to project-level creds and don't set labels. we need enforcement + backfill.
Ben Carter: quick reproduction: my local job uses the shared pool and i didn't get prompted to set label. we should default a label on the compute template.
InfraBot: Deploy 17592 succeeded in infra-shared (autoscaler v3).
Kevin Li: proposed plan: 1) run a backfill query to map untagged charges by resource id -> try to infer team from git repo/owner OR instance naming 2) add a deny policy for create without tag for new instances 3) run a weekly job to alert on >5% untagged GPU spend
Maria Gomez: agree. for (1) can we start with BigQuery and GCP resource labels? here's a query i used last time to surface GPU-hour untagged spend:
```sql
SELECT service.description, sku.description, labels, SUM(cost) as total_cost
FROM `billing_project.gcp_billing_export_v1_v2.gcp_billing_export_v1_*`
WHERE EXTRACT(MONTH FROM usage_start_time)=2 AND sku.description LIKE '%NVIDIA%'
GROUP BY service.description, sku.description, labels
ORDER BY total_cost DESC
LIMIT 200
```
Lina Park: nice. add resource.name to correlate with instance/VM id. also join with metadata table to try owner label inference.
Sachin Rao: for backfill heuristics: instance name prefix -> team mapping, project IAM last modified -> likely owner, and GCS artifact egress logs to map repos. none perfect but should get most.
Abby (Finance): Important — Finance needs a clean mapping for chargeback this quarter. if backfill is noisy we should tag ambiguous as "shared-pool" and bill to central infra budget, not product teams.
Ben Carter: +1. prefer central first then reallocate if teams accept responsibility. prevents surprise invoices.
Maria Gomez: also need to revisit reserved commitments. current committed GPU hours for us-central1 (reserved) aren't aligned to consumption — we're paying for unused reserved capacity in ap-southeast while demand is in eu-west4. Suggest: evaluate convertible/reservable commitments or move some commitment credits to region-flex if provider allows.
Kevin Li: action: run per-region 12-week utilization for GPU SKUs, output table: region, sku, reserved_hours, used_hours, utilization_pct, potential_savings_if_rightsize. i can script it.
Lina Park: note: Dedicated (reserved) pools for Dedicated customers vs shared pools for infra-shared have different billing attachments. don't mix commitments with shared ephemeral pools unless we can partition quotas.
Sachin Rao: cloud provider offers regional discount tiers at 1yr/3yr. we should model cost under current growth: if we commit to 1yr in us-central we save ~22% but could leave us exposed if workloads move. consider convertible reservations or shorter terms for volatile workloads.
Abby (Finance): run two models: conservative (no growth), expected growth (10% qtr over qtr), and aggressive (30%). show NPV and payback period. finance can sign off on a 1-yr commit if payback <9 months.
Maria Gomez: For chargeback mechanics: propose tagging schema: cost_center, product, env, owner, pool. enforce cost_center and owner on any GPU allocation. Also add an automated Slack notify when pool usage spikes > threshold and list top 5 untagged instances.
Kevin Li: we already have a Cloud Function that runs on new instance create — i'll add label enforcement and auto-reject if missing (with a bypass for infra-admin role and an exception request flow).
Ben Carter: for existing VMs: i'll run a backfill job that writes inferred labels to a staging table and creates PRs to owners for confirmation. PRs will be auto-approved after 14 days and then labels applied.
InfraBot: Alert: untagged-gpu-hours > 50 in last 24h (infra-shared).
Maria Gomez: ok timeline: Kevin + Ben do backfill + enforcement this week. Sachin to produce 12-week utilization report by Friday. Abby will run commit financial models by next Wed. Lina will update ops runbook and add exception flow.
Lina Park: raising one constraint — network egress between regions inflates cross-region cost, so moving workloads between regions to match commitments may introduce net-negative savings. account for inter-region egress in modeling.
Sachin Rao: good catch. i'll include estimated egress costs per TB between candidate regions.
Abby (Finance): please add a column for tax & marketplace fees; they vary by region.
Maria Gomez: owners: @Kevin Li (labels + enforcement), @Ben Carter (backfill), @Sachin Rao (utilization + egress modeling), @Abby (financial models), @Lina Park (runbook). ping me if blocked. :thumbsup:
Kevin Li: i'll create the PRs and a small helm chart change for default labels, linking to enforcement policy.
Ben Carter: starting backfill job now, will post results in this thread when the first pass finishes.
Maria Gomez: thanks all, want this done before next billing close.
Ben Carter: first-pass results: 62% of untagged GPU-hours were attributable to 3 project IDs (sandbox-*, experiments-*). leaving the rest as shared-pool. I'll upload csv to gs://infra-ops/chargeback/backfill-1755500123.csv and post a summary in #eng-infra-next-week.
Maria Gomez: perfect, let's review on THU 11:00am.

title: #eng-infra: 1755500123
preview:
Maria Gomez: FYI — noticed a ~28% bump in GPU spend for Feb compared to forecast. Most of it shows up as "untagged" in the billing export. Anyone else seeing this?
Kevin Li: can you paste the billing export link? we should run the usual BigQuery grouping.
Maria Gomez: https://console.cloud.google.com/billing/exports — I exported to bigquery this morning. top-line is GPUs in us-central1 and europe-west4 appearing under project:infra-shared.
Sachin Rao: infra-shared is our shared GPU pool for sandbox teams, right? sounds like workloads are spinning up without cost center tags.
Lina Park: yep, some onboarding scripts default to project-level creds and don't set labels. we need enforcement + backfill.
Ben Carter: quick reproduction: my local job uses the shared pool and i didn't get prompted to set label. we should default a label on the compute template.
InfraBot: Deploy 17592 succeeded in infra-shared (autoscaler v3).
Kevin Li: proposed plan: 1) run a backfill query to map untagged charges by resource id -> try to infer team from git repo/owner OR instance naming 2) add a deny policy for create without tag for new instances 3) run a weekly job to alert on >5% untagged GPU spend
Maria Gomez: agree. for (1) can we start with BigQuery and GCP resource labels? here's a query i used last time to surface GPU-hour untagged spend:
```sql
SELECT service.description, sku.description, labels, SUM(cost) as total_cost
FROM `billing_project.gcp_billing_export_v1_v2.gcp_billing_export_v1_*`
WHERE EXTRACT(MONTH FROM usage_start_time)=2 AND sku.description LIKE '%NVIDIA%'
GROUP BY service.description, sku.description, labels
ORDER BY total_cost DESC
LIMIT 200
```
Lina Park: nice. add resource.name to correlate with instance/VM id. also join with metadata table to try owner label inference.
Sachin Rao: for backfill heuristics: instance name prefix -> team mapping, project IAM last modified -> likely owner, and GCS artifact egress logs to map repos. none perfect but should get most.
Abby (Finance): Important — Finance needs a clean mapping for chargeback this quarter. if backfill is noisy we should tag ambiguous as "shared-pool" and bill to central infra budget, not product teams.
Ben Carter: +1. prefer central first then reallocate if teams accept responsibility. prevents surprise invoices.
Maria Gomez: also need to revisit reserved commitments. current committed GPU hours for us-central1 (reserved) aren't aligned to consumption — we're paying for unused reserved capacity in ap-southeast while demand is in eu-west4. Suggest: evaluate convertible/reservable commitments or move some commitment credits to region-flex if provider allows.
Kevin Li: action: run per-region 12-week utilization for GPU SKUs, output table: region, sku, reserved_hours, used_hours, utilization_pct, potential_savings_if_rightsize. i can script it.
Lina Park: note: Dedicated (reserved) pools for Dedicated customers vs shared pools for infra-shared have different billing attachments. don't mix commitments with shared ephemeral pools unless we can partition quotas.
Sachin Rao: cloud provider offers regional discount tiers at 1yr/3yr. we should model cost under current growth: if we commit to 1yr in us-central we save ~22% but could leave us exposed if workloads move. consider convertible reservations or shorter terms for volatile workloads.
Abby (Finance): run two models: conservative (no growth), expected growth (10% qtr over qtr), and aggressive (30%). show NPV and payback period. finance can sign off on a 1-yr commit if payback <9 months.
Maria Gomez: For chargeback mechanics: propose tagging schema: cost_center, product, env, owner, pool. enforce cost_center and owner on any GPU allocation. Also add an automated Slack notify when pool usage spikes > threshold and list top 5 untagged instances.
Kevin Li: we already have a Cloud Function that runs on new instance create — i'll add label enforcement and auto-reject if missing (with a bypass for infra-admin role and an exception request flow).
Ben Carter: for existing VMs: i'll run a backfill job that writes inferred labels to a staging table and creates PRs to owners for confirmation. PRs will be auto-approved after 14 days and then labels applied.
InfraBot: Alert: untagged-gpu-hours > 50 in last 24h (infra-shared).
Maria Gomez: ok timeline: Kevin + Ben do backfill + enforcement this week. Sachin to produce 12-week utilization report by Friday. Abby will run commit financial models by next Wed. Lina will update ops runbook and add exception flow.
Lina Park: raising one constraint — network egress between regions inflates cross-region cost, so moving workloads between regions to match commitments may introduce net-negative savings. account for inter-region egress in modeling.
Sachin Rao: good catch. i'll include estimated egress costs per TB between candidate regions.
Abby (Finance): please add a column for tax & marketplace fees; they vary by region.
Maria Gomez: owners: @Kevin Li (labels + enforcement), @Ben Carter (backfill), @Sachin Rao (utilization + egress modeling), @Abby (financial models), @Lina Park (runbook). ping me if blocked. :thumbsup:
Kevin Li: i'll create the PRs and a small helm chart change for default labels, linking to enforcement policy.
Ben Carter: starting backfill job now, will post results in this thread when the first pass finishes.
Maria Gomez: thanks all, want this done before next billing close.
Ben Carter: first-pass results: 62% of untagged GPU-hours were attributable to 3 project IDs (sandbox-*, experiments-*). leaving the rest as shared-pool. I'll upload csv to gs://infra-ops/chargeback/backfill-1755500123.csv and post a summary in #eng-infra-next-week.
Maria Gomez: perfect, let's review on THU 11:00am.

source.text:
Vanessa Ortiz: Need to lock capacity reservations for benchmark sweeps in eu-west + APAC. @Jonas Weber @Rina Kobayashi can you confirm model list + rough run window next week?

Jonas Weber: Yep. Publishing regions: us-east, eu-west, apac-sg. Models: Llama 3.1 8B/70B, Qwen2.5 7B/72B, Mixtral 8x7B. We want to start sweeps Mon-Wed and rerun if stability gate trips.

Rina Kobayashi: Runner can do region sweep matrix but I need to know what hardware we’re standardizing on per model. If we mix instance types across regions, p95 variance gets messy.

Vanessa Ortiz: That’s the main issue: eu-west has decent H100 coverage; apac-sg is constrained on H100 and the A100 pool is fragmented.

Harish Reddy: Adding current fleet reality:
- eu-west: h100x8 nodes available (80GB), plus a100x8 (80GB) as fallback
- apac-sg: limited h100x8, more a100x8 but several are already allocated to customer Dedicated
- apac-tokyo: basically no spare H100 next week (maintenance + existing commits)

Claire Dubois: For 70B/72B we can run TP=2 on H100 80GB fine. On A100 80GB TP=2 is OK but we may need to lower max batch / be careful on long context.

Mateo Alvarez: Runtime POV: if we do 70B/72B on A100 in apac-sg but H100 in eu-west, the “latency/$” story will be questioned. Ideally same GPU class across publish regions.

Vanessa Ortiz: Agree. But if we insist on H100 in apac-sg we might only get a narrow window overnight local.

Jonas Weber: Overnight is fine. We can schedule. We mainly need deterministic throughput + minimal queueing noise.

Sean Gallagher: +1 please don’t run during APAC business hours if we’re sharing pool with hosted traffic. Also we need to pre-warm caches / avoid thundering herd on the cluster.

Harish Reddy: Proposed reservation plan (draft):
- eu-west: reserve 2x h100x8 nodes for 48h (Mon 00:00 UTC -> Wed 00:00 UTC)
- apac-sg: reserve 1x h100x8 node for 12h blocks (Mon/Tue 18:00-06:00 SGT)
If APAC H100 slips, fallback is a100x8 but we annotate + potentially keep APAC as “directional” until we can rerun on H100.

Rina Kobayashi: If apac-sg switches to A100, I’d rather hold publish and keep the run internal. Otherwise every external person will ask “why is APAC worse.”

Vanessa Ortiz: Understood. Let’s treat A100 fallback as emergency to unblock harness testing, not publish.

Neha Kapoor: Decision needed: are we OK delaying APAC publish if H100 doesn’t free up? I’d prefer to publish fewer regions than publish shaky data.

Jonas Weber: Yes. We can do internal APAC A100 run as a sanity check but only publish APAC once we get clean H100 sweep.

Vanessa Ortiz: Cool. Then I’ll request H100 only for apac-sg.

Harish Reddy: Instance type constraint callout: apac-sg h100x8 are split across two racks; cross-rack latency isn’t an issue for inference but if you’re doing multi-node it matters. Please keep TP within one node (no multi-node tensor parallel).

Claire Dubois: We’re single-node TP only. No multi-node.

Mateo Alvarez: For Mixtral 8x7B: it’s more sensitive to batching. Can we reserve enough headroom so we don’t hit queueing? I’d like to keep utilization <70% during measurement.

Jonas Weber: Concurrency target is 32–64 depending on workload slice. We can run in “steady state” and sample for ~20 mins per configuration. We can cap at 60% GPU util.

Sean Gallagher: Please add guardrails: max QPS limiter on the benchmark runners so a misconfig doesn’t hammer the shared control plane.

Rina Kobayashi: I can set `--qps-cap` and `--max-inflight` per runner. Also will randomize start offsets to avoid synchronized bursts.

Vanessa Ortiz: Ok, what’s the exact node count needed? If you reserve 1x h100x8 in apac-sg, can you run all models sequentially on that one node?

Jonas Weber: Yes, sequential is fine. We just need time. 8B/7B can even run on a single GPU but we’ll keep the same node class for consistency.

Harish Reddy: One more constraint: apac-sg has only 1 spare h100x8 at the moment and it’s currently earmarked for a potential Dedicated expansion. I can block it for the benchmark window but we need sign-off from @Neha Kapoor.

Neha Kapoor: Approved for the Mon/Tue overnight blocks as long as we release by Wed 06:00 SGT. If Sales screams, we may need to adjust.

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: Thanks. I’ll put in the reservation and label it “BENCHMARK_SWEEP_APAC_SG_H100” so it doesn’t get reclaimed.

Noah Patel: Quick check: do we have eu-west H100 of the same stepping/driver as apac-sg? Kernel selection differences have bitten us before.

Harish Reddy: eu-west H100 pool is on driver 550.54; apac-sg is 550.54 as well. CUDA minor versions match. We’re good.

Rina Kobayashi: Great. I’ll pin runner image digest anyway (dockerized runner) so deps are deterministic.

Vanessa Ortiz: Action items:
- Harish: file the reservations in fleet tool for eu-west 2x h100x8 (Mon-Wed UTC) + apac-sg 1x h100x8 (Mon/Tue 18:00-06:00 SGT)
- Jonas/Rina: confirm sweep duration estimate + send me the manifest matrix so I can sanity check runtime
- Sean: share the launch-week oncall window so we don’t collide with any planned maintenance

Sean Gallagher: No maintenance planned. But please post in #eng-oncall when runners start, so we know if alerts are benchmark-related.

Jonas Weber: Will do. Also: if apac-sg H100 becomes unavailable, we pause APAC publish and only publish us-east + eu-west in v1.

Vanessa Ortiz: Works. I’ll update the capacity doc + drop a note in the GTM thread so PMM isn’t surprised.

Harish Reddy: Reservations submitted. Pending approval in fleet system for eu-west H100 because one node is currently “warm spare” for an enterprise customer. I’ll resolve today.

Vanessa Ortiz: thx. Keep me posted if eu-west drops to 1 node; we can still run but reruns will take longer.

Rina Kobayashi: FYI runner settings I’m planning:
`--region eu-west --node-type h100x8 --qps-cap 40 --max-inflight 256 --warmup-minutes 15 --sample-minutes 20`
Same for apac-sg. Will tune if we see queueing.

Claire Dubois: Looks good. Please ensure we capture “capacity reserved” in run manifest so we can state results weren’t affected by noisy neighbors.

Jonas Weber: +1. We’ll include that in the methodology notes.

Vanessa Ortiz: Closing loop: once fleet approvals clear I’ll paste reservation IDs here.

Harish Reddy: eu-west H100 pool is on driver 550.54; apac-sg is 550.54 as well. CUDA minor versions match. We’re good.

Rina Kobayashi: Great. I’ll pin runner image digest anyway (dockerized runner) so deps are deterministic.

Vanessa Ortiz: Action items:
- Harish: file the reservations in fleet tool for eu-west 2x h100x8 (Mon-Wed UTC) + apac-sg 1x h100x8 (Mon/Tue 18:00-06:00 SGT)
- Jonas/Rina: confirm sweep duration estimate + send me the manifest matrix so I can sanity check runtime
- Sean: share the launch-week oncall window so we don’t collide with any planned maintenance

Sean Gallagher: No maintenance planned. But please post in #eng-oncall when runners start, so we know if alerts are benchmark-related.

Jonas Weber: Will do. Also: if apac-sg H100 becomes unavailable, we pause APAC publish and only publish us-east + eu-west in v1.

Vanessa Ortiz: Works. I’ll update the capacity doc + drop a note in the GTM thread so PMM isn’t surprised.

Harish Reddy: Reservations submitted. Pending approval in fleet system for eu-west H100 because one node is currently “warm spare” for an enterprise customer. I’ll resolve today.

Vanessa Ortiz: thx. Keep me posted if eu-west drops to 1 node; we can still run but reruns will take longer.

Rina Kobayashi: FYI runner settings I’m planning:
`--region eu-west --node-type h100x8 --qps-cap 40 --max-inflight 256 --warmup-minutes 15 --sample-minutes 20`
Same for apac-sg. Will tune if we see queueing.

Claire Dubois: Looks good. Please ensure we capture “capacity reserved” in run manifest so we can state results weren’t affected by noisy neighbors.

Jonas Weber: +1. We’ll include that in the methodology notes.

Vanessa Ortiz: Closing loop: once fleet approvals clear I’ll paste reservation IDs here.

source.thread:
Vanessa Ortiz: ok we need to lock the game day plan for predictive autoscaling + bounds this week. goal is to shake out failure modes before expanding pilot. can folks drop proposed scenarios + who’s driving + what we watch?

Rafael Mendes: +1. SRE ask: we should be explicit on (a) stop conditions, (b) rollback lever (feature flag), (c) comms channel + incident-lite roles.

Keiko Yamamoto: i can drive the actual exercise (autoscaler knobs + replay harness). can we pick a region/deployment target? would prefer a pilot-like dedicated deployment in us-east-1.

Jamal Pierce: +1 on us-east-1; capacity is stable enough this week. pls avoid wed 10-12 PT (capacity planning sync).

Allison Grant: for metrics: we should have the dashboard up and shared before starting. key panels i want:
- desired_replicas (raw) vs bounded_desired_replicas
- bound_hit_count (min/max) + bound_hit_rate
- queue_depth + queue_time
- saturation (gpu_util, kv_cache_pressure if available)
- scale_event timeline (reason, step size)
- prediction vs actual demand (and prediction staleness)

Sergio Costa: i can prep a dedicated “game day” dashboard view w/ per-deployment filters + annotations for each injected scenario. if there’s a canonical deployment_id, send it.

Miles Thornton: scenarios i’d like:
1) predictor returns stale forecast (timestamp old) -> ensure we fall back to reactive, no scale-to-zero, and we emit clear reason.
2) predictor returns null / empty -> same as above (this is where we had the pilot hotfix).
3) prediction overshoots by 2x during ramp -> ensure anti-oscillation + cooldown prevent thrash, and reactive can still scale down gradually.
4) max bound hit while traffic keeps rising -> confirm queue depth + latency alerts and we surface bounded_state for routing.
5) min bound cannot be met due to capacity shortage -> degraded state surfaced + alert + backoff behavior (no tight loop).

Mei Lin: for (1)/(2) i can simulate forecast provider outages by blocking the forecast endpoint in staging or returning known payloads. what environment are we running? if prod pilot, we should do “safe” toggles only.

Vanessa Ortiz: let’s do this in the Dedicated pilot sandbox account (not customer) but with prod-like configs. we can run the replay harness against that deployment and not affect customers.

Rafael Mendes: +1. We should still run with incident-lite discipline:
- Driver: Keiko
- Ops/Safety (stop/rollback): Rafael
- Observability: Allison/Sergio
- Forecast/pipeline: Mei/Sana
- Capacity: Jamal
Vanessa as overall coordinator.

Sana Farid: i can own the “prediction correctness” evaluation during the run. metrics:
- prediction_error_abs / prediction_error_pct
- lead_time (forecast horizon) actually used
- staleness_seconds
Also want to log when reactive overrides prediction (and why).

Keiko Yamamoto: proposed injected scenarios + order (low risk -> higher):
A) baseline: predictive OFF (reactive only) w/ bounds set (min=4, max=20) -> sanity check clamp + metrics
B) predictive ON, normal ramp (traffic increases linearly over 20m)
C) forecast stale -> should flip to reactive
D) forecast null -> should flip to reactive (verify we don’t downscale below safe baseline)
E) forecast overshoot -> watch oscillation/cooldowns
F) max bound hit -> ramp continues beyond max
G) min unmet -> simulate capacity shortage by limiting allocatable GPUs (if we can) or using the shortage simulator flag

Jamal Pierce: for G we can use the allocator “denylist” in the sandbox to mimic shortage (effectively caps provisionable). i can be on hand to flip it and revert.

Samantha Roy: do we want to validate the routing signal consumer too? i.e., when bounded at max, routing sees bounded_state=true and can degrade gracefully.

Vanessa Ortiz: yes. add as a check during F: confirm routing receives bounded_state within N seconds and we see fallback policy kick in (without violating bounds).

Allison Grant: +1. metrics to watch for that:
- routing_fallback_rate (or whatever Mina’s counter is)
- 4xx/5xx from routing
- p95 latency before/after fallback
If we don’t have routing metrics on the same dashboard, at least have links.

Sergio Costa: i’ll add links to:
- Autoscaling Bounds Dashboard (game day view)
- Routing capacity-signal dashboard
- Alert preview panel (max bound saturation + sustained queue)

Miles Thornton: stop conditions (suggest):
- p95 latency > 2x baseline for >5 min
- error rate > 1% for >2 min
- scale event loop > 1/min sustained (oscillation)
- any observed attempt to go below min bound
Immediate rollback: disable predictive mode flag for the deployment.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: + we should have a “hard stop” if bounded_desired != clamp(desired, min, max) (i.e., invariants violated). that’s a correctness bug.

Keiko Yamamoto: re tools: we’ll use the replay harness (pr-48279) + perf-canary scenario “traffic_ramp_20m”. i’ll paste the commands in the thread day-of.

Mei Lin: for forecast staleness/null, i can set the forecast provider to return:
- stale: valid forecast but `generated_at` - 45m
- null: 200 with empty body OR 204
Question: what’s the expected behavior between “null” and “error”? same fallback path?

Vanessa Ortiz: per ADR, both should map to “prediction unavailable” => reactive path, emit reason + metric. (no scale-down aggressive moves)

Sana Farid: please make sure we capture the decision logs/audit events during each scenario. we’ll need them for postmortem-style review.

Allison Grant: +1. if audit events are in Honeycomb, we should have a query saved: filter by deployment_id, group by decision_reason, show requested_desired vs bounded_desired.

Sergio Costa: i can prep that query + pin it. also will add annotations for each scenario start/stop.

Rafael Mendes: scheduling: can we do Thu 1pm PT? 60-90 min.

Jamal Pierce: Thu 1pm PT works.

Mei Lin: works.

Sana Farid: works.

Keiko Yamamoto: works. i’ll book it + send calendar with checklist.

Vanessa Ortiz: action items:
- Keiko: calendar invite + run sheet (scenario steps + expected outcomes)
- Sergio/Allison: dashboards + saved queries + alert preview ready by EOD Wed
- Jamal: shortage simulation toggle details + how to revert
- Mei/Sana: forecast provider payloads + staleness/null toggles
- Rafael: game day roles + stop/rollback criteria posted in invite

Rafael Mendes: will also post comms plan: we’ll use #eng-oncall for live updates and #incidents only if we trip stop conditions. :+1:

Samantha Roy: can someone confirm which deployment we’re using (name/id)? i’ll make sure telemetry labels are wired (customer_id=internal-sandbox, deployment_id=...).

Keiko Yamamoto: using dedicated deployment `ded-pilot-sandbox-use1` (i’ll drop the deployment_id once i grab it). thanks.

Vanessa Ortiz: cool. please keep notes in the run sheet + we’ll do a 20m retro after (what broke / missing metrics / docs updates).

source.content:
Irene Choi: Kicking off a thread to lock *module boundaries + ownership* for the VPC Terraform modules (alpha). We keep getting pulled into different directions by pilots, so would love to converge today and point folks to the ADR drafts. @Vivek Kulkarni @Victor Alvarez @Nina Petrova @Marcus Lin @Hanae Suzuki @Sahana Iyer @Bruno Silva @Connor O'Brien

Irene Choi: Proposed module split (names tbd):
- `vpc-core` (VPC + IGW + subnets + route tables + NAT)
- `vpc-endpoints` (GW + interface endpoints + private DNS behavior)
- `vpc-logging` (flow logs + destinations)
- `security-groups` (opinionated SG patterns for EKS/private deployment)
- `iam-scaffold` (installer execution role + boundaries)
- `examples/` (minimal env + “design partner” tfvars)

Irene Choi: Key question: who is DRI for each module + who is reviewer/approver? We also need to lock outputs expected by the installer.

Vivek Kulkarni: +1. From product POV: alpha should be opinionated and supportable. If we have 40 knobs it’s dead on arrival for SE/PS. I’m good with this breakdown.

Victor Alvarez: For split: `vpc-core` should include subnet layout + routing. Otherwise we’ll end up with circular deps across modules. I can DRI `vpc-core` + `vpc-endpoints` (at least initial defaults + docs).

Nina Petrova: Agree. Also please keep EKS constraints in mind:
- private subnets must have outbound path for pulling images *or* endpoints for ECR + S3 + STS etc
- if customers do private cluster endpoint, they still need endpoint access to AWS APIs
- avoid assumptions like “2 AZ only”

Nina Petrova: For `security-groups`: we should keep patterns simple: node->controlplane, node->endpoints, egress to NAT/endpoints, and ingress only via LB (if used). Don’t bake in pod-level network policies here.

Marcus Lin: Security take: ownership is fine, but we need gating on:
- IAM boundaries (no `*` on actions/resources unless explicitly justified)
- default flow logs enabled (with sane retention)
- endpoint policy defaults (at minimum, deny public bucket access patterns?)

Marcus Lin: I’d like Security Eng to be *required reviewer* for `iam-scaffold` + `vpc-logging` + any `vpc-endpoints` changes that affect private DNS / endpoint policies.

Hanae Suzuki: +1. Also please make sure modules don’t accidentally enable public egress by default in “private” mode. Customers interpret “private deployment” as “no internet egress unless explicitly allowed.”

Irene Choi: Makes sense. On egress: `vpc-core` will create NAT by default for alpha (to keep installs from failing), but we can include a flag like `enable_nat_gateway` + document that if disabled they must have endpoints (and/or proxy) for required services.

Victor Alvarez: I’m OK with NAT-by-default for alpha as long as:
1) it’s configurable
2) docs call out “this is an egress path”
3) SG defaults don’t open wide egress beyond what’s needed

Bruno Silva: Implementation thought: `vpc-core` outputs should be *stable* and minimal:
- `vpc_id`
- `private_subnet_ids` / `public_subnet_ids`
- `private_route_table_ids`
- `nat_gateway_ids` (if enabled)
- `availability_zones`
Then other modules take `vpc_id` + subnet IDs.

Sahana Iyer: From CI side, splitting is good. We can run unit validation on every module quickly and keep the expensive integration tests only on `examples/` or nightly. Ownership question: Infra can DRI CI + provider pinning + release workflow.

Connor O'Brien: Re: release ownership: happy to DRI versioning + changelog automation, but we need to agree semver semantics for alpha (breaking changes still happen, but we communicate + pin versions).

Irene Choi: Proposed ownership (DRI / required reviewers):
- `vpc-core`: DRI @Victor Alvarez; reviewers @Irene Choi @Nina Petrova
- `vpc-endpoints`: DRI @Victor Alvarez; reviewers @Nina Petrova + required @Marcus Lin or @Hanae Suzuki when private DNS/policies change
- `security-groups`: DRI @Bruno Silva; reviewers @Nina Petrova + required @Marcus Lin
- `vpc-logging`: DRI @Bruno Silva (unless someone else wants); required @Marcus Lin / @Hanae Suzuki
- `iam-scaffold`: DRI @Bruno Silva; required @Marcus Lin / @Hanae Suzuki
- CI/release: DRI @Sahana Iyer + @Connor O'Brien; reviewers @Irene Choi

Irene Choi: Any objections?

Marcus Lin: Looks good. One request: for `iam-scaffold` we should *not* try to encode every customer policy. Keep it to:
- permission boundary support
- role assumption path for installer
- minimal policies w/ clear extension points
Security will provide an allowlist of actions for installer (will sync separately).

Hanae Suzuki: +1. And in docs: explicitly state what is created vs what is expected to exist (e.g., customer-owned KMS keys, log destinations).

Vivek Kulkarni: No objections. Can we also get a quick pointer to the ADR drafts so SE knows where to comment?

Irene Choi: Yep. Drafts are here (still WIP):
- ADR-027 Module boundaries + interface: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-027-Terraform-module-boundaries-and-interface
- ADR-028 Validation + integration tests: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-028-Validation-and-integration-test-strategy-for-terraform
Please comment there for anything “principle-level” so we don’t relitigate in PRs.

Victor Alvarez: Adding a note on endpoint defaults: I suggest we treat these as “required for installer success in private subnets”:
- ECR (api + dkr)
- S3 gateway endpoint
- STS
- EC2
- CloudWatch Logs (if shipping logs there)
Optional:
- KMS
- SSM

Victor Alvarez: Private DNS: default enable for interface endpoints unless customer opts out. If they opt out, docs must include resolver rules + consequences.

Nina Petrova: +1. Also add a callout that intermittent DNS in private subnets is a common failure mode (resolver settings, DHCP options, etc.). We should expose `enable_dns_hostnames` and `enable_dns_support` as non-negotiable defaults.

Bruno Silva: For `security-groups`, can we align on which SGs exist? Suggest:
- `cluster_nodes_sg`
- `control_plane_access_sg` (if needed)
- `endpoint_access_sg` (attached to interface endpoints)
- `lb_ingress_sg` (optional)
Then outputs are SG IDs for installer / helm values.

Marcus Lin: That’s fine. Please avoid “allow all egress 0.0.0.0/0” as the default. If NAT exists, we can restrict egress to VPC CIDR + endpoint SGs + explicit `allowed_egress_cidrs` variable.

Irene Choi: (reacts :thumbsup:) We’ll bake that into the patterns. Also: we need to avoid cross-module implicit dependencies. If `security-groups` needs endpoint SG, it should take endpoint ENI SG IDs as inputs.

Sahana Iyer: CI proposal:
- per-PR: `terraform fmt -check`, `terraform validate`, `tflint` (aws ruleset), `checkov` baseline, `tfsec` optional (tbd)
- per-PR lightweight integration: apply `examples/minimal` in a sandbox account behind a manual gate OR only on `main`
- nightly: full apply/destroy across 2-3 permutations (NAT on/off, private DNS on/off)

Sahana Iyer: We’ll need a budgeted sandbox account; I’ll open an internal ticket.

Connor O'Brien: Semver for alpha: my suggestion:
- still use `v0.x.y`
- breaking changes bump `x`
- non-breaking features/bugfix bump `y`
- always publish changelog with “breaking / behavior change / migration notes” sections

Vivek Kulkarni: Works. For design partners we’ll pin versions in the example repo and only bump with coordination.

Irene Choi: Action items:
1) @Victor Alvarez finalize module list + README stub (today/tomorrow)
2) @Bruno Silva start `security-groups` + `iam-scaffold` skeletons with TODOs for Security
3) @Marcus Lin / @Hanae Suzuki add initial IAM action allowlist + logging defaults comments to ADR-027/028
4) @Sahana Iyer draft CI pipeline stages + note which run per PR vs nightly
5) @Connor O'Brien draft release tagging + changelog template

Irene Choi: We’ll treat ADR-027 as the “contract.” Once we merge it, we stop changing outputs without a loud callout.

Marcus Lin: (reacts :white_check_mark:) + will add required approvals in CODEOWNERS for the security-critical dirs once repo structure is set.

Victor Alvarez: Cool. I’ll also add a short “supported topologies” section (2-3 AZ, single/one-NAT vs per-AZ NAT, endpoints required list). No more than that for alpha.

Nina Petrova: Please include a note: per-AZ NAT is preferred for HA but expensive; alpha default could be “one NAT per AZ if >=2 AZ” unless cost becomes an issue in tests. We can discuss.

Irene Choi: Yeah, defaulting to per-AZ NAT is safest, but will watch CI cost/time. We can expose `nat_gateway_mode = "one_per_az"|"single"` and default to `one_per_az`.

Bruno Silva: Got it. I’ll mirror that in examples.

Vivek Kulkarni: Thanks all. Loop me in when ADR-027 is ready for sign-off; I’ll also ask SE to add comments in Confluence instead of Slack.

Irene Choi: Done. Closing the loop here once we get initial skeleton PR up.

source.text:
Vanessa Ortiz: kicking off a quick thread to de-risk bounds implementation before we merge the autoscaler changes. Need alignment on (1) edge cases + semantics (2) defaulting strategy for existing Dedicated deployments (3) who owns migration/backfill. I’m looking at ENG-22140 + ENG-22153.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1. I’m in the autoscaler loop code now. Biggest question: do we expect control plane to always send explicit `min_replicas` / `max_replicas`, or can they be null? i can clamp either way but need deterministic behavior.

Benji Okafor: schema PR (ENG-22141) is leaning nullable fields: `bounds_enabled` + `min_replicas?` + `max_replicas?` + cooldowns. Validation: if bounds_enabled=true then require both min/max and enforce min<=max. If bounds_enabled=false, we treat min/max as ignored even if present.

Jordan Lee: from policy engine POV, we can compute an “effective policy” snapshot that the autoscaler reads. Proposal:
- if feature flag off OR bounds_enabled=false => `effective_min`/`effective_max` = empty, autoscaler uses legacy behavior
- if bounds_enabled=true => clamp desired replicas into [min,max]
We should also emit a reason string for audit/logging: `clamp:min` / `clamp:max` / `no-bounds`.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok but we still need to decide what happens to *existing* deployments when we first roll out schema. we can’t suddenly require min/max everywhere.

Michael Nguyen: on migration/backfill: I can add columns/fields with null defaults and backfill policy rows in a “legacy mode” so reads don’t break. But we need to decide what value to set for existing deployments in DB.

Ethan Park: strong preference: do NOT backfill min/max for existing customers. Too risky (we’ll guess wrong). Instead:
- add new policy object w/ bounds_enabled=false by default
- only set bounds_enabled=true when explicitly enabled via console/api + feature flag
That preserves behavior.

Keiko Yamamoto: works for me as long as autoscaler treats “no policy / legacy policy” identically. I’ll code defensively: if policy missing or bounds_enabled=false => skip clamp.

Rafael Mendes: SRE lens: please avoid any migration that changes fleet behavior without explicit enablement. If we do backfill, it should be a no-op. Also we need a clear story on “max below current replicas” if a customer tightens bounds.

Vanessa Ortiz: yeah, edge cases list i want to cover:
1) min > max (should be rejected)
2) max < current_replicas on update
3) min > current_replicas on update
4) min=0 (do we allow scale-to-zero?)
5) predictor missing/stale; predictive wants 0 or null
6) region shortage: can’t meet min

Benji Okafor: (1) will be API validation error 400 w/ field-level message.
Re (4) min=0: I’d allow only if deployment has `allow_scale_to_zero=true` (we don’t expose that broadly for Dedicated today). Otherwise enforce min>=1.

Jordan Lee: +1. For Dedicated, scale-to-zero is basically never appropriate (baseline SLO + warm cache). We can have `min_replicas >= 1` as validation for Dedicated deployments.

Keiko Yamamoto: (2) if max < current, what’s desired? immediate downscale to max? or just prevent scale-ups and let reactive scale-down eventually bring it down?

Rafael Mendes: please do not immediately drop capacity on config change unless user explicitly asked and we can do it safely. But also: the customer *is* setting a hard ceiling, so we can’t keep running above max indefinitely.

Ethan Park: semantics I’d propose:
- ceiling is enforced on scale-up decisions immediately (never increase above max)
- if currently above max, autoscaler should schedule a controlled scale-down toward max, subject to cooldown + “safe scale-down” constraints (no step-down bigger than X per interval)
We should document that it may take up to N minutes to converge.

Vanessa Ortiz: i like that. Keiko can you implement “converge down to max” with dampening? We already have cooldown plumbing in the loop.

Keiko Yamamoto: yep. I’ll add:
- if current > max: treat desired = max (then pass through existing scale-down guards + cooldown)
- step size cap on downscale (e.g. 20% or at least 1 replica)
- emit `bound_exceeded_current` metric so dashboard shows “running above max” state.

Samantha Roy: for telemetry: can we standardize metrics now? I can wire:
- `autoscaler_desired_replicas`
- `autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas`
- `autoscaler_bound_hit_total{bound=min|max}`
- `autoscaler_current_above_max` gauge
Need labels: customer_id, deployment_id, region (with cardinality guard).

Vanessa Ortiz: yes, this thread is exactly to pin that down. Also (6) region shortage: if min cannot be met, autoscaler will keep requesting min, but scheduler may not place. We need degraded surfacing.

Rafael Mendes: + alert. “min unmet for >10m” is customer-impacting. But make sure we don’t page on transient placement delays.

Jordan Lee: policy engine can include a `min_unmet_behavior` later, but for v1 simplest:
- autoscaler continues to request min
- control plane marks deployment status `DEGRADED_MIN_UNMET` when provisioning fails with capacity shortage signal
- emit event + metric

Michael Nguyen: ownership question: who’s writing the backfill job? I can handle DB migration + backfill to set `bounds_enabled=false` for all existing Dedicated deployments. But I need the canonical definition of “existing deployment has no record” vs “record w/ bounds_enabled=false” for the autoscaler.

Keiko Yamamoto: from my side either works. If no record: treat as legacy. If record exists but bounds_enabled=false: treat as legacy. Just don’t 500.

Ethan Park: let’s do explicit record on backfill so console reads something consistent. But content should be equivalent to legacy behavior. i.e. bounds_enabled=false, predictive_enabled=false, cooldown fields default to current platform defaults.

Benji Okafor: careful: if we backfill cooldowns, we might accidentally change tuning. Suggest leaving cooldown fields null too and have the autoscaler apply its legacy defaults unless bounds_enabled/predictive_enabled flips on.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. legacy defaults should stay in autoscaler config for now. Only apply policy cooldowns when enabled.

Michael Nguyen: ok, plan:
- migration adds new policy table/fields
- backfill creates policy rows for existing Dedicated deployments with `bounds_enabled=false`, `predictive_enabled=false`, other fields null
- also write an audit log event `scaling_policy_initialized`? or skip to avoid noise?

Rafael Mendes: skip audit events for backfill IMO (or mark actor as `system` and suppress customer-visible export). Compliance folks might want it recorded but not spammy.

Jordan Lee: we can do: actor=`system/migration`, reason=`schema_backfill`, and in export pipeline we can filter those from default customer views. (Kevin Osei asked for that pattern in another thread.)

Vanessa Ortiz: good. last big one: predictor returning null/stale. We’ve seen “predictor says 0” in earlier prototypes. We need safe behavior.

Keiko Yamamoto: I’ll implement: if predictive_enabled=true and predictor is missing/stale/null => fall back to reactive desired (no predictive bias). Also NEVER treat null as 0. (I’ll add unit test; this bit me before :/)

Ethan Park: can we make that deterministic and logged? i.e. reason `prediction_unavailable_fallback=reactive`.

Samantha Roy: yep, I can add structured log field `prediction_state={ok|stale|null|error}`.

Jamal Pierce: on defaulting: do we want a “recommended range” shown in Console even if bounds disabled? Product is asking. That’s separate from enforcement, but we should not conflate recommended with hard bounds.

Vanessa Ortiz: agreed: recommended range is UI only. hard bounds are enforcement. We’ll keep them separate.

Rafael Mendes: action items:
- Vanessa/Keiko: confirm semantics doc for edge cases (max < current, min unmet)
- Michael: own migration/backfill implementation (ENG-22153)
- Benji/Jordan: lock validation rules (min>=1 for Dedicated, min<=max, bounds_enabled gating)
- Samantha: metric names + labels in line w/ observability standards

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: i’ll summarize in ENG-22140 and link this thread. Keiko, once you have a PR up, tag me + Rafael for review (esp. the “currently above max converge down” behavior).

Keiko Yamamoto: will do. aiming EOD tomorrow for the clamp + metrics + edge-case tests.

Michael Nguyen: starting migration branch now. I’ll coordinate with Benji on schema shape so I don’t drift.

Benji Okafor: i’ll drop the latest protobuf/json schema snippet here once it’s in review.

Vanessa Ortiz: thanks all.

3) min > current_replicas on update
4) min=0 (do we allow scale-to-zero?)
5) predictor missing/stale; predictive wants 0 or null
6) region shortage: can’t meet min

Benji Okafor: (1) will be API validation error 400 w/ field-level message.
Re (4) min=0: I’d allow only if deployment has `allow_scale_to_zero=true` (we don’t expose that broadly for Dedicated today). Otherwise enforce min>=1.

Jordan Lee: +1. For Dedicated, scale-to-zero is basically never appropriate (baseline SLO + warm cache). We can have `min_replicas >= 1` as validation for Dedicated deployments.

Keiko Yamamoto: (2) if max < current, what’s desired? immediate downscale to max? or just prevent scale-ups and let reactive scale-down eventually bring it down?

Rafael Mendes: please do not immediately drop capacity on config change unless user explicitly asked and we can do it safely. But also: the customer *is* setting a hard ceiling, so we can’t keep running above max indefinitely.

Ethan Park: semantics I’d propose:
- ceiling is enforced on scale-up decisions immediately (never increase above max)
- if currently above max, autoscaler should schedule a controlled scale-down toward max, subject to cooldown + “safe scale-down” constraints (no step-down bigger than X per interval)
We should document that it may take up to N minutes to converge.

Vanessa Ortiz: i like that. Keiko can you implement “converge down to max” with dampening? We already have cooldown plumbing in the loop.

Keiko Yamamoto: yep. I’ll add:
- if current > max: treat desired = max (then pass through existing scale-down guards + cooldown)
- step size cap on downscale (e.g. 20% or at least 1 replica)
- emit `bound_exceeded_current` metric so dashboard shows “running above max” state.

Samantha Roy: for telemetry: can we standardize metrics now? I can wire:
- `autoscaler_desired_replicas`
- `autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas`
- `autoscaler_bound_hit_total{bound=min|max}`
- `autoscaler_current_above_max` gauge
Need labels: customer_id, deployment_id, region (with cardinality guard).

Vanessa Ortiz: yes, this thread is exactly to pin that down. Also (6) region shortage: if min cannot be met, autoscaler will keep requesting min, but scheduler may not place. We need degraded surfacing.

Rafael Mendes: + alert. “min unmet for >10m” is customer-impacting. But make sure we don’t page on transient placement delays.

Jordan Lee: policy engine can include a `min_unmet_behavior` later, but for v1 simplest:
- autoscaler continues to request min
- control plane marks deployment status `DEGRADED_MIN_UNMET` when provisioning fails with capacity shortage signal
- emit event + metric

Michael Nguyen: ownership question: who’s writing the backfill job? I can handle DB migration + backfill to set `bounds_enabled=false` for all existing Dedicated deployments. But I need the canonical definition of “existing deployment has no record” vs “record w/ bounds_enabled=false” for the autoscaler.

Keiko Yamamoto: from my side either works. If no record: treat as legacy. If record exists but bounds_enabled=false: treat as legacy. Just don’t 500.

Ethan Park: let’s do explicit record on backfill so console reads something consistent. But content should be equivalent to legacy behavior. i.e. bounds_enabled=false, predictive_enabled=false, cooldown fields default to current platform defaults.

Benji Okafor: careful: if we backfill cooldowns, we might accidentally change tuning. Suggest leaving cooldown fields null too and have the autoscaler apply its legacy defaults unless bounds_enabled/predictive_enabled flips on.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. legacy defaults should stay in autoscaler config for now. Only apply policy cooldowns when enabled.

Michael Nguyen: ok, plan:
- migration adds new policy table/fields
- backfill creates policy rows for existing Dedicated deployments with `bounds_enabled=false`, `predictive_enabled=false`, other fields null
- also write an audit log event `scaling_policy_initialized`? or skip to avoid noise?

Rafael Mendes: skip audit events for backfill IMO (or mark actor as `system` and suppress customer-visible export). Compliance folks might want it recorded but not spammy.

Jordan Lee: we can do: actor=`system/migration`, reason=`schema_backfill`, and in export pipeline we can filter those from default customer views. (Kevin Osei asked for that pattern in another thread.)

Vanessa Ortiz: good. last big one: predictor returning null/stale. We’ve seen “predictor says 0” in earlier prototypes. We need safe behavior.

Keiko Yamamoto: I’ll implement: if predictive_enabled=true and predictor is missing/stale/null => fall back to reactive desired (no predictive bias). Also NEVER treat null as 0. (I’ll add unit test; this bit me before :/)

Ethan Park: can we make that deterministic and logged? i.e. reason `prediction_unavailable_fallback=reactive`.

Samantha Roy: yep, I can add structured log field `prediction_state={ok|stale|null|error}`.

Jamal Pierce: on defaulting: do we want a “recommended range” shown in Console even if bounds disabled? Product is asking. That’s separate from enforcement, but we should not conflate recommended with hard bounds.

Vanessa Ortiz: agreed: recommended range is UI only. hard bounds are enforcement. We’ll keep them separate.

Rafael Mendes: action items:
- Vanessa/Keiko: confirm semantics doc for edge cases (max < current, min unmet)
- Michael: own migration/backfill implementation (ENG-22153)
- Benji/Jordan: lock validation rules (min>=1 for Dedicated, min<=max, bounds_enabled gating)
- Samantha: metric names + labels in line w/ observability standards

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: i’ll summarize in ENG-22140 and link this thread. Keiko, once you have a PR up, tag me + Rafael for review (esp. the “currently above max converge down” behavior).

Keiko Yamamoto: will do. aiming EOD tomorrow for the clamp + metrics + edge-case tests.

Michael Nguyen: starting migration branch now. I’ll coordinate with Benji on schema shape so I don’t drift.

Benji Okafor: i’ll drop the latest protobuf/json schema snippet here once it’s in review.

Vanessa Ortiz: thanks all.

Benji Okafor: schema PR (ENG-22141) is leaning nullable fields: `bounds_enabled` + `min_replicas?` + `max_replicas?` + cooldowns. Validation: if bounds_enabled=true then require both min/max and enforce min<=max. If bounds_enabled=false, we treat min/max as ignored even if present.

- if feature flag off OR bounds_enabled=false => `effective_min`/`effective_max` = empty, autoscaler uses legacy behavior
- if bounds_enabled=true => clamp desired replicas into [min,max]

- add new policy object w/ bounds_enabled=false by default
- only set bounds_enabled=true when explicitly enabled via console/api + feature flag
That preserves behavior.

Keiko Yamamoto: works for me as long as autoscaler treats “no policy / legacy policy” identically. I’ll code defensively: if policy missing or bounds_enabled=false => skip clamp.

- emit `bound_exceeded_current` metric so dashboard shows “running above max” state.

- `autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas`
- `autoscaler_bound_hit_total{bound=min|max}`
- `autoscaler_current_above_max` gauge

source.text:
Bruno Silva: Heads up: Terraform module changes for KMS are ready for review. This is the follow-on to the least-privilege work + rotation role split. Draft PR: https://github.com/redwood-terraform/pull/611 ("add least-privilege KMS IAM modules").

Bruno Silva: TL;DR
- New submodules: `kms_runtime_role` (Decrypt/DataKey) and `kms_rotation_role` (DescribeKey + CreateGrant/PutKeyPolicy bits depending on cloud).
- Default behavior stays close to existing, but we *will* add new outputs and a couple of variables.
- Need to decide version bump + compatibility notes for existing Private customers.

Jordan Lee: looking now. Do we currently have customers pinning the module version in their Private deploy repos? If yes, we need to be careful about a breaking major.

Bruno Silva: yep, most Private customers pin `redwood/private` terraform modules. Some are pinned by installer-generated `versions.tf` too. That’s why I’m trying to avoid a surprise break.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE side: the big concern is rollout sequence. If we ship module changes that tighten IAM and someone applies before the runtime wrapper cutover is fully deployed, we get decrypt failures.

Bruno Silva: +1. In PR I kept the existing policy attachments as defaults, and added a new `enable_separate_rotation_principal` flag (default false) so we don’t force split principals immediately.

Jordan Lee: I see it. Variable name: `separate_rotation_principal_enabled` in one file and `enable_separate_rotation_principal` in README. Can we standardize? :)

Bruno Silva: good catch, will fix.

Marcus Lin: Security POV: we should still message this as “recommended split roles” and call out that rotation actions should not run under the runtime decrypt principal. But I’m ok with defaulting to current behavior for compatibility.

Allison Grant: also please make sure module exports the role ARN consistently. Observability hooks expect the label `kms_principal` to match what we document (role ARN string).

Bruno Silva: module outputs right now:
- existing: `kms_role_arn`
- new: `kms_runtime_role_arn`, `kms_rotation_role_arn`
I left `kms_role_arn` as an alias to runtime for now.

Jordan Lee: that alias helps. Please document that `kms_role_arn` is deprecated but still present.

Vivek Kulkarni: for Private: installer will generate terraform config. We can update installer templates to use the new outputs, but we need a path for existing customers who don’t upgrade installer immediately.

Bruno Silva: exactly. Proposed compatibility plan:
1) Terraform module release: minor bump (e.g. 3.6.0 -> 3.7.0) w/ new roles behind flag, keep old outputs.
2) Installer update references new outputs but continues to accept old variables if present.
3) After 1-2 quarters, we can do a major bump that removes deprecated outputs.

Connor O'Brien: release process question: do we publish these modules with semver tags? if so, we should treat output removals as major.

Bruno Silva: yes, internal semver tags. That’s why I’m leaning minor now.

Sean Gallagher: can we also add a “safety check” note: apply in staging first; verify decrypt calls before tightening? People will copy/paste.

Bruno Silva: yep. Adding README section: “Upgrade notes (Private existing customers)” with a checklist:
- Ensure KMS wrapper + circuit breaker changes deployed (>= runtime build X)
- Apply terraform
- Confirm dashboards: KMS AccessDenied=0, latency stable
- Then enable `enable_separate_rotation_principal=true` in a follow-up change

Jordan Lee: please be explicit about permissions.
Runtime principal should need:
- `kms:Decrypt`
- `kms:GenerateDataKey` (or `GenerateDataKeyWithoutPlaintext` depending)
Rotation principal should need:
- `kms:DescribeKey`
- and whatever is used to update alias/key versioning from our control plane (we generally don’t want `kms:PutKeyPolicy` unless customer model requires it)

Marcus Lin: +1. Also mention: customers may have SCPs / org policies; we should avoid adding optional permissions by default.

Bruno Silva: PR currently uses a conservative base policy and exposes `extra_kms_actions_runtime` / `extra_kms_actions_rotation` lists if needed.

Allison Grant: any renames to metric tags needed? If the role changes, we should ensure dashboards don’t break. But that’s probably on the app side.

Sean Gallagher: oncall pain point: customers apply Terraform and then file a ticket with an AccessDenied. If we can add a note: “common failure mode: key policy doesn’t allow new role ARN” that’d save cycles.

Bruno Silva: adding to README troubleshooting:
- “AccessDenied after applying: update key policy to trust the runtime role ARN; if enabling split roles, ensure both ARNs are trusted during transition.”

Vivek Kulkarni: can we align this with installer preflight validation? The installer PR adds checks (I think PR-392). If terraform changes cause new role ARN, preflight should warn if key policy doesn’t include it.

Bruno Silva: yep, I’ll sync w/ that. https://github.com/redwood-private-installer/pull/392 is the one.

Jordan Lee: one more: module currently changes the default session name / role path? Some customers rely on predictable IAM paths.

Bruno Silva: no role path change. Names will be stable unless `name_prefix` differs. I did add suffixes `-runtime` and `-rotation` when split enabled.

Connor O'Brien: what’s the plan for existing customers who already have a single role and don’t want terraform to replace it? Will enabling split force replacement?

Bruno Silva: enabling split creates *additional* role resources, doesn’t destroy existing. If they keep split disabled, resources are unchanged.

Sean Gallagher: good. Also please avoid “force new” on the role by changing assume role policy unexpectedly.

Bruno Silva: no assume role policy changes when split disabled.

Marcus Lin: version bump proposal sounds right: minor bump now with deprecation warnings.

Jordan Lee: can you add a changelog entry that explicitly says “no breaking changes if you don’t enable split”? People skim.

Bruno Silva: will do.

Allison Grant: if we can get a single canonical snippet for Private upgrade docs, I can link it from the KMS dashboards page too.

Bruno Silva: I’ll paste draft snippet here once README updates are in.

Sean Gallagher: request: include a note about staged rollout: enable split roles only after verifying dual-read / rotation window logic if they’re doing key rotation same week.

Bruno Silva: added note: “Don’t combine IAM tightening + key rotation cutover in the same maintenance window unless you have rollback.”

Vivek Kulkarni: :thumbsup: also note some customers do quarterly rotations w/ audit evidence; they’ll ask if this changes their rotation procedure.

Marcus Lin: we can answer: “no change required; split roles is recommended hardening; rotation procedure is the same, just uses different principal.”

Bruno Silva: ok, action items from this thread:
- Fix variable naming consistency
- Add README: upgrade notes, troubleshooting, staged rollout checklist
- Add changelog entry for minor bump
- Confirm no unexpected role replacement
I’ll push updates and re-request review on PR-611 today.

Jordan Lee: thanks. I’ll do detailed policy review once updated.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll skim for rollout hazards / defaults.

Connor O'Brien: once merged, ping me and I’ll coordinate tagging + internal announcement so Private folks don’t get surprised.

Bruno Silva: will do.

- Need to decide version bump + compatibility notes for existing Private customers.

Jordan Lee: looking now. Do we currently have customers pinning the module version in their Private deploy repos? If yes, we need to be careful about a breaking major.

Bruno Silva: yep, most Private customers pin `redwood/private` terraform modules. Some are pinned by installer-generated `versions.tf` too. That’s why I’m trying to avoid a surprise break.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE side: the big concern is rollout sequence. If we ship module changes that tighten IAM and someone applies before the runtime wrapper cutover is fully deployed, we get decrypt failures.

Bruno Silva: +1. In PR I kept the existing policy attachments as defaults, and added a new `enable_separate_rotation_principal` flag (default false) so we don’t force split principals immediately.

Jordan Lee: I see it. Variable name: `separate_rotation_principal_enabled` in one file and `enable_separate_rotation_principal` in README. Can we standardize? :)

Bruno Silva: good catch, will fix.

Marcus Lin: Security POV: we should still message this as “recommended split roles” and call out that rotation actions should not run under the runtime decrypt principal. But I’m ok with defaulting to current behavior for compatibility.

Allison Grant: also please make sure module exports the role ARN consistently. Observability hooks expect the label `kms_principal` to match what we document (role ARN string).

Bruno Silva: module outputs right now:
- existing: `kms_role_arn`
- new: `kms_runtime_role_arn`, `kms_rotation_role_arn`
I left `kms_role_arn` as an alias to runtime for now.

Jordan Lee: that alias helps. Please document that `kms_role_arn` is deprecated but still present.

Vivek Kulkarni: for Private: installer will generate terraform config. We can update installer templates to use the new outputs, but we need a path for existing customers who don’t upgrade installer immediately.

Bruno Silva: exactly. Proposed compatibility plan:
1) Terraform module release: minor bump (e.g. 3.6.0 -> 3.7.0) w/ new roles behind flag, keep old outputs.
2) Installer update references new outputs but continues to accept old variables if present.
3) After 1-2 quarters, we can do a major bump that removes deprecated outputs.

Connor O'Brien: release process question: do we publish these modules with semver tags? if so, we should treat output removals as major.

Bruno Silva: yes, internal semver tags. That’s why I’m leaning minor now.

Sean Gallagher: can we also add a “safety check” note: apply in staging first; verify decrypt calls before tightening? People will copy/paste.

Bruno Silva: yep. Adding README section: “Upgrade notes (Private existing customers)” with a checklist:
- Ensure KMS wrapper + circuit breaker changes deployed (>= runtime build X)
- Apply terraform
- Confirm dashboards: KMS AccessDenied=0, latency stable
- Then enable `enable_separate_rotation_principal=true` in a follow-up change

Jordan Lee: please be explicit about permissions.
Runtime principal should need:
- `kms:Decrypt`
- `kms:GenerateDataKey` (or `GenerateDataKeyWithoutPlaintext` depending)
Rotation principal should need:
- `kms:DescribeKey`
- and whatever is used to update alias/key versioning from our control plane (we generally don’t want `kms:PutKeyPolicy` unless customer model requires it)

Marcus Lin: +1. Also mention: customers may have SCPs / org policies; we should avoid adding optional permissions by default.

Bruno Silva: PR currently uses a conservative base policy and exposes `extra_kms_actions_runtime` / `extra_kms_actions_rotation` lists if needed.

Allison Grant: any renames to metric tags needed? If the role changes, we should ensure dashboards don’t break. But that’s probably on the app side.

Sean Gallagher: oncall pain point: customers apply Terraform and then file a ticket with an AccessDenied. If we can add a note: “common failure mode: key policy doesn’t allow new role ARN” that’d save cycles.

Bruno Silva: adding to README troubleshooting:
- “AccessDenied after applying: update key policy to trust the runtime role ARN; if enabling split roles, ensure both ARNs are trusted during transition.”

Vivek Kulkarni: can we align this with installer preflight validation? The installer PR adds checks (I think PR-392). If terraform changes cause new role ARN, preflight should warn if key policy doesn’t include it.

Bruno Silva: yep, I’ll sync w/ that. https://github.com/redwood-private-installer/pull/392 is the one.

Jordan Lee: one more: module currently changes the default session name / role path? Some customers rely on predictable IAM paths.

Bruno Silva: no role path change. Names will be stable unless `name_prefix` differs. I did add suffixes `-runtime` and `-rotation` when split enabled.

Connor O'Brien: what’s the plan for existing customers who already have a single role and don’t want terraform to replace it? Will enabling split force replacement?

Bruno Silva: enabling split creates *additional* role resources, doesn’t destroy existing. If they keep split disabled, resources are unchanged.

Sean Gallagher: good. Also please avoid “force new” on the role by changing assume role policy unexpectedly.

Bruno Silva: no assume role policy changes when split disabled.

Marcus Lin: version bump proposal sounds right: minor bump now with deprecation warnings.

Jordan Lee: can you add a changelog entry that explicitly says “no breaking changes if you don’t enable split”? People skim.

Bruno Silva: will do.

Allison Grant: if we can get a single canonical snippet for Private upgrade docs, I can link it from the KMS dashboards page too.

Bruno Silva: I’ll paste draft snippet here once README updates are in.

Sean Gallagher: request: include a note about staged rollout: enable split roles only after verifying dual-read / rotation window logic if they’re doing key rotation same week.

Bruno Silva: added note: “Don’t combine IAM tightening + key rotation cutover in the same maintenance window unless you have rollback.”

Vivek Kulkarni: :thumbsup: also note some customers do quarterly rotations w/ audit evidence; they’ll ask if this changes their rotation procedure.

Marcus Lin: we can answer: “no change required; split roles is recommended hardening; rotation procedure is the same, just uses different principal.”

Bruno Silva: ok, action items from this thread:
- Fix variable naming consistency
- Add README: upgrade notes, troubleshooting, staged rollout checklist
- Add changelog entry for minor bump
- Confirm no unexpected role replacement
I’ll push updates and re-request review on PR-611 today.

Jordan Lee: thanks. I’ll do detailed policy review once updated.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll skim for rollout hazards / defaults.

Connor O'Brien: once merged, ping me and I’ll coordinate tagging + internal announcement so Private folks don’t get surprised.

Bruno Silva: will do.

source.body:
Connor O'Brien: quick q for min-pack: do we *need* OTLP log export in v1, or is “supported sinks = Splunk HEC + Elastic/OpenSearch + (maybe) Loki” enough? trying to lock scope for the release branch.

Logan Wright: depends what we mean by “OTLP”. If we ship an otel-collector in the cluster and say “send to your OTLP endpoint”, that’s a whole support surface (auth, retries, tls, backpressure, perf). For v1 I’d rather standardize on 1 collector + 2-3 explicit outputs.

Colin O'Donnell: +1. from customer calls: 80% ask “Splunk HEC?” / “Elastic?” and then “can you not break our cluster”. OTLP comes up mostly as “we already have an otel pipeline” but they usually can adapt if we give them JSON logs and a stable namespace.

Allison Grant: reminder: standards doc currently says “provide export options suitable for private envs (SIEM/Splunk/ELK/Loki/OTLP endpoints)” but it doesn’t say *all* are required for v1. We can define OTLP as “documented integration” vs “supported”.

Rafael Mendes: from oncall POV: OTLP = lots of “why is nothing showing up” tickets. Splunk HEC and Elastic are already in the support playbooks, easier to reproduce.

Caleb Johnson: question: are we talking logs only, or traces too? OTLP for traces is even more contentious. v1 scope says “where feasible traces”, but we’ve been treating traces as best-effort.

Connor O'Brien: mainly logs. but I’m getting push from a couple folks that “OTLP is the standard”, we should do it right.

Diego Santos: packaging angle: air-gapped installs. If we ship otel-collector we have to pin image versions + config, and customers will inevitably tweak. Then upgrades get fun.

Neha Kapoor: also security review: OTLP endpoints often require mTLS and customer-managed cert chains. That’s totally doable but it becomes a long tail. Splunk HEC also has TLS but it’s a narrower config surface.

Markus Klein: fwiw from SE side: enterprise platform teams like OTLP because it plugs into existing observability pipelines. But if we can say “we ship vector/fluent-bit collector; here’s a tested recipe to forward to your OTLP gateway”, that may satisfy without us owning every permutation.

Logan Wright: what if we split:
- v1 “Supported sinks”: Splunk HEC, Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, Loki (if we keep it)
- “Experimental/Documented”: OTLP HTTP (logs only) behind a values flag, no SLA
- traces: explicitly out-of-scope for minimum pack (or “off by default”)

Allison Grant: I like that, but wording matters. “Supported” should mean Support can triage w/ known-good configs. “Documented” can be “we don’t guarantee, but here’s an example.”

Colin O'Donnell: +1 and for OTLP we should limit to *one* mode. OTLP/HTTP is simpler than OTLP/gRPC in locked-down networks. Also keep auth methods minimal (bearer token + tls).

Caleb Johnson: OTLP/HTTP for logs is reasonable. But if we do it, please cap batch sizes + retry to avoid the “collector OOMs because endpoint is down” scenario. We saw that in hosted.

Rafael Mendes: :point_up: and default to “drop on backpressure” vs “block app” (or at least make it very explicit). I don’t want inference latency coupled to a customer SIEM outage.

Diego Santos: if we add OTLP, we need installer preflight checks? like DNS reachability / NetworkPolicy egress? otherwise people will enable it and it silently fails.

Connor O'Brien: for min pack I’d rather not add more preflights. already have storage + scrape targets + grafana ds auth checks.

Neha Kapoor: also: log content. if OTLP is enabled and customer ships logs out, they need the same redaction guarantees. So any OTLP config must not encourage exporting request payloads etc. (same as splunk/elk)

Logan Wright: ok, proposal:
1) v1 GA: Splunk HEC + Elastic/OpenSearch are “Supported export targets”
2) Loki is “Supported” only if we ship it as part of bundle (still TBD)
3) OTLP/HTTP logs: “Documented integration (beta)” with strict schema + limited knobs; no preflight; off by default
4) Traces: not in minimum pack v1 (mention future)

Allison Grant: works. please make sure docs clearly list support boundaries and have a troubleshooting section: “how to see collector queue/backpressure metrics”, “how to verify export attempts”.

Colin O'Donnell: can we add one line to docs: “If you already run your own otel collector, prefer scraping kubernetes logs and forwarding yourself; our supported path is Splunk/Elastic.” sets expectation.

Caleb Johnson: if we do the beta OTLP, I can add a sample config snippet and make sure we emit collector metrics (exporter errors, dropped logs) into Prometheus. That at least gives us signals.

Rafael Mendes: + runbook link in alert annotations. even for beta, we should avoid silent failure.

Connor O'Brien: cool. I’ll update release brief + scope doc: OTLP not required for v1, beta doc-only integration acceptable. owners?

Logan Wright: I’ll own the wording + values schema change.

Caleb Johnson: I’ll own the sample OTLP/HTTP config + metrics exposure (if we have time).

Colin O'Donnell: I’ll sanity check against customer integration matrix + make sure Splunk/Elastic stays the headline.

Neha Kapoor: tag me on the PRs touching export + retention so security can review.

Allison Grant: please also add a note about label/cardinality if you add exporter metrics; keep it bounded.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: locking this for v1: Splunk/ELK supported; OTLP optional beta. I’ll drop a comment on the Linear ticket and proceed with branch plan.
