Logan Wright: Heads up / quick retro from the Sev1 earlier — telemetry query latency spiked hard right when incident-bot started pulling saturation cards.

Grafana Bot: Alert triggered: telemetry-query-service p95_latency_ms > 5000 for 10m (current: 8120ms). Dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/telemetry-query-service

Rafael Mendes: +1, oncall perspective: bot was timing out and then responders started manually refreshing dashboards -> made it worse. Need guardrails.

Miles Thornton: I pulled logs from incident-bot. We went from ~3 qps to ~70 qps in ~2 mins when people hit “Refresh signals” in multiple threads. Also Slack interactive actions retried a few times when ack was late.

Devon Price: ack deadline for Slack interactive is 3s; we were doing live query before ack in a couple paths. That causes retry storms. We can flip to ack-immediately + async update, but still need query cost controls.

Nadia Rahman: Looking at query service traces — the expensive ones are the “regional saturation overview” fanout queries. They expand to ~18 metric series per region * pool * model, and then we do 5m + 30m windows. During the incident, cardinality was already elevated.

Caleb Johnson: Yep, and some are uncached due to tenant param being part of cache key even when query is region-level. So we miss cache and re-run same query N times.

Jacob Stein: This aligns with what we saw: bot card mostly needs the *summary* (e.g., GPU util high + queue depth high + KV cache pressure) and then links to deeper dashboards. We shouldn’t compute the whole dashboard query set inline.

Aisha Bello: +1. Also: we should define a query budget for incident-bot per thread + per workspace. If it exceeds budget, bot should fall back to cached context and post deep links.

Allison Grant: For “fallback to links” behavior, can we standardize the deep links and time windows? During incident I saw people open dashboards with wrong time range.

Dylan Brooks: Proposal:
1) Add request coalescing in telemetry-query-service for identical queries (keyed by normalized query + time bucket).
2) Add cache with small TTL (30-60s) + stale-while-revalidate for bot use.
3) Enforce hard limits: max fanout, max series returned, and max query time (e.g., 2s) for bot scope.
4) If any limit hits: return partial + “degraded mode” payload with links.

Rafael Mendes: What do we mean by “normalized query”? We need to ensure it collapses equivalent requests across threads. Eg same region/service but different incident id.

Caleb Johnson: Normalize away incident_id/thread_id; round end_time to nearest 30s bucket; enforce fixed lookback windows for bot (5m, 30m). Also canonicalize label filters ordering.

Nadia Rahman: Another thing: query service currently lets callers ask arbitrary window. Bot sometimes requested 2h because it used “incident start” which changed as people edited the incident doc. Should pin to a fixed set of windows.

Miles Thornton: On bot side: we can remove “Refresh signals” from v2 cards during active incident unless you’re in a whitelisted oncall role. Or throttle it (1 refresh / 60s / thread).

Devon Price: We should also make refresh idempotent + ignore repeated clicks while one is in-flight. Slack UI feels like “nothing happened” so folks click 5x.

Logan Wright: I’m also worried about the query service being in the same blast radius. During incidents, telemetry is often degraded. Bot should not depend on it being healthy to be useful.

Jacob Stein: Exactly. For saturation card we can do:
- Prefer cached last-known-good for region/pool summary
- If live query >2s, stop + show cached + “data may be stale”
- Always attach links to dashboards + traces/logs for drilldown

Allison Grant: Re links: we have agreed formats in the incident-context guidelines draft. Needs to include service, region, and an explicit time window (now-30m..now). Also a “copy query” for power users.

Aisha Bello: Also want alerts tuned so we don’t page on the *telemetry tool* being slow unless it impacts SLO diagnosis broadly. But we do need a canary to detect when bot queries are slow (separate from generic query load).

Dylan Brooks: There’s already a ticket in flight for caching/coalescing (ENG-5427). We should add explicit “bot budget” enforcement + circuit breaker semantics.

Nadia Rahman: Circuit breaker idea: if p95 > X for Y minutes, query service returns 429-like “budget exceeded” to bot scope, and bot flips to link-only mode automatically. Avoid death spiral.

Rafael Mendes: +1. In the incident, responders were basically DDoSing telemetry without knowing. A clear message in the bot card would have helped.

Incident Bot: :warning: Some diagnostics may be incomplete due to telemetry query latency. Try dashboard links or rerun later.

Miles Thornton: Can we make that message more actionable?
Something like: “Telemetry is slow (p95 8s). Showing cached signals from 2m ago. For live data: open Saturation Overview dashboard (region prefilled).”

Devon Price: Yes. Also: we should ack Slack instantly and then update message when ready. If it’s degraded, update with that explanation. No retries.

Caleb Johnson: One more culprit: a couple queries were pulling scheduler backpressure with high-cardinality labels (fallback routing tags). I saw series count jump 10x. There was a recent hotfix PR to reduce that cardinality; ensure it’s deployed everywhere.

Logan Wright: Noted. I’ll check rollout status w/ release eng. Also, action items below.

Logan Wright: Action items (proposed):
- Telemetry team (Nadia/Caleb): implement query normalization + coalescing + TTL cache + stale-while-revalidate for bot scope. Add max-series + max-fanout + max-runtime enforcement. (maps to ENG-5427 + new subtask)
- Bot team (Miles/Devon/Dylan): ack-first, in-flight dedupe, per-thread refresh throttle, and degraded-mode messaging w/ links. (maps to ENG-5425 + ENG-5429)
- Observability (Allison/Aisha): confirm link formats/time windows + add perf canary for bot query latency and alert route. (maps to perf-canary PR)

Aisha Bello: Looks good. For budgets, can we define something concrete?
E.g. per incident thread: <= 10 live queries / 5 min; per workspace: <= 100 live queries / 5 min; else cached/links.

Nadia Rahman: Reasonable. We should enforce server-side too (query service) so bots can’t bypass.

Caleb Johnson: +1. Also separate budgets for tenant-aware queries (more expensive) vs region-level.

Rafael Mendes: For SRE: please add a snippet to oncall playbook: if bot shows degraded mode, don’t spam refresh; use links; if needed, run manual CLI which is rate-limited separately.

Jacob Stein: I’ll update the runbook section for “telemetry degraded” + what the saturation card means when cached.

Allison Grant: I’ll sync with Chloe on Console deep links. Need to ensure links land on the right region/pool and default to now-30m.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll open the additional Linear subtask for query budgets + degraded-mode contract between bot and query service.

Logan Wright: Thanks all. Let’s aim to have the cache/coalesce + bot throttle in before v2 broad rollout. This was a good warning shot.

source.thread:
Vanessa Ortiz: We need to lock initial guardrail defaults for burst GA. Specifically: max burst ratio, cooldown window, and *how we tune without per-customer snowflakes*. Can we align today so Fleet can size pools and SRE can write runbooks?

Keiko Yamamoto: +1. Current draft in the autoscaler doc is max burst ratio = 2.0x baseline reserved GPUs, cooldown = 10 min after hitting cap, and global fleet safety cap = 25% of burst pool capacity per region. But those are placeholders.

Jamal Pierce: From the capacity model side, if we assume 2.0x baseline allowed, we should expect people to try it. Our pool sizing gets ugly if “2x” is the default for everyone.

Harish Reddy: Also operationally, if we make defaults too aggressive we’ll spend the first month explaining denials + rolling back. Support will get buried.

Jordan Lee: Reminder: ratio is only one lever. The fairness/token bucket layer will still enforce instantaneous limits based on available burst credits + pool saturation. But I agree defaults matter because they shape customer expectations.

Ethan Park: On “no snowflakes”: policy engine can support profiles. e.g. `burst_profile = standard|conservative|aggressive` where each maps to a small fixed set of params (max_ratio, cooldown, queue_limit, priority tier). Per-tenant overrides exist but require admin approval + audit log.

Sean Gallagher: SRE POV: we need predictable behavior to page on. If every big customer has bespoke knobs, alerts become meaningless. I’d prefer 2-3 profiles max, with an explicit “custom” requiring Sev2 change control.

Peter Holtz: +1. Also routing needs deterministic fallback semantics. If cooldown varies per tenant wildly, debugging becomes “why was I denied?” across 100 configs.

Vanessa Ortiz: What’s a sane *default* ratio then? 1.2x? 1.5x? 2x?

Jamal Pierce: If we’re talking “default for standard tier” I’d vote 1.5x baseline as the max *sustained* burst. For instantaneous spikes we can still allow brief above that via token bucket burstiness, but over a 5-10 min window 1.5x.

Keiko Yamamoto: Clarify: ratio in config is peak ceiling, not sustained. But we can set it to 1.5x and still allow the token bucket to have e.g. 60–120 seconds of additional burst credits (depending on pool health).

Jordan Lee: We can express this cleanly:
- `max_burst_ratio` (hard ceiling)
- `burst_credit_window_sec` (token bucket capacity)
- `cooldown_sec` (after denial due to cap)
We should keep `burst_credit_window_sec` global per pool or per profile, not per tenant.

Ravi Menon: Routing angle: we’d like cooldown to be long enough to avoid thundering herds retrying, but short enough that “legit peaks” don’t get punished for a full hour. 10 min feels long; 2-5 min maybe.

Mina Haddad: Agree. Clients will retry with backoff. If cooldown is 10 min and they’re over cap, they’ll see a wall of 429s and blame us.

Sean Gallagher: We can keep cooldown shorter but couple it with “denial reason codes” that instruct behavior. E.g. `BURST_COOLDOWN` vs `POOL_SATURATED` vs `FLEET_SAFETY_OPEN`. Then docs say: for cooldown, backoff longer; for saturation, fallback model; etc.

Peter Holtz: If cooldown is too short, you can get oscillation: admit → pool p95 blows up → deny → admit. We need dampening.

Keiko Yamamoto: One idea: cooldown is per-tenant and only kicks in when the tenant exceeds cap *and* pool is above saturation threshold. If pool is healthy, they can keep using burst as allowed. If pool is unhealthy, cooldown becomes a real brake.

Jordan Lee: That’s implementable in policy engine as “conditional cooldown,” but we need to be careful about determinism under telemetry lag. If saturation signal flips due to lag, customers see flip-flop denials.

Ethan Park: We can use smoothed pool health (EWMA over 30-60s) and only change state on threshold crossings with hysteresis. That should reduce flapping.

Vanessa Ortiz: OK but we still need to propose numbers for GA defaults. Can we pick:
- standard: 1.5x max ratio
- conservative: 1.25x
- aggressive: 2.0x (only for Gold / explicit)
?

Jamal Pierce: That maps to procurement reality. If most tenants sit on standard (1.5x), we can size pools to a utilization target without assuming everyone doubles.

Harish Reddy: Works for ops too. We can say “aggressive requires capacity review” and treat it as an exception.

Sean Gallagher: Cooldown: I’d like 300s (5 min) for standard, 600s for aggressive (because they’ll push harder), 300s for conservative as well.

Ravi Menon: Why longer for aggressive? If they’re paying for aggressive, they’ll be the loudest when they hit cooldown.

Sean Gallagher: Not “punishment,” it’s stabilization. Aggressive means higher likelihood of impacting neighbors; longer cooldown reduces oscillation.

Peter Holtz: Another way: cooldown constant (300s) but aggressive has lower “pool health” threshold before we trigger cooldown. i.e. we clamp them earlier when the pool is stressed.

Keiko Yamamoto: I like that better. Keep cooldown uniform to reduce customer confusion, but change admission sensitivity by profile.

Jordan Lee: +1. Profiles could be:
- conservative: max_ratio=1.25, requires pool_health>=0.90
- standard: max_ratio=1.5, requires pool_health>=0.80
- aggressive: max_ratio=2.0, requires pool_health>=0.70 AND only if priority tier allows
Cooldown fixed 300s when denied due to cap.

Ethan Park: Careful: “pool_health” is abstract. In config we’ll likely expose thresholds in terms of signals: `pool_saturation_pct`, `queue_depth`, `tail_latency_delta`. But internally we can map to a single gate.

Vanessa Ortiz: Can we avoid surfacing pool health semantics to customers entirely? Customers only see “admitted/denied + reason.” Profiles are internal contract packaging.

Sean Gallagher: Yes, please. Customer-facing: max burst limit + expected denial behaviors. Internal: the triggers and gates.

Harish Reddy: On “no snowflakes”: can we state a hard rule that CS/Sales can’t promise custom cooldown/ratios? Only pick among profiles, and any custom needs approvals from Fleet + SRE.

Vanessa Ortiz: Yes. Let’s make it explicit: 3 profiles GA. Custom = “Professional Services / capacity review” and we don’t guarantee.

Mina Haddad: For docs, we should also explain that 1.5x is “up to,” not guaranteed. Admission depends on pool availability + fleet safety.

Ravi Menon: Also we should define what happens when burst is denied. Routing will fallback to baseline dedicated capacity; if customer continues above baseline they’ll see backpressure/429 depending on their queue config.

Keiko Yamamoto: Proposed default queue/backpressure for burst requests: queue up to 2s then 429. That avoids building up huge tail latency.

Peter Holtz: +1. A long queue is just hiding overload.

Jordan Lee: We should ensure denial reasons are consistent:
- `TENANT_BURST_CAP_REACHED`
- `TENANT_COOLDOWN_ACTIVE`
- `POOL_SATURATED`
- `FLEET_SAFETY_OPEN`
- `TELEMETRY_STALE` (admit conservatively)
That will help Support.

Ethan Park: Action item: I’ll update the policy engine defaults to reflect:
- Profiles: conservative(1.25), standard(1.5), aggressive(2.0)
- Cooldown: 300s uniform (triggered on cap reached)
- Conditional admission gates based on smoothed pool telemetry + hysteresis
and add “custom profile requires approvals” comment + audit event.

Jamal Pierce: I’ll re-run the sizing spreadsheet with 1.5x standard assumption and share impacts by region/hardware class.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll ping Allison/Sergio to ensure dashboards show cooldown denials separately from saturation denials. That’s an important debugging split.

Vanessa Ortiz: Great. Let’s treat this as tentative default unless beta data says otherwise. Next checkpoint after game day results.

Keiko Yamamoto: :thumbsup: I’ll update autoscaler doc + make sure we have sane conservative behavior when telemetry is stale (deny aggressive first, keep standard as long as baseline unaffected).

Peter Holtz: +1 on “deny aggressive first.” That’s the right neighbor-protection story.

Vanessa Ortiz: Cool. I’ll summarize in the Fleet guardrails section and link this thread in the sizing policy doc.

source.content:
Harish Reddy: need to lock staged rollout criteria for us-east / A100 pool before we request go/no-go. can folks confirm success metrics + stop conditions + sign-off list? aiming to kick off tomorrow AM.

Connor O'Brien: +1. release train window is Thu 10:00–14:00 ET. if we’re missing any approvals I need to know today.

Vanessa Ortiz: Let’s define it crisply. Scope is *only* us-east, A100 dedicated pool(s) behind the feature flag. No other regions/GPU types.

Keiko Yamamoto: For the record: feature flag is `fleet.provisioning.staged_rollout.enabled` and is constrained by `region=us-east` + `gpu=A100` in config.

Rafael Mendes: SRE angle: success = we can enable without waking someone up all night. would like explicit paging thresholds.

Aisha Bello: I’ll paste proposed success metrics + alert thresholds. Using dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/provision-to-ready/step-breakdown?var-region=us-east&var-gpu=A100

Aisha Bello: Success metrics (first 24h after enable):
- Time-to-ready P50 <= 35m (baseline ~58m)
- Time-to-ready P95 <= 75m
- Overall provision->ready success rate >= 97% (excluding planned quarantines)
- Validation flake rate (retry-success) <= 3%
- Quarantine rate <= 5% of nodes (and no single failure mode > 2%)
- No new P1/P2 incidents attributable to rollout

Miles Thornton: +1 on metrics. small nuance: success rate definition should be based on state machine reaching Ready within 2h of Allocated. Otherwise we can hide “stuck validating” forever.

Benji Okafor: Control plane can compute that: `allocated_at` to `ready_at`. The readiness service is emitting `time_to_ready_seconds` now. Agree on 2h max window for “success”.

Ethan Park: Yep, readiness service has:
- states: Allocated -> Provisioning -> Validating -> Ready OR Quarantined
- terminal timeout is enforced by orchestrator (currently 2h). We should codify “success within 2h” as Miles said.

Sahana Iyer: Image pipeline note: rollout is pinned to image channel `candidate-2025.02.19` for A100. we should call out rollback trigger if we see driver parity fail spike.

Vanessa Ortiz: Stop conditions (hard) should be:
- any increase in “stuck in Validating” > 10 nodes for > 20m
- driver parity failures > 3 nodes in 30m
- kubelet join failures > 5% of attempts in 1h
- quarantine > 10% in 2h
- any metric/tag cardinality issue (we had that last time…)

Aisha Bello: Re cardinality: we removed per-node tags in the metrics PR but can add a stop condition: if Mimir ingest rate spikes > 2x and top series includes node IDs -> disable flag immediately.

Rafael Mendes: paging / stop condition: if `time_to_ready_p95` > 120m for 2 consecutive 15m windows, page oncall and roll back.

Miles Thornton: +1, also page if success rate < 95% over 30m (during rollout only).

Connor O'Brien: Can we separate “stop conditions” vs “page but maybe continue”? For go/no-go we need clear rollback triggers.

Vanessa Ortiz: Agree. Let’s do:
Rollback triggers (immediate disable flag):
1) Any P1/P2 incident declared tied to provisioning
2) Quarantine rate > 10% over rolling 2h
3) Success rate < 95% over rolling 30m
4) P95 time-to-ready > 120m for 2 consecutive 15m windows
5) Validation agent crashlooping on > 5 nodes
6) Observability/cardinality regression impacting monitoring

Vanessa Ortiz: Non-rollback but investigate (ticket + watch):
- Flake/retry-success 3–8%
- Single validation step latency regression > 2x baseline

Keiko Yamamoto: For (5) we should specify: validation agent DS `node-validation-agent` in `kube-system`. CrashLoopBackOff count > 5 nodes for > 10m.

Ethan Park: Also add stop condition if readiness API write errors > 1% for 15m (that would break state transitions and lead to “ghost” nodes).

Benji Okafor: +1. Metric is `readiness_service_write_errors_total` / `readiness_service_writes_total`. Can add to the rollout checklist.

Sean Gallagher: jumping in. this looks good. one more: ensure oncall ownership is clear for first 24h. if we roll back, who flips the flag + who owns comms?

Connor O'Brien: Flag owner during window: Harish (primary) + Vanessa (backup). Comms: Connor to #eng-releases + #capacity-ops, and Rafael to #eng-oncall if paging.

Harish Reddy: works. I can be hands-on for first 6h. will also monitor quarantine counts and do safe drain/reimage per runbook if needed.

Rafael Mendes: SRE oncall will be aware but prefer we only page on the rollback triggers list. Anything else -> ticket.

Aisha Bello: I’ll tune alert routing: rollback triggers page `#eng-oncall` + incident-bot. Non-rollback -> JIRA ticket to Infra queue.

Sahana Iyer: Rollback plan should explicitly include image rollback too:
- disable feature flag
- pin pool back to `stable-2025.02.05`
- stop new allocations to affected pool until canary passes

Vanessa Ortiz: Good call. For this stage we’re not auto-promoting candidate->stable. manual pin only.

Connor O'Brien: Sign-off list — please confirm names/titles:
- Vanessa Ortiz (Infra/Fleet EM) – rollout approval
- Rafael Mendes (SRE mgr on behalf of Sean) – paging coverage + alert thresholds
- Ethan Park (Platform EM) – readiness service + state machine changes are ready
- Sahana Iyer (Release Eng) – image channel pinned + rollback verified
- Harish Reddy (Fleet ops) – oncall/ops readiness + runbook confirmed

Sean Gallagher: I’m ok with Rafael signing off for SRE as long as alert thresholds match what we just wrote. please add me as FYI on the go/no-go email.

Ethan Park: Platform sign-off yes, contingent on using the latest control-plane build (includes readiness gate + artifacts). If we’re not on that build, I’m not comfortable.

Harish Reddy: We’re deploying that build tonight. I’ll post the deploy bot link once complete.

Keiko Yamamoto: One more success metric: “nodes admitted to serving pool only after Ready”. We should ensure no nodes join serving while in Validating.

Benji Okafor: That’s enforced by the readiness gate in the admission controller. Metric `serving_pool_join_blocked_total` should be > 0 during rollout and `serving_pool_join_while_not_ready_total` must stay at 0.

Aisha Bello: Added to dashboard notes. Also adding a panel for that.

Vanessa Ortiz: Final ask: Connor, can you compile these into the rollout checklist doc + attach to go/no-go request?

Connor O'Brien: Yep. I’ll update the draft rollout plan and link here in ~30.

Harish Reddy: Thanks all. Once Connor drops the doc, I’ll send the go/no-go email with sign-offs requested. :thumbsup:

Connor O'Brien: Updated checklist + criteria here: https://drive.google.com/redwood/provision-to-ready-rollout-us-east-a100 (section “Stage 1 Go/No-Go”). Please review.

Rafael Mendes: reviewed. looks aligned. SRE sign-off pending final alert routes (Aisha).

Aisha Bello: Alert routes updated + tested in staging. SRE sign-off good to go.

Vanessa Ortiz: Fleet sign-off: approved, with rollback triggers as listed. Please ensure we have a human watching the dashboards for first 2h.

Harish Reddy: ack. I’ll be on it and will post status updates hourly in #eng-infra during the window.

source.text:
Irene Choi: Trying to close out the provisioning checklist deltas for Premium tier today. Need crisp differences vs Plus/Standard: warm pool, spare nodes, HA. Can folks sanity check?

Irene Choi: Draft deltas (Premium only):
- Warm pool (pre-warmed) capacity maintained in-region
- N+1 spare node(s) in the Dedicated pool
- HA config (multi-AZ) + failover runbook validated
- Additional perf validation (burn-in)

Vanessa Ortiz: +1 on having it explicit. For warm pool: can we define as “1 node equivalent per region per Premium customer” or is it per shape class?

Jamal Pierce: per customer warm pool is gonna be rough in us-east if they pick H100. Supply is tight. I’d rather define as “warm pool availability subject to shape availability” but that gets contract-y.

Harish Reddy: Operationally we can do warm pool as “one spare *slot*” that can be any compatible node, not necessarily identical GPU SKU. But perf/SLO implications.

Nina Petrova: If it’s not the same SKU, failover can violate their throughput floor. For Premium we should keep warm pool same shape family (e.g., H100 8x stays H100 8x).

Rafael Mendes: From SRE side: HA story needs to be clear. Premium implies we *actually* test failover (not just have Terraform). Checklist item should include “simulate node loss + verify throughput stays above floor within X mins”.

Keiko Yamamoto: Re: spare nodes, are we saying N+1 per deployment (so if customer has 2 nodes we maintain 1 spare) or 1 spare minimum regardless? Autoscaler can take advantage but cost is real.

Vanessa Ortiz: For Premium deployments we’ve been assuming minimum 1 spare per deployment, and if >4 nodes then 20% spare (rounded up). But that’s not written anywhere.

Irene Choi: Ok. Let’s translate into checklist language, not policy language.

Irene Choi: Proposed Premium checklist items:
1) Warm pool:
   - Confirm warm pool reservation exists in same region + same shape family
   - Node(s) are pre-imaged + driver/runtime pinned to current Dedicated baseline
   - Run “warm validation” (model load + first token latency smoke)

2) Spare capacity:
   - Ensure at least 1 spare node capacity available for deployment (same shape family)
   - Confirm spare is in “ready” state (joined cluster, health checks passing, not serving traffic)

3) HA:
   - Deploy spans >=2 AZs (or failure domains if provider doesn’t map to AZ)
   - Validate quorum/etcd/control plane redundancy
   - Execute failover drill: drain one node + kill one node, confirm routing + throughput floor recovery within 10 min

4) Burn-in:
   - Run 30 min burn-in at target concurrency using Redwood perf harness
   - Capture tokens/sec + p95 latency + GPU util; attach to handoff ticket

Thoughts?

Nina Petrova: Looks good. On HA: add explicit “networking” check: cross-AZ bandwidth + security group rules for NCCL. We’ve had surprises where multi-AZ is configured but comms are throttled.

Nina Petrova: Suggest add:
- Validate interconnect: `ibstat`/`nvidia-smi topo -m` + bandwidth smoke
- Verify ingress LB health checks per AZ

Harish Reddy: +1. Also add “spare node is on a separate rack/failure domain if available”. Doesn’t always apply but we can do best-effort.

Keiko Yamamoto: Warm pool “pre-imaged + model load smoke” is key. I’d add a requirement: warm pool nodes keep KV cache disabled / cleared. We don’t want weird cache state from previous canary loads.

Rafael Mendes: On failover drill, I’d like it to include “customer-facing endpoint still returns 2xx” plus “no auth/rate-limit regressions” (we’ve seen miswired policies on secondary).

Connor O'Brien: Release angle: please add “feature flags for tier-aware routing enabled” + “fallback policy set to Premium defaults” before handoff. Otherwise they get provisioned as Premium but routed like Plus.

Vanessa Ortiz: For the spare node, make sure the checklist clarifies it’s capacity in the *Dedicated pool* not just ‘available in region’. Sales keeps asking for “spare guaranteed” and we need to define it as in-pool.

Irene Choi: Ack. Updating checklist with these additions:
- HA networking/interconnect validation
- Failover drill includes 2xx + policy validation
- Tier routing flags + fallback policy verification
- Spare node explicitly “in-pool”

Jamal Pierce: One more note: if Premium is multi-AZ, provisioning lead time is longer (IP blocks, subnets, quota). Can we add “confirm cloud quota (EIPs/NICs/IB) before promising date” as a Premium-only precheck?

Vanessa Ortiz: yes please. We should add a preflight section: quota + capacity + AZ availability.

Irene Choi: Good call. Adding Premium preflight:
- Confirm shape capacity in >=2 AZs
- Confirm quota: GPU, IB/NVSwitch (if relevant), EIP/NIC, local SSD
- Confirm warm pool + spare node reservation can be met

Rafael Mendes: Also include: “page SRE for failover drill scheduling” or at least “notify oncall window”. We don’t want random chaos testing during peak.

Irene Choi: I’ll add “schedule failover drill in agreed window; notify #eng-oncall + attach run command output”.

Nina Petrova: Can we standardize the commands we want attached?
- `kubectl get nodes -o wide`
- `nvidia-smi -q -x | head`
- perf harness summary
- routing config snapshot

Irene Choi: Yep. I’ll include a snippet in the checklist.

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: once you paste final, I’ll drop into the runbook doc.

Connor O'Brien: +1. Also please tag me in the runbook PR so release checklist stays consistent.

Irene Choi: Final draft deltas for Premium provisioning checklist (vs Plus/Standard):
PRE-FLIGHT (Premium)
- Confirm target region has shape capacity in >=2 AZs/failure domains
- Confirm cloud quotas: GPU, networking (NIC/EIP), storage, IB/NVSwitch where applicable
- Confirm warm pool reservation available (same region + same shape family)
- Confirm spare node capacity available *in Dedicated pool* (same shape family)

PROVISIONING (Premium)
- Multi-AZ deployment (or equivalent failure domains) + control plane redundancy validated
- Networking/interconnect validated (NCCL path not degraded; cross-AZ bandwidth smoke)
- Tier-aware routing enabled + Premium fallback policy configured

VALIDATION (Premium)
- Warm pool nodes pre-imaged, pinned runtime/driver baseline, model load smoke completed
- Spare node is “ready” (cluster-joined, health checks passing, not serving)
- Burn-in: 30 min at target concurrency; capture tokens/sec + p95 latency + GPU util
- Failover drill scheduled/ran: drain + kill node; confirm endpoint 2xx + policy intact; throughput floor recovers within 10 min

ARTIFACTS TO ATTACH
- `kubectl get nodes -o wide`
- `nvidia-smi topo -m` + `nvidia-smi`
- perf harness summary output
- routing/tier config snapshot

If no objections in next ~1h I’ll consider it locked.

Vanessa Ortiz: looks solid. no objections.

Nina Petrova: good.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1.

Rafael Mendes: works. thanks.

Harish Reddy: ship it.

Connor O'Brien: :shipit: tag me on the runbook update.

title: #eng-infra: 1739738455
preview:
Irene Choi: Trying to close out the provisioning checklist deltas for Premium tier today. Need crisp differences vs Plus/Standard: warm pool, spare nodes, HA. Can folks sanity check?

Irene Choi: Draft deltas (Premium only):
- Warm pool (pre-warmed) capacity maintained in-region
- N+1 spare node(s) in the Dedicated pool
- HA config (multi-AZ) + failover runbook validated
- Additional perf validation (burn-in)

Vanessa Ortiz: +1 on having it explicit. For warm pool: can we define as “1 node equivalent per region per Premium customer” or is it per shape class?

Jamal Pierce: per customer warm pool is gonna be rough in us-east if they pick H100. Supply is tight. I’d rather define as “warm pool availability subject to shape availability” but that gets contract-y.

Harish Reddy: Operationally we can do warm pool as “one spare *slot*” that can be any compatible node, not necessarily identical GPU SKU. But perf/SLO implications.

Nina Petrova: If it’s not the same SKU, failover can violate their throughput floor. For Premium we should keep warm pool same shape family (e.g., H100 8x stays H100 8x).

Rafael Mendes: From SRE side: HA story needs to be clear. Premium implies we *actually* test failover (not just have Terraform). Checklist item should include “simulate node loss + verify throughput stays above floor within X mins”.

Keiko Yamamoto: Re: spare nodes, are we saying N+1 per deployment (so if customer has 2 nodes we maintain 1 spare) or 1 spare minimum regardless? Autoscaler can take advantage but cost is real.

Vanessa Ortiz: For Premium deployments we’ve been assuming minimum 1 spare per deployment, and if >4 nodes then 20% spare (rounded up). But that’s not written anywhere.

Irene Choi: Ok. Let’s translate into checklist language, not policy language.

Irene Choi: Proposed Premium checklist items:
1) Warm pool:
   - Confirm warm pool reservation exists in same region + same shape family
   - Node(s) are pre-imaged + driver/runtime pinned to current Dedicated baseline
   - Run “warm validation” (model load + first token latency smoke)

2) Spare capacity:
   - Ensure at least 1 spare node capacity available for deployment (same shape family)
   - Confirm spare is in “ready” state (joined cluster, health checks passing, not serving traffic)

3) HA:
   - Deploy spans >=2 AZs (or failure domains if provider doesn’t map to AZ)
   - Validate quorum/etcd/control plane redundancy
   - Execute failover drill: drain one node + kill one node, confirm routing + throughput floor recovery within 10 min

4) Burn-in:
   - Run 30 min burn-in at target concurrency using Redwood perf harness
   - Capture tokens/sec + p95 latency + GPU util; attach to handoff ticket

Thoughts?

Nina Petrova: Looks good. On HA: add explicit “networking” check: cross-AZ bandwidth + security group rules for NCCL. We’ve had surprises where multi-AZ is configured but comms are throttled.

Nina Petrova: Suggest add:
- Validate interconnect: `ibstat`/`nvidia-smi topo -m` + bandwidth smoke
- Verify ingress LB health checks per AZ

Harish Reddy: +1. Also add “spare node is on a separate rack/failure domain if available”. Doesn’t always apply but we can do best-effort.

Keiko Yamamoto: Warm pool “pre-imaged + model load smoke” is key. I’d add a requirement: warm pool nodes keep KV cache disabled / cleared. We don’t want weird cache state from previous canary loads.

Rafael Mendes: On failover drill, I’d like it to include “customer-facing endpoint still returns 2xx” plus “no auth/rate-limit regressions” (we’ve seen miswired policies on secondary).

Connor O'Brien: Release angle: please add “feature flags for tier-aware routing enabled” + “fallback policy set to Premium defaults” before handoff. Otherwise they get provisioned as Premium but routed like Plus.

Vanessa Ortiz: For the spare node, make sure the checklist clarifies it’s capacity in the *Dedicated pool* not just ‘available in region’. Sales keeps asking for “spare guaranteed” and we need to define it as in-pool.

Irene Choi: Ack. Updating checklist with these additions:
- HA networking/interconnect validation
- Failover drill includes 2xx + policy validation
- Tier routing flags + fallback policy verification
- Spare node explicitly “in-pool”

Jamal Pierce: One more note: if Premium is multi-AZ, provisioning lead time is longer (IP blocks, subnets, quota). Can we add “confirm cloud quota (EIPs/NICs/IB) before promising date” as a Premium-only precheck?

Vanessa Ortiz: yes please. We should add a preflight section: quota + capacity + AZ availability.

Irene Choi: Good call. Adding Premium preflight:
- Confirm shape capacity in >=2 AZs
- Confirm quota: GPU, IB/NVSwitch (if relevant), EIP/NIC, local SSD
- Confirm warm pool + spare node reservation can be met

Rafael Mendes: Also include: “page SRE for failover drill scheduling” or at least “notify oncall window”. We don’t want random chaos testing during peak.

Irene Choi: I’ll add “schedule failover drill in agreed window; notify #eng-oncall + attach run command output”.

Nina Petrova: Can we standardize the commands we want attached?
- `kubectl get nodes -o wide`
- `nvidia-smi -q -x | head`
- perf harness summary
- routing config snapshot

Irene Choi: Yep. I’ll include a snippet in the checklist.

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: once you paste final, I’ll drop into the runbook doc.

Connor O'Brien: +1. Also please tag me in the runbook PR so release checklist stays consistent.

Irene Choi: Final draft deltas for Premium provisioning checklist (vs Plus/Standard):
PRE-FLIGHT (Premium)
- Confirm target region has shape capacity in >=2 AZs/failure domains
- Confirm cloud quotas: GPU, networking (NIC/EIP), storage, IB/NVSwitch where applicable
- Confirm warm pool reservation available (same region + same shape family)
- Confirm spare node capacity available *in Dedicated pool* (same shape family)

PROVISIONING (Premium)
- Multi-AZ deployment (or equivalent failure domains) + control plane redundancy validated
- Networking/interconnect validated (NCCL path not degraded; cross-AZ bandwidth smoke)
- Tier-aware routing enabled + Premium fallback policy configured

VALIDATION (Premium)
- Warm pool nodes pre-imaged, pinned runtime/driver baseline, model load smoke completed
- Spare node is “ready” (cluster-joined, health checks passing, not serving)
- Burn-in: 30 min at target concurrency; capture tokens/sec + p95 latency + GPU util
- Failover drill scheduled/ran: drain + kill node; confirm endpoint 2xx + policy intact; throughput floor recovers within 10 min

ARTIFACTS TO ATTACH
- `kubectl get nodes -o wide`
- `nvidia-smi topo -m` + `nvidia-smi`
- perf harness summary output
- routing/tier config snapshot

If no objections in next ~1h I’ll consider it locked.

Vanessa Ortiz: looks solid. no objections.

Nina Petrova: good.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1.

Rafael Mendes: works. thanks.

Harish Reddy: ship it.

Connor O'Brien: :shipit: tag me on the runbook update.

source.content:
Gabe Wilson: rolling forward on the node validation agent DaemonSet rollout today (helm pr-244). need to lock RBAC + helm values before we can flip it on for us-east/a100. can someone sanity check what perms we *actually* need?

Vanessa Ortiz: thx. main thing: we can’t ship anything that effectively becomes cluster-admin. agent should:
- detect node + gpu health locally
- emit results to readiness svc (http)
- optionally create a k8s Event on the node/pod
No reason it should be patching Nodes unless we’re doing that for join gating (i’d prefer control-plane only).

Sahana Iyer: +1. current chart has `rbac.create=true` but i left rules wide to unblock. we should trim.

Gabe Wilson: what i have right now (too broad):
```
# ClusterRole (current, probably too much)
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["nodes", "pods", "pods/log", "events", "configmaps"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "patch", "update"]
- apiGroups: ["coordination.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["leases"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch"]
```
agent uses a lease for leader-ish behavior to avoid spamming readiness svc when kubelet flaps. but we can drop if needed.

Keiko Yamamoto: do we even need leader election? it’s a DS, each node should report its own state. i’d rather avoid leases entirely (and coordination api access) unless we have a clear use.

Gabe Wilson: fair. lease was for “one node runs cluster-level checks” (e.g., api reachability), but we can punt cluster-level checks to orchestrator.

Vanessa Ortiz: yep punt. DS should be node-scoped. if it’s only reporting its own node, it only needs:
- read its own pod (optional)
- create Events (optional)
- read Node object for labels/taints (nodeName mapping)
No list/watch across all nodes.

Jordan Lee: small wrinkle: kube API doesn’t have “get node by my nodeName without list”. but we can `get` a single node by name if we have it. from DS we have `spec.nodeName` via downward API.

Sahana Iyer: exactly. we can inject `NODE_NAME` env:
`valueFrom: fieldRef: fieldPath: spec.nodeName`
then client does `GET /api/v1/nodes/$NODE_NAME`.

Fiona McCarthy: security note: avoid `list/watch nodes` if we can. `get nodes` is still cluster-scoped, but narrower. definitely no secrets, no configmaps, no pods/log unless required.

Gabe Wilson: do we need pods/log? only for bundling our own container logs on failure. but we can just ship logs via stdout + fluentbit. readiness svc artifact is a pointer anyway.

Vanessa Ortiz: drop pods/log.

Ethan Park: from control-plane side: agent should not patch node labels/annotations. readiness service is source of truth, and control-plane will gate “join serving pool” based on readiness state. events are okay (helpful for on-call), but not strictly required.

Connor O’Brien: rollout-wise: would love events. when nodes are stuck in Validating, having a node event that says “nccl timeout” is huge for first responder.

Gabe Wilson: ok, so minimal RBAC:
- get nodes (by name)
- create events (in its namespace?)
- get pods (its own) maybe optional
Anything else?

Fiona McCarthy: if creating Events, it’s `events` in core API, namespace-scoped. clusterrole not needed for that part. but nodes are cluster-scoped so you’ll still need ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding for nodes get.

Sahana Iyer: we can do:
- ClusterRole: nodes get
- Role (namespace): events create,patch (k8s sometimes does patch on events?)
But helm chart currently only supports one RBAC bundle; i can split into ClusterRole + Role.

Jordan Lee: k8s events API: clients usually `create` only. but some libs do “create or patch” on retries. safer to allow `create` and `patch` on events.

Vanessa Ortiz: allow patch on events is fine.

Gabe Wilson: any PSA/pod security stuff? agent needs host access to run `nvidia-smi` + read `/dev/nvidia*` and run the nccl smoke test container.

Sahana Iyer: chart sets:
- `securityContext.privileged: true`
- `hostNetwork: true` (for IB checks)
- mounts `/dev`, `/var/lib/kubelet`, `/run/containerd/containerd.sock` (for runtime sanity)
This will not pass restricted policy. we’re deploying in `fleet-validation` namespace with `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=privileged` label (already used for node-problem-detector).

Fiona McCarthy: ok if namespace is explicitly labeled + audited. make sure SA token is not mounted unless needed.

Gabe Wilson: we *do* need api access for node GET + event create, so token has to be mounted. but we can set `automountServiceAccountToken: true` only on the pod, not namespace-wide.

Vanessa Ortiz: and please pin SA name, no default SA.

Sahana Iyer: ack.

Gabe Wilson: here’s the proposed helm values snippet for us-east canary:
```
validationAgent:
  enabled: true
  image:
    repository: 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/fleet/node-validation-agent
    tag: "0.6.2"
  rbac:
    create: true
  serviceAccount:
    create: true
    name: node-validation-agent
  podLabels:
    app: node-validation-agent
  podAnnotations:
    prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
    prometheus.io/port: "9090"
  automountServiceAccountToken: true
  hostNetwork: true
  dnsPolicy: ClusterFirstWithHostNet
  tolerations:
    - operator: Exists
  nodeSelector:
    node.kubernetes.io/instance-type: p4d.24xlarge
  resources:
    requests:
      cpu: "200m"
      memory: "256Mi"
    limits:
      cpu: "1"
      memory: "1Gi"
  env:
    - name: NODE_NAME
      valueFrom:
        fieldRef:
          fieldPath: spec.nodeName
    - name: READINESS_API_URL
      value: "https://fleet-readiness.service.consul"
    - name: IMAGE_CHANNEL
      value: "candidate"
  securityContext:
    privileged: true
    readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
  volumeMounts:
    - name: dev
      mountPath: /dev
    - name: containerd-sock
      mountPath: /run/containerd/containerd.sock
  volumes:
    - name: dev
      hostPath:
        path: /dev
    - name: containerd-sock
      hostPath:
        path: /run/containerd/containerd.sock
```

Keiko Yamamoto: nodeSelector should probably be a label we control (gpu sku), not instance-type (cloud-specific). but ok for first canary.

Connor O’Brien: +1, for rollout docs we’ll refer to “us-east A100 pool” not instance type.

Sahana Iyer: i can switch to:
`nodeSelector: { redwood.ai/gpu-sku: "a100" }`
assuming that label is present.

Vanessa Ortiz: it is. use `redwood.ai/pool-type: dedicated` too so it doesn’t land on shared.

Gabe Wilson: good call.

Fiona McCarthy: also `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` + privileged can be weird depending on agent writing temp files. ensure it writes to an `emptyDir` mounted at /tmp.

Gabe Wilson: yep, agent currently writes a small bundle to /var/tmp. i’ll change to /tmp and mount emptyDir.

Sahana Iyer: RBAC proposal (trimmed) — ok?
```
# ClusterRole: node get only
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["nodes"]
  verbs: ["get"]

# Role (namespace fleet-validation): events write
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["events"]
  verbs: ["create", "patch"]

# Role (namespace): pods get (optional)
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get"]
```

Jordan Lee: pods get is fine. mostly for looking up pod UID for event correlation, but can also be done via downward API. if we can avoid pods get, even better.

Gabe Wilson: we can do downward API for pod name/namespace, but UID is annoying. not critical. i’m fine dropping pods get.

Vanessa Ortiz: drop it.

Ethan Park: +1 drop pods get. keep it super tight.

Fiona McCarthy: agreed. also please ensure no `watch` permissions anywhere.

Sahana Iyer: cool. i’ll update pr-244 with:
- remove leases + related RBAC
- ClusterRole nodes get
- Role events create/patch
- emptyDir /tmp
- nodeSelector labels per Vanessa

deploy-bot: PR #244 updated: “add node-validation-agent DaemonSet (RBAC tightened, remove leases)” — waiting on reviews (Sahana Iyer, Fiona McCarthy)

Connor O’Brien: once merged, can we gate rollout behind feature flag values? i want helm default `validationAgent.enabled=false` and only enable via region-specific values file.

Sahana Iyer: already default false. canary values file lives under `environments/us-east/dedicated-a100/values.yaml`.

Vanessa Ortiz: timeline: if this merges by EOD, we can start canary tomorrow morning with Harish on watch.

Gabe Wilson: :thumbsup: i’ll run a quick `kubectl auth can-i` check in staging once chart is updated:
- `get nodes` as SA
- `create events` as SA
and confirm it can’t list nodes.

Fiona McCarthy: pls paste output in the PR. also add a note in chart README: “privileged namespace required” + rationale.

Gabe Wilson: will do.

Vanessa Ortiz: thanks all. call out if anything blocks. (reactions: :thumbsup: x3)

source.text:
Sahana Iyer: seeing a bunch of CI flakes on the `private-upgrade-e2e` suite again. last 10 nightly runs: 4 failures, and at least 3 look non-deterministic (rerun passed). this is the ENG-4877 track but it’s now blocking a couple PRs due to required checks.

Sahana Iyer: failure modes from the last two:
- k8s cluster never reaches ready (kind) within 8m, then everything cascades
- snapshot hook step hangs (CSI) and times out
- intermittent `helm upgrade` failing w/ `context deadline exceeded` when waiting on pre-upgrade job

Sahana Iyer: question: do we add retries (rerun jobs / rerun steps) or should we invest in more deterministic fixtures first? trying to pick the right “stop the bleeding” move.

github-actions[bot]: Workflow run failed: private-upgrade-e2e / scenario=onprem-offline-bundle
Logs: https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-private-installer/actions/runs/93218810

Devon Price: +1 we should stop the bleeding, but blanket retries make the signal worse.

Devon Price: can we do targeted retries based on error signatures? ex: retry only if we see `failed to pull image` / `TLS handshake timeout` / `apiserver not ready`.

Devon Price: we can also add `--atomic=false` to avoid helm auto-rollback masking what actually happened (then we can still clean up in teardown). also please attach `kubectl describe pod` + events as artifacts.

Leila Farouk: my vote: deterministic fixtures first, but agree we need a short-term mitigation.

Leila Farouk: a lot of our “e2e” is accidentally testing the internet + docker hub + runner variance:
- images aren’t pinned to digests everywhere
- we don’t pre-pull images, so a slow pull trips timeouts
- DB seed/migration duration varies depending on node load

Leila Farouk: if we can:
1) pin image digests for the test environment
2) pre-pull into the cluster (or local registry mirror)
3) set fixed resource requests/limits in the test charts
…we’ll cut 80% of the flakes.

Irene Choi: infra context: shared GH runners are a big part of this. we see CPU steal + network egress throttling during US morning hours.

Irene Choi: the `kind` “never ready” is usually just the node container starting slowly. we can add a readiness probe that fails fast with a clear error *and* bump the timeout (8m is tight when the runner is slow).

Irene Choi: medium term: I can help get a small dedicated runner pool for these upgrade e2e jobs (or move the nightly to our internal runners). but that won’t land this week.

Rafael Mendes: +1 on not turning retries into a broom.

Rafael Mendes: for release gating, we need to be able to say “this passed once” actually means something. otherwise we’ll start shipping regressions and blaming CI.

Rafael Mendes: suggestion:
- classify flakes into known transient buckets
- only auto-retry those buckets (max 1 retry)
- anything else is a hard fail

Rafael Mendes: also, snapshot hook timeouts might be real: the CSI backend in tests is sometimes not actually supporting what our hook expects. are we running hostpath in kind for the storage path that should be “CSI”? if yes, that’s basically random.

Sahana Iyer: re snapshot: current fixture uses hostpath + a fake snapshot controller in kind. it’s… fragile. we did it to avoid needing real cloud storage in CI.

Leila Farouk: that explains a lot. fake snapshot controllers are notorious.

Leila Farouk: could we split the matrix?
- PR gating: logical backup path only (no snapshot), plus rollback state machine checks
- nightly: include snapshot path tests (and accept higher runtime)

Connor O'Brien: jumping in — for Private release gating we should not rely on a flaky scenario.

Connor O'Brien: +1 to splitting. PR gating should cover N-1 → N happy path with deterministic infra. snapshot coverage can be nightly + pre-release.

Connor O'Brien: for the short-term, I’m okay with 1 retry at the *job* level for known infra flakes, but we need to log when it happened and keep a metric (“flake rate”) so it doesn’t become permanent.

Devon Price: we can implement “known infra flake” detection pretty quickly:
- parse the junit + job logs for strings
- if match, rerun job once and mark the PR check as `passed_with_retry` (or at least annotate in summary)

Devon Price: GH Actions doesn’t have a first-class “passed with retry”, but we can print it in the check summary + emit a metric.

Irene Choi: on timeouts: the helm wait failure (`context deadline exceeded`) might just be a bad timeout default in the pre-upgrade hook job. we recently added more prechecks + artifact verification, which increases that job’s runtime.

Irene Choi: can we bump the hook timeout in the test values file, and separately keep prod defaults conservative?

Sahana Iyer: makes sense. i can bump in fixture values for e2e. will keep prod values unchanged.

Rafael Mendes: also add better debug on failure:
- `kubectl get jobs -A`
- `kubectl logs job/<preupgrade> -n <ns> --tail=-1`
- `kubectl get events -A --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | tail -200`

Rafael Mendes: half the “flake” is just “we don’t know why it failed.”

Leila Farouk: +1. and for the determinism work, I can take the “pin digests + pre-pull” action item.

Leila Farouk: we should also freeze the test bundle inputs. i saw a couple runs pull latest from the artifact mirror, which is… not ideal for a reproducible upgrade test.

Sahana Iyer: action plan proposal:
1) immediate: add 1 auto-retry ONLY for known infra flakes (apiserver/kind ready, image pull/TLS timeouts). Devon, can you help with the log signature list?
2) split matrix: PR gating uses logical backup path only; snapshot path nightly/pre-release. Connor ok?
3) improve fixtures: pin image digests + pre-pull; freeze artifact bundle versions.
4) increase e2e-only timeout for the helm pre-upgrade job + add better artifact collection on failure.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: for #2. PR gating should be stable + representative. snapshot path nightly is fine.

Devon Price: yep, i’ll draft the signature list and wire the rerun (max 1). will drop it behind a flag so we can disable quickly.

Irene Choi: i’ll look into runner pool options + whether we can run nightly on internal runners (and what it costs). but that’s separate from the immediate fixes.

Leila Farouk: taking #3. will open a ticket + PR for digest pinning + pre-pull init job in the test chart.

Sahana Iyer: awesome. i’ll do #4 today and update ENG-4877 + INT-3302 with these decisions. thanks all.

title: #eng-infra: 1739511102
preview:
Sahana Iyer: seeing a bunch of CI flakes on the `private-upgrade-e2e` suite again. last 10 nightly runs: 4 failures, and at least 3 look non-deterministic (rerun passed). this is the ENG-4877 track but it’s now blocking a couple PRs due to required checks.

Sahana Iyer: failure modes from the last two:
- k8s cluster never reaches ready (kind) within 8m, then everything cascades
- snapshot hook step hangs (CSI) and times out
- intermittent `helm upgrade` failing w/ `context deadline exceeded` when waiting on pre-upgrade job

Sahana Iyer: question: do we add retries (rerun jobs / rerun steps) or should we invest in more deterministic fixtures first? trying to pick the right “stop the bleeding” move.

github-actions[bot]: Workflow run failed: private-upgrade-e2e / scenario=onprem-offline-bundle
Logs: https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-private-installer/actions/runs/93218810

Devon Price: +1 we should stop the bleeding, but blanket retries make the signal worse.

Devon Price: can we do targeted retries based on error signatures? ex: retry only if we see `failed to pull image` / `TLS handshake timeout` / `apiserver not ready`.

Devon Price: we can also add `--atomic=false` to avoid helm auto-rollback masking what actually happened (then we can still clean up in teardown). also please attach `kubectl describe pod` + events as artifacts.

Leila Farouk: my vote: deterministic fixtures first, but agree we need a short-term mitigation.

Leila Farouk: a lot of our “e2e” is accidentally testing the internet + docker hub + runner variance:
- images aren’t pinned to digests everywhere
- we don’t pre-pull images, so a slow pull trips timeouts
- DB seed/migration duration varies depending on node load

Leila Farouk: if we can:
1) pin image digests for the test environment
2) pre-pull into the cluster (or local registry mirror)
3) set fixed resource requests/limits in the test charts
…we’ll cut 80% of the flakes.

Irene Choi: infra context: shared GH runners are a big part of this. we see CPU steal + network egress throttling during US morning hours.

Irene Choi: the `kind` “never ready” is usually just the node container starting slowly. we can add a readiness probe that fails fast with a clear error *and* bump the timeout (8m is tight when the runner is slow).

Irene Choi: medium term: I can help get a small dedicated runner pool for these upgrade e2e jobs (or move the nightly to our internal runners). but that won’t land this week.

Rafael Mendes: +1 on not turning retries into a broom.

Rafael Mendes: for release gating, we need to be able to say “this passed once” actually means something. otherwise we’ll start shipping regressions and blaming CI.

Rafael Mendes: suggestion:
- classify flakes into known transient buckets
- only auto-retry those buckets (max 1 retry)
- anything else is a hard fail

Rafael Mendes: also, snapshot hook timeouts might be real: the CSI backend in tests is sometimes not actually supporting what our hook expects. are we running hostpath in kind for the storage path that should be “CSI”? if yes, that’s basically random.

Sahana Iyer: re snapshot: current fixture uses hostpath + a fake snapshot controller in kind. it’s… fragile. we did it to avoid needing real cloud storage in CI.

Leila Farouk: that explains a lot. fake snapshot controllers are notorious.

Leila Farouk: could we split the matrix?
- PR gating: logical backup path only (no snapshot), plus rollback state machine checks
- nightly: include snapshot path tests (and accept higher runtime)

Connor O'Brien: jumping in — for Private release gating we should not rely on a flaky scenario.

Connor O'Brien: +1 to splitting. PR gating should cover N-1 → N happy path with deterministic infra. snapshot coverage can be nightly + pre-release.

Connor O'Brien: for the short-term, I’m okay with 1 retry at the *job* level for known infra flakes, but we need to log when it happened and keep a metric (“flake rate”) so it doesn’t become permanent.

Devon Price: we can implement “known infra flake” detection pretty quickly:
- parse the junit + job logs for strings
- if match, rerun job once and mark the PR check as `passed_with_retry` (or at least annotate in summary)

Devon Price: GH Actions doesn’t have a first-class “passed with retry”, but we can print it in the check summary + emit a metric.

Irene Choi: on timeouts: the helm wait failure (`context deadline exceeded`) might just be a bad timeout default in the pre-upgrade hook job. we recently added more prechecks + artifact verification, which increases that job’s runtime.

Irene Choi: can we bump the hook timeout in the test values file, and separately keep prod defaults conservative?

Sahana Iyer: makes sense. i can bump in fixture values for e2e. will keep prod values unchanged.

Rafael Mendes: also add better debug on failure:
- `kubectl get jobs -A`
- `kubectl logs job/<preupgrade> -n <ns> --tail=-1`
- `kubectl get events -A --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | tail -200`

Rafael Mendes: half the “flake” is just “we don’t know why it failed.”

Leila Farouk: +1. and for the determinism work, I can take the “pin digests + pre-pull” action item.

Leila Farouk: we should also freeze the test bundle inputs. i saw a couple runs pull latest from the artifact mirror, which is… not ideal for a reproducible upgrade test.

Sahana Iyer: action plan proposal:
1) immediate: add 1 auto-retry ONLY for known infra flakes (apiserver/kind ready, image pull/TLS timeouts). Devon, can you help with the log signature list?
2) split matrix: PR gating uses logical backup path only; snapshot path nightly/pre-release. Connor ok?
3) improve fixtures: pin image digests + pre-pull; freeze artifact bundle versions.
4) increase e2e-only timeout for the helm pre-upgrade job + add better artifact collection on failure.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: for #2. PR gating should be stable + representative. snapshot path nightly is fine.

Devon Price: yep, i’ll draft the signature list and wire the rerun (max 1). will drop it behind a flag so we can disable quickly.

Irene Choi: i’ll look into runner pool options + whether we can run nightly on internal runners (and what it costs). but that’s separate from the immediate fixes.

Leila Farouk: taking #3. will open a ticket + PR for digest pinning + pre-pull init job in the test chart.

Sahana Iyer: awesome. i’ll do #4 today and update ENG-4877 + INT-3302 with these decisions. thanks all.

source.text:
Gabe Wilson: seeing NCCL post-provision smoke test flaking again in us-east A100. validation agent marks node as failed -> orchestrator retries -> sometimes passes on 2nd attempt. anyone else looking at this?

Gabe Wilson: log excerpt from one of the failures (from /var/log/redwood/validation/nccl-smoke.log):
```bash
+ timeout 90s bash -lc 'NCCL_DEBUG=INFO NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME=eth0 ./all_reduce_perf -b 8M -e 256M -f 2 -g 8'
NCCL version 2.18.3+cuda12.1
...
WARN NET/Socket : Connection closed by remote peer
INFO transport/net_socket.cc:67 -> 2
WARN init.cc:868 -> 2
ncclInternalError: Internal check failed.
Last error:
Error: failed to connect to 10.42.18.93<32415>
```

Vanessa Ortiz: 90s seems tight if the node is still warming up / ib devices settling. what’s the pass latency when it succeeds?

Gabe Wilson: on success, the test itself is like ~18-30s. but we have a few that take 70-85s end-to-end (incl pulling image + init). failures tend to be right around the timeout.

Nina Petrova: “connection closed by remote peer” smells like fabric not ready or transient kube networking / CNI not settled. are we running this immediately after kubelet join?

Gabe Wilson: yep. flow is: JoinCluster -> LabelNode -> Deploy validation DaemonSet -> run nccl check.

Keiko Yamamoto: if it’s right after join, node can still be in the window where routes / iptables rules are being applied. can we add backoff between join and nccl smoke?

Harish Reddy: +1. ops perspective: we’d rather add 2 mins to provisioning than quarantine a good node and then reimage.

Sahana Iyer: we also saw occasional image pull slowness on first boot (containerd doing snapshotter work). that can eat into the timeout.

Miles Thornton: what’s the observed flake rate? if it’s a couple % we should treat as retryable with structured telemetry, not quarantine-worthy on first attempt.

Gabe Wilson: from last 24h in the staged rollout pool: 14 failures / 312 nodes attempted (~4.5%). of those, 9 passed on immediate retry, 3 passed after re-running validations manually, 2 quarantined.

Aisha Bello: if we’re going to retry, please ensure metrics don’t explode cardinality-wise. tag by step + failure class, not node id. (we already got burned once :))

Rafael Mendes: SRE side: we can tolerate a retry as long as we cap total time-to-ready. agree quarantine should be after repeated failures + evidence it’s not just timing.

Vanessa Ortiz: propose:
1) increase nccl smoke timeout to 240s
2) add explicit pre-check that the validation pod has been running for N seconds and has pulled image already
3) retry policy: 3 attempts, exponential backoff (30s, 60s, 120s) before marking step failed
4) only quarantine after nccl fails on 3 attempts (and only if error matches “connection closed/timeout” bucket?)

Nina Petrova: careful on quarantining based solely on that string; we should split error classes:
- timeout (test didn’t finish)
- socket connect failures
- NCCL errors w/ Xid / nvlink (hardware)
Only the last category should auto-quarantine quickly.

Gabe Wilson: good point. another snippet from a different node (this one *should* quarantine):
```bash
NCCL WARN Call to connect returned Connection timed out, retrying
NCCL WARN NET/Socket : call to connect returned Connection refused
...
CUDA failure 'an illegal memory access was encountered'
NCCL WARN Cuda failure 'an illegal memory access was encountered'
```

Sahana Iyer: illegal mem access -> likely bad GPU / driver issue. but the earlier “remote peer” could just be timing.

Keiko Yamamoto: can we gate nccl smoke test on a simple “network ready” check first? like ping to peer pods or verify node has expected routes? even a tiny TCP check between pods could reduce false negatives.

Gabe Wilson: we already do a basic DNS + outbound check. we don’t do pod-to-pod. could add a lightweight “mesh handshake” between the nccl pods before running all_reduce.

Sergio Costa: if you add handshake, instrument it separately so we can see which sub-step is failing (handshake vs nccl). otherwise dashboards just show “nccl failed” and it’s opaque.

Aisha Bello: +1. also keep span names stable. something like:
- provision.validation.nccl.handshake
- provision.validation.nccl.all_reduce

Vanessa Ortiz: action items:
- Gabe: update validation agent to (a) longer timeout, (b) retries/backoff, (c) classify errors into buckets. link it to ENG-4824 + ENG-4825.
- Keiko: propose where to insert delay between join and validation start (or check “node network ready”)
- Sahana: confirm containerd image pull time distribution; if needed we pre-pull nccl image in the golden image build or early daemon.

Gabe Wilson: ack. I’ll draft changes behind a config flag so we can tune without redeploy. probably values:
- `nccl.timeoutSeconds`: 240
- `nccl.maxAttempts`: 3
- `nccl.backoffSeconds`: 30 (exp)
- `nccl.quarantineOn`: `hardware_error_only`

Rafael Mendes: please ensure quarantine policy change is consistent with the post-provision quarantine draft. we don’t want agents making unilateral quarantine decisions without emitting the right reason.

Gabe Wilson: yep, quarantine will still be orchestrator decision; agent just returns `failure_class=transient_network|timeout|hardware` + log pointer.

Miles Thornton: also: include the observed attempt count/duration in the validation artifact so we can compute “would have passed if we waited longer”.

Gabe Wilson: will add fields: `attempt`, `elapsed_ms`, `exit_code`, `failure_class`, `last_20_lines`.

Vanessa Ortiz: cool. let’s try this in us-east A100 only. success criteria: nccl flake <1% and no big hit to P95 time-to-ready.

Aisha Bello: I’ll watch the dashboard once the change lands. ping me when the metric names are finalized so I can ensure alerts don’t page on the transient bucket during rollout.

Sergio Costa: +1, share the metric names. if you’re adding `failure_class`, that’s a good dimension.

Gabe Wilson: will do. PR later today.

Harish Reddy: from ops: if nodes are stuck in Validating longer due to retries, can we surface “attempt 2/3 (backing off 60s)” somewhere? makes oncall less nervous.

Gabe Wilson: good call. readiness service already supports “details” string; we can populate with current attempt/backoff.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok let’s do it. thanks all.

```bash
CUDA failure 'an illegal memory access was encountered'
NCCL WARN Cuda failure 'an illegal memory access was encountered'
```

Sahana Iyer: illegal mem access -> likely bad GPU / driver issue. but the earlier “remote peer” could just be timing.

Keiko Yamamoto: can we gate nccl smoke test on a simple “network ready” check first? like ping to peer pods or verify node has expected routes? even a tiny TCP check between pods could reduce false negatives.

Gabe Wilson: we already do a basic DNS + outbound check. we don’t do pod-to-pod. could add a lightweight “mesh handshake” between the nccl pods before running all_reduce.

Sergio Costa: if you add handshake, instrument it separately so we can see which sub-step is failing (handshake vs nccl). otherwise dashboards just show “nccl failed” and it’s opaque.

Aisha Bello: +1. also keep span names stable. something like:
- provision.validation.nccl.handshake
- provision.validation.nccl.all_reduce

Vanessa Ortiz: action items:
- Gabe: update validation agent to (a) longer timeout, (b) retries/backoff, (c) classify errors into buckets. link it to ENG-4824 + ENG-4825.
- Keiko: propose where to insert delay between join and validation start (or check “node network ready”)
- Sahana: confirm containerd image pull time distribution; if needed we pre-pull nccl image in the golden image build or early daemon.

Gabe Wilson: ack. I’ll draft changes behind a config flag so we can tune without redeploy. probably values:
- `nccl.timeoutSeconds`: 240
- `nccl.maxAttempts`: 3
- `nccl.backoffSeconds`: 30 (exp)
- `nccl.quarantineOn`: `hardware_error_only`

Rafael Mendes: please ensure quarantine policy change is consistent with the post-provision quarantine draft. we don’t want agents making unilateral quarantine decisions without emitting the right reason.

Gabe Wilson: yep, quarantine will still be orchestrator decision; agent just returns `failure_class=transient_network|timeout|hardware` + log pointer.

Miles Thornton: also: include the observed attempt count/duration in the validation artifact so we can compute “would have passed if we waited longer”.

Gabe Wilson: will add fields: `attempt`, `elapsed_ms`, `exit_code`, `failure_class`, `last_20_lines`.

Vanessa Ortiz: cool. let’s try this in us-east A100 only. success criteria: nccl flake <1% and no big hit to P95 time-to-ready.

Aisha Bello: I’ll watch the dashboard once the change lands. ping me when the metric names are finalized so I can ensure alerts don’t page on the transient bucket during rollout.

Sergio Costa: +1, share the metric names. if you’re adding `failure_class`, that’s a good dimension.

Gabe Wilson: will do. PR later today.

Harish Reddy: from ops: if nodes are stuck in Validating longer due to retries, can we surface “attempt 2/3 (backing off 60s)” somewhere? makes oncall less nervous.

Gabe Wilson: good call. readiness service already supports “details” string; we can populate with current attempt/backoff.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok let’s do it. thanks all.

source.messages:
Irene Choi: pulling folks into this thread re backup/snapshot mechanism for Private upgrades. need something that works across AWS EBS, generic CSI (EKS + on-prem), and fallback to logical backups. what’s the realistic lowest common denom?

Vanessa Ortiz: for VPC installs on AWS we can do EBS snapshots pretty reliably, but if PV is not EBS (gp3) then we’re back in CSI land. also: data plane caches are “nice to have” restore, control plane DB is “must be consistent”.

Fatima Noor: on-prem: no EBS, obviously. and not every customer cluster has CSI snapshot CRDs installed/enabled. some are on Ceph/Rook (supports snapshots), some on vSphere (depends), some on bare metal NFS (no snapshots). anything that *requires* VolumeSnapshot will be a blocker.

Bruno Silva: +1. I’ve seen air-gapped customers with storageclass = nfs-client provisioner. there is no snapshot capability. also no object store sometimes, just local S3-compatible minio.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE side: during upgrade we need “backup succeeded” to mean we can actually roll back without guesswork. if the backup is best-effort, we should label it WARN and require operator acknowledgement.

Irene Choi: current proposal in backup strategy doc is:
- primary: DB logical backup (`pg_dump` / `pg_dumpall` depending) + config/state export
- optional: PV snapshot where supported (EBS snapshot or CSI VolumeSnapshot)
- optional: model cache snapshot (can be skipped)
Question is: do we treat PV snapshots as REQUIRED for rollback guarantee?

Rafael Mendes: I’d avoid “required” unless we can detect support and provide fallback. rollback should work even if only DB/config backed up. caches can be repopulated.

Vanessa Ortiz: agree. but we should be explicit what PVs we care about. control plane DB volume is biggest one. if DB is external (RDS) then snapshots are a different API anyway.

Bruno Silva: installer can detect topology:
- embedded postgres (statefulset + PVC) -> do pg_dump + (if available) volume snapshot
- external postgres (customer managed) -> require credentials + we can do pg_dump OR they provide a backup hook (we just verify last backup timestamp?)

Connor O'Brien: release eng perspective: need deterministic behavior in upgrade plan. I’m nervous about “try snapshot if possible” because it changes rollback semantics run-to-run. can we encode snapshot strategy in plan output?

Irene Choi: yes. plan generator can output something like:
- backup_steps: ["preflight.storage.snapshot_api_available", "db.logical_dump", "pvc.snapshot (skipped: not_supported)"]
so it’s auditable.

Devon Price: if we’re using CSI snapshots, we should standardize on K8s VolumeSnapshot API. but we need preflight that checks:
- CRDs exist
- at least 1 VolumeSnapshotClass
- the PVC storageclass supports it (not always easy)

Sahana Iyer: for tests: we can cover EBS snapshots in AWS ephemeral clusters, and CSI snapshots via a test driver (maybe hostpath csi w/ snapshot support). but NFS no-snapshot path should also be validated (logical only).

Fatima Noor: on-prem constraint: many clusters are locked down; installing snapshot-controller CRDs is a change request. we should *not* require that to upgrade. if present, great.

Sean Gallagher: also: time. snapshots are fast but `pg_dump` on large DB can be slow; we need to set expectations and timeouts. if pg_dump fails, that should block upgrade.

Vanessa Ortiz: + remind: consistent backup needs app quiesce. at least: put control plane into “maintenance mode” / stop writes before snapshot/dump.

Irene Choi: yep. sequencing we’ve been using:
1) prechecks
2) pause reconcilers / maintenance mode
3) backup (db dump + optional snapshots)
4) upgrade control plane
5) run migrations
6) upgrade data plane
7) resume reconcilers

Bruno Silva: how do we implement EBS snapshot? call AWS API directly from installer? that gets gnarly w/ IAM.

Vanessa Ortiz: for EKS we can rely on CSI snapshot (ebs-csi supports it). better than bespoke AWS API in installer.

Fatima Noor: but note: some customers run older EKS and don’t have snapshot CRDs installed even though EBS CSI driver supports it.

Devon Price: preflight can detect. something like:
`kubectl get crd volumesnapshots.snapshot.storage.k8s.io` and `kubectl get volumesnapshotclass`
If missing -> mark snapshot path unavailable.

Rafael Mendes: what about “filesystem-level” backup? (rsync) probably not reliable/too slow.

Irene Choi: not planning filesystem copy. we’ll keep it to logical dump + snapshot API.

Sean Gallagher: rollback semantics: if only logical DB dump exists, restore is “restore DB + redeploy previous version”. if we also had PVC snapshot, restore is “restore PVC + redeploy previous version”. we need clear precedence.

Irene Choi: makes sense: prefer volume snapshot restore if present (faster) *but* only if taken after maintenance mode. else we fall back to logical restore.

Connor O'Brien: can we document the rule: "Snapshots are opportunistic accelerators, not the source of truth"? so rollback guarantee comes from logical backup.

Fatima Noor: +1. that’s the only story that works on NFS.

Sahana Iyer: can we make preflight print a one-liner summary like:
- DB backup: logical dump (required) -> OK
- Volume snapshots: not supported (warn)
so operators know.

Irene Choi: yup. Also: we should capture in the upgrade report artifact.

Bruno Silva: one more thing: where do we store logical dumps for air-gapped? local path + they can move it?

Fatima Noor: many on-prem want dump stored on an internal S3 (minio) or shared filesystem. installer should support `--backup-target` (s3 endpoint + creds) OR local dir.

Sean Gallagher: from reliability: please default to “backup location must be on durable storage”. local node path is risky if pod dies.

Vanessa Ortiz: + if we’re in k8s job, local path is on container FS unless we mount something. so local mode should require a mounted PVC or hostPath (hostPath is also meh).

Irene Choi: action items:
- add preflight checks: snapshot CRDs/classes present; warn if absent
- backup strategy: logical DB dump is REQUIRED; CSI snapshots optional
- on-prem: support S3-compatible endpoint + local mounted PVC target
- update plan schema to record which snapshot method used / skipped

Devon Price: I’ll add to installer preflight framework: `storage.snapshot_api` check + remediation text (install snapshot-controller, create VolumeSnapshotClass).

Connor O'Brien: I’ll note in release gating checklist that upgrade plan must include backup strategy summary.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: also please ensure we emit audit log event with backup mode (logical only vs logical+snapshot).

Irene Choi: yep, tying into the audit events work. will loop in Allison later.

Sahana Iyer: for CI, I’ll add a scenario: "no snapshot CRDs" -> upgrade proceeds with logical backup only, and rollback uses logical restore.

Fatima Noor: thanks. also please avoid wording like “snapshot required” in operator docs. it’ll trigger a bunch of change-control tickets.

Irene Choi: noted. I’ll update the runbook draft language. closing this thread for now; will link the updated confluence section once it’s in.
