source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: Heads up from yesterday’s incident review: incident-bot diagnostics caused a mini thundering herd on telemetry when multiple responders hit “refresh signals” in #incidents. We need hard bounds (rate limits, circuit breakers, backoff) before v2 goes wider.

Sean Gallagher: +1. This is exactly how we get cascading failures during Sev1/Sev0. What’s our current shape? Any global caps on diag queries?

Miles Thornton: Right now bot fans out to 3 backends (deploy-metadata, config-diff, telemetry-query). Telemetry is the big one: ~8–15 queries per card render depending on tenant/region selection. We have some basic per-process concurrency limit but no distributed/global limit.

Logan Wright: Telemetry side: the query gateway already has per-user limits, but bot uses a service identity so it bypasses “human” shaping. When the bot bursts it shows up as a single principal doing 200+ QPS.

Nadia Rahman: We can add a dedicated “diagnostics” quota bucket at the gateway keyed on service principal + route. But we also need the bot to degrade (serve cached summaries / links) instead of retrying forever.

Jacob Stein: From the SRE side we need two layers:
1) Client-side: concurrency cap + exponential backoff + request coalescing in the bot.
2) Server-side: token bucket rate limits + circuit breaker in telemetry-query-service.

Dylan Brooks: Agree. For v2 we’re already adding caching in the bot (ENG-5427) but infra guardrails should assume worst case (a bunch of people spamming buttons, and Slack retries).

Sean Gallagher: Can we decide numbers? Like max QPS for bot diagnostics per region? Also should be “per incident thread” so one noisy incident doesn’t starve everyone.

Logan Wright: Suggest:
- Global per-region cap for incident-bot principal at gateway: 20 QPS sustained, burst 60 for 10s.
- Additionally cap concurrency: max 25 in-flight per region.
We can tune based on p95 query latency.

Caleb Johnson: Also please cap cardinality / query complexity. Some of the proposed saturation queries use tag combos that explode. We should enforce a max time window and max series returned.

Miles Thornton: On bot side we can:
- Coalesce identical requests within 30s (same incident_id+service+region+window).
- Add a “cooldown” for the interactive refresh button (e.g., 60s per thread) so we don’t stampede.
- Set per-thread token bucket (2 refresh/min) with a friendly message.

Rafael Mendes: Love the per-thread bucket. We can treat the thread_ts as a key.

Nadia Rahman: Server-side: I can add a circuit breaker on telemetry gateway responses for the diagnostics route. Something like:
- Open breaker if error rate > 30% over 30s OR p95 latency > 8s over 2m.
- When open: return 429 with `Retry-After` and a “degraded_mode=true” header.

Dylan Brooks: If we can get a clear signal (429 + degraded header), the bot can immediately switch to cached context + deep links and stop retrying.

Sean Gallagher: This is important: no automatic retries on 429. Only retry on timeouts maybe, but with cap.

Sahana Iyer: Re: retries. Please don’t do “retry storms.” Rule of thumb:
- retry only idempotent GETs
- max 2 retries
- full jitter backoff (e.g., 250ms, 1s)
- and stop immediately on 429/403.

Connor O'Brien: Deploy metadata endpoint is cheap-ish, but during outages our internal service registry can be slow. Do we also want circuit breaking there?

Jacob Stein: Yes but smaller risk. Telemetry is the main blast radius. Still: a general “fanout budget” would help.

Miles Thornton: We can implement a per-card render budget:
- total render timeout 4s for initial message
- each backend gets 1.5s and we take partial results
- if any backend times out, show link and “try again later”
This keeps Slack UX usable.

Logan Wright: On telemetry query complexity: we can enforce:
- max lookback 30m for incident bot
- fixed aggregations (avg/max) rather than raw series
- max 50 series
If bot asks for more, return 400 with guidance.

Caleb Johnson: + also sanitize label sets. For example, do not allow freeform tenant_id OR request_id in metrics queries. Use our tenant-safe query primitive only.

Rafael Mendes: Ok, concrete action items:
1) Infra/telemetry (Logan/Nadia): gateway rate limit for incident-bot principal + diagnostics route caps (QPS + burst) + circuit breaker returning 429/Retry-After + degraded header.
2) Bot/tooling (Miles/Dylan): per-thread refresh cooldown + request coalescing + respect 429 (no retries) + retry policy w/ jitter.
3) Observability (Caleb): add dashboard for “diagnostics query load” and alert if bot is >X% of gateway traffic.

Sean Gallagher: Can we also add a circuit breaker “load shedding” mode on the bot itself? If it detects upstream unhealthy it should stop calling telemetry for a while.

Dylan Brooks: Yes. We can implement a simple client-side breaker:
- track last N telemetry calls
- if >50% are 429/timeouts over 60s, disable live queries for 5m and show cached+links.

Miles Thornton: I’ll wire that into the renderer. Copy for user:
“Diagnostics temporarily in degraded mode (telemetry under load). Showing cached signals from <time>. Use links for live dashboards.”

Grafana: (auto) Dashboard created: "Diagnostics / Query Gateway Load" https://grafana.redwood.example/d/diag-qg-load

Caleb Johnson: Nice. I’ll add panels for `qg_requests_total{principal="incident-bot"}` and p95 latency per route.

Nadia Rahman: One nuance: 429 vs 503. For circuit breaker, 429 is better (explicit backoff). 503 tends to get retried automatically by some clients.

Sahana Iyer: +1. Also ensure we set `Retry-After` in seconds.

Connor O'Brien: Deploy metadata endpoint: we can add local cache in bot too (deploy context doesn’t change minute-to-minute). TTL 2m?

Miles Thornton: Works. Deploy context TTL 2m, config diff TTL 5m (but invalidate if new config push event), telemetry TTL 30–60s.

Rafael Mendes: Any objections to the suggested limits? 20 QPS sustained / 60 burst per region for telemetry route?

Logan Wright: That’s fine as a starting point. If p95 goes up we’ll lower it; if it’s too strict we can raise after we see production usage.

Sean Gallagher: Make sure we have a “break glass” override for Sev0 where oncall can request a higher budget (but audited). Not required for first pass.

Dylan Brooks: We can do a manual flag for SRE-only to raise per-thread limit (still within global cap).

Jacob Stein: Please document the behavior in the runbook: responders should not spam refresh; rely on links if degraded.

title: #eng-infra: 1739298841
preview:
Rafael Mendes: Heads up from yesterday’s incident review: incident-bot diagnostics caused a mini thundering herd on telemetry when multiple responders hit “refresh signals” in #incidents. We need hard bounds (rate limits, circuit breakers, backoff) before v2 goes wider.

Sean Gallagher: +1. This is exactly how we get cascading failures during Sev1/Sev0. What’s our current shape? Any global caps on diag queries?

Miles Thornton: Right now bot fans out to 3 backends (deploy-metadata, config-diff, telemetry-query). Telemetry is the big one: ~8–15 queries per card render depending on tenant/region selection. We have some basic per-process concurrency limit but no distributed/global limit.

Logan Wright: Telemetry side: the query gateway already has per-user limits, but bot uses a service identity so it bypasses “human” shaping. When the bot bursts it shows up as a single principal doing 200+ QPS.

Nadia Rahman: We can add a dedicated “diagnostics” quota bucket at the gateway keyed on service principal + route. But we also need the bot to degrade (serve cached summaries / links) instead of retrying forever.

Jacob Stein: From the SRE side we need two layers:
1) Client-side: concurrency cap + exponential backoff + request coalescing in the bot.
2) Server-side: token bucket rate limits + circuit breaker in telemetry-query-service.

Dylan Brooks: Agree. For v2 we’re already adding caching in the bot (ENG-5427) but infra guardrails should assume worst case (a bunch of people spamming buttons, and Slack retries).

Sean Gallagher: Can we decide numbers? Like max QPS for bot diagnostics per region? Also should be “per incident thread” so one noisy incident doesn’t starve everyone.

Logan Wright: Suggest:
- Global per-region cap for incident-bot principal at gateway: 20 QPS sustained, burst 60 for 10s.
- Additionally cap concurrency: max 25 in-flight per region.
We can tune based on p95 query latency.

Caleb Johnson: Also please cap cardinality / query complexity. Some of the proposed saturation queries use tag combos that explode. We should enforce a max time window and max series returned.

Miles Thornton: On bot side we can:
- Coalesce identical requests within 30s (same incident_id+service+region+window).
- Add a “cooldown” for the interactive refresh button (e.g., 60s per thread) so we don’t stampede.
- Set per-thread token bucket (2 refresh/min) with a friendly message.

Rafael Mendes: Love the per-thread bucket. We can treat the thread_ts as a key.

Nadia Rahman: Server-side: I can add a circuit breaker on telemetry gateway responses for the diagnostics route. Something like:
- Open breaker if error rate > 30% over 30s OR p95 latency > 8s over 2m.
- When open: return 429 with `Retry-After` and a “degraded_mode=true” header.

Dylan Brooks: If we can get a clear signal (429 + degraded header), the bot can immediately switch to cached context + deep links and stop retrying.

Sean Gallagher: This is important: no automatic retries on 429. Only retry on timeouts maybe, but with cap.

Sahana Iyer: Re: retries. Please don’t do “retry storms.” Rule of thumb:
- retry only idempotent GETs
- max 2 retries
- full jitter backoff (e.g., 250ms, 1s)
- and stop immediately on 429/403.

Connor O'Brien: Deploy metadata endpoint is cheap-ish, but during outages our internal service registry can be slow. Do we also want circuit breaking there?

Jacob Stein: Yes but smaller risk. Telemetry is the main blast radius. Still: a general “fanout budget” would help.

Miles Thornton: We can implement a per-card render budget:
- total render timeout 4s for initial message
- each backend gets 1.5s and we take partial results
- if any backend times out, show link and “try again later”
This keeps Slack UX usable.

Logan Wright: On telemetry query complexity: we can enforce:
- max lookback 30m for incident bot
- fixed aggregations (avg/max) rather than raw series
- max 50 series
If bot asks for more, return 400 with guidance.

Caleb Johnson: + also sanitize label sets. For example, do not allow freeform tenant_id OR request_id in metrics queries. Use our tenant-safe query primitive only.

Rafael Mendes: Ok, concrete action items:
1) Infra/telemetry (Logan/Nadia): gateway rate limit for incident-bot principal + diagnostics route caps (QPS + burst) + circuit breaker returning 429/Retry-After + degraded header.
2) Bot/tooling (Miles/Dylan): per-thread refresh cooldown + request coalescing + respect 429 (no retries) + retry policy w/ jitter.
3) Observability (Caleb): add dashboard for “diagnostics query load” and alert if bot is >X% of gateway traffic.

Sean Gallagher: Can we also add a circuit breaker “load shedding” mode on the bot itself? If it detects upstream unhealthy it should stop calling telemetry for a while.

Dylan Brooks: Yes. We can implement a simple client-side breaker:
- track last N telemetry calls
- if >50% are 429/timeouts over 60s, disable live queries for 5m and show cached+links.

Miles Thornton: I’ll wire that into the renderer. Copy for user:
“Diagnostics temporarily in degraded mode (telemetry under load). Showing cached signals from <time>. Use links for live dashboards.”

Grafana: (auto) Dashboard created: "Diagnostics / Query Gateway Load" https://grafana.redwood.example/d/diag-qg-load

Caleb Johnson: Nice. I’ll add panels for `qg_requests_total{principal="incident-bot"}` and p95 latency per route.

Nadia Rahman: One nuance: 429 vs 503. For circuit breaker, 429 is better (explicit backoff). 503 tends to get retried automatically by some clients.

Sahana Iyer: +1. Also ensure we set `Retry-After` in seconds.

Connor O'Brien: Deploy metadata endpoint: we can add local cache in bot too (deploy context doesn’t change minute-to-minute). TTL 2m?

Miles Thornton: Works. Deploy context TTL 2m, config diff TTL 5m (but invalidate if new config push event), telemetry TTL 30–60s.

Rafael Mendes: Any objections to the suggested limits? 20 QPS sustained / 60 burst per region for telemetry route?

Logan Wright: That’s fine as a starting point. If p95 goes up we’ll lower it; if it’s too strict we can raise after we see production usage.

Sean Gallagher: Make sure we have a “break glass” override for Sev0 where oncall can request a higher budget (but audited). Not required for first pass.

Dylan Brooks: We can do a manual flag for SRE-only to raise per-thread limit (still within global cap).

Jacob Stein: Please document the behavior in the runbook: responders should not spam refresh; rely on links if degraded.

Rafael Mendes: Cool. I’ll post a summary in #eng-sre and link this thread. Let’s target the gateway rate limit + bot 429 handling this week, before expanding the beta cohort.

incident-bot: (system) Reminder: When posting incident context, avoid sharing unredacted tenant config. Use approved “customer-safe” context cards only.

source.text:
Vanessa Ortiz: We need to lock quarantine policy this week so orchestrator + readiness svc can ship. Question: do we auto-quarantine after N validation failures, or always reimage + retry until it passes?

Harish Reddy: speaking from capacity ops: always reimage is expensive + slow, but auto-quarantine too aggressive can starve pools during expansions. We need a middle ground + operator override.

Jamal Pierce: +1, in big Dedicated adds we can’t afford 10–20% to quarantine on flaky checks. If quarantine rate spikes, we drop available throughput and support pings us.

Keiko Yamamoto: What’s the failure distribution from baseline? Are we talking deterministic failures (driver mismatch) vs transient (network jitter / registry timeouts)?

Vanessa Ortiz: Baseline notes: most common are kubelet join flake, NCCL smoke timeout, occasional driver parity mismatch when image channel drifts. Driver mismatch is deterministic; NCCL/Network sometimes flaky.

Nina Petrova: Deterministic failures should quarantine fast (don’t burn cycles). Flaky validations should be retried w/ backoff, and reimage only after repeat.

Benji Okafor: For readiness state machine, easiest is: Validating -> (pass) Ready; (fail) stays Validating with `attempt_count++` and records artifacts; when attempts exceed threshold, transition to Quarantined with failure_reason. We can also support `operator_clear_quarantine`.

Jordan Lee: +1. Also: we should define “attempt” semantics: is it re-running the validation checks, or full re-provision/reimage? Those are different and should be explicit in artifacts.

Harish Reddy: Exactly. From ops POV: first try should be “rerun validation” (cheap). If still failing, then “reimage” (expensive). If still failing after reimage, quarantine.

Rafael Mendes: SRE concern: if we don’t quarantine quickly, we might keep churning bad nodes and inflate time-to-ready + noise. But if we quarantine too quickly, we create capacity shortfalls. Need guardrails and clear alerting.

Sean Gallagher: Can we define classes of failures?
- Class A: hardware/driver/agent integrity -> immediate quarantine
- Class B: cluster join / config -> retry then reimage then quarantine
- Class C: perf thresholds / NCCL -> retry w/ tuned timeouts, maybe ticket not page

Vanessa Ortiz: That’s roughly what I had in mind. Proposed draft:
1) Run preflight checks (agent heartbeat, disk layout, time sync) once; if missing critical bits -> reimage.
2) Postflight validations run up to 2 times w/ exponential backoff.
3) If still failing, trigger one reimage + rerun validations.
4) If fails again, auto-quarantine.

Keiko Yamamoto: What’s “2 times” here? 2 validation reruns, then reimage (1), then 1 more validation cycle? So max 4 total? Need to be crisp.

Vanessa Ortiz: Good call. Concrete:
- Validation reruns: max 2 (same image)
- If fail after 2 reruns -> reimage once
- After reimage -> run validations once
- If fail after reimage validation -> quarantine
So max validation attempts recorded = 3 (2 + 1 after reimage).

Jamal Pierce: Can we allow a pool-level override for “don’t auto-quarantine on perf canary failures during expansion”, just mark NotReady and keep retrying? If we’re short on capacity, ops might accept a slower canary.

Nina Petrova: Careful: letting perf canary slide can push bad nodes into serving and create tail latency incidents. Better: keep the readiness gate strict, but allow ops to temporarily disable the *canary check* via feature flag (with audit trail).

Benji Okafor: Control plane can support per-pool “validation profile” reference (e.g., `strict`, `expansion-relaxed`) and we log when it changes. But for sprint scope we can add a simple config knob: `quarantine_on_step_failures=[...]` and `max_attempts`.

Sahana Iyer: Image pipeline angle: if the failure is driver parity mismatch or missing kernel module, rerunning validations won’t fix it. We should quarantine immediately or at least force reimage with a pinned stable channel.

Fiona McCarthy: From security/compliance: if agent reports image provenance mismatch OR OS hardening checks fail, do not keep retrying/reimaging indefinitely. Quarantine + ticket. Also need audit event when operators override quarantine or relax validation profile.

Harish Reddy: +1 on audit trail. Also we need a “quarantine reason” that’s actionable. Nothing worse than Quarantined: UNKNOWN.

Rafael Mendes: For paging: I’d rather we page on quarantine rate spike by pool/region, not individual nodes. Individual node failures should create tickets unless it’s a systemic step failure.

Vanessa Ortiz: Ok. Let’s enumerate immediate-quarantine triggers. Proposal:
- GPU health: ECC DBE, persistent Xid codes, GPU missing, NVML errors
- Image integrity: image channel mismatch vs pool pin, missing signed artifacts, hardening baseline fail
- Driver/toolkit mismatch: driver version != expected for model matrix
Everything else: retry -> reimage -> quarantine.

Nina Petrova: Add “fabric / NCCL errors that are deterministic” e.g. fails at same stage across retries. But that might be indistinguishable from transient. Maybe treat NCCL as retryable but limit retries.

Keiko Yamamoto: Agree. NCCL all-reduce smoke test: retryable twice, then reimage, then if still failing -> quarantine (likely fabric).

Jamal Pierce: Ops ask: when quarantined, do we auto-replace capacity? Or do we just surface it and humans request new instances?

Vanessa Ortiz: For Dedicated pools, capacity manager will request replacements when nodes never hit Ready within a window. Not in this thread, but quarantine should emit a clear signal so the automation can replace.

Benji Okafor: We’ll expose `state=Quarantined` + `failure_reason` + `step` + `attempts` in readiness API so tooling can do replacements.

Harish Reddy: Practical remediation order in runbook should be: drain (if it somehow joined), then quarantine, then reimage. Not “reimage first” because it can leave the cluster in weird state.

Sean Gallagher: Sounds like consensus:
- auto-quarantine after bounded retries
- one reimage attempt max before quarantine
- immediate quarantine for safety/security/hardware
- operator override + audit logging

Vanessa Ortiz: ✅ I’ll update quarantine policy draft with:
- max retries: 2 reruns + 1 after reimage
- immediate quarantine triggers list
- operator override + required reason
- metrics: quarantine_count by reason/step + quarantine_rate alerting (pool-level)

Rafael Mendes: Please add: if quarantine rate > X% in 30m for a pool, auto-disable quarantine (kill switch) AND page Infra on-call? Or at least flip to ticket + require manual action.

Keiko Yamamoto: I’d rather not auto-disable quarantine (safety). But we can add a kill switch that on-call can flip quickly. Default: page + stop rollout.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. We’ll use rollout feature flag to stop adding nodes if quarantine rate spikes, and page.

Sahana Iyer: I’ll add a note: driver mismatch should reference image channel in the reason string, e.g. `DRIVER_PARITY_FAIL expected=535.129.03 got=535.146.02 channel=candidate`.

Harish Reddy: Works for ops. As long as we can see reason + have a quick “reimage and retry” button in tooling later.

Vanessa Ortiz: Cool. Action items:
- VO: update policy doc + share in #eng-infra
- Benji/Jordan: confirm readiness API fields support attempts + step + reason + operator override
- Rafael: propose pool-level quarantine spike alert thresholds

Jamal Pierce: :thumbsup: please ping me when thresholds are proposed; want to sanity-check against expansion patterns.

Fiona McCarthy: And ping security for the immediate quarantine triggers list before it’s final.

Vanessa Ortiz: will do.

Fiona McCarthy: And ping security for the immediate quarantine triggers list before it’s final.

Vanessa Ortiz: will do.
text:
Vanessa Ortiz: We need to lock quarantine policy this week so orchestrator + readiness svc can ship. Question: do we auto-quarantine after N validation failures, or always reimage + retry until it passes?

Harish Reddy: speaking from capacity ops: always reimage is expensive + slow, but auto-quarantine too aggressive can starve pools during expansions. We need a middle ground + operator override.

Jamal Pierce: +1, in big Dedicated adds we can’t afford 10–20% to quarantine on flaky checks. If quarantine rate spikes, we drop available throughput and support pings us.

Keiko Yamamoto: What’s the failure distribution from baseline? Are we talking deterministic failures (driver mismatch) vs transient (network jitter / registry timeouts)?

Vanessa Ortiz: Baseline notes: most common are kubelet join flake, NCCL smoke timeout, occasional driver parity mismatch when image channel drifts. Driver mismatch is deterministic; NCCL/Network sometimes flaky.

Nina Petrova: Deterministic failures should quarantine fast (don’t burn cycles). Flaky validations should be retried w/ backoff, and reimage only after repeat.

Benji Okafor: For readiness state machine, easiest is: Validating -> (pass) Ready; (fail) stays Validating with `attempt_count++` and records artifacts; when attempts exceed threshold, transition to Quarantined with failure_reason. We can also support `operator_clear_quarantine`.

Jordan Lee: +1. Also: we should define “attempt” semantics: is it re-running the validation checks, or full re-provision/reimage? Those are different and should be explicit in artifacts.

Harish Reddy: Exactly. From ops POV: first try should be “rerun validation” (cheap). If still failing, then “reimage” (expensive). If still failing after reimage, quarantine.

Rafael Mendes: SRE concern: if we don’t quarantine quickly, we might keep churning bad nodes and inflate time-to-ready + noise. But if we quarantine too quickly, we create capacity shortfalls. Need guardrails and clear alerting.

Sean Gallagher: Can we define classes of failures?
- Class A: hardware/driver/agent integrity -> immediate quarantine
- Class B: cluster join / config -> retry then reimage then quarantine
- Class C: perf thresholds / NCCL -> retry w/ tuned timeouts, maybe ticket not page

Vanessa Ortiz: That’s roughly what I had in mind. Proposed draft:
1) Run preflight checks (agent heartbeat, disk layout, time sync) once; if missing critical bits -> reimage.
2) Postflight validations run up to 2 times w/ exponential backoff.
3) If still failing, trigger one reimage + rerun validations.
4) If fails again, auto-quarantine.

Keiko Yamamoto: What’s “2 times” here? 2 validation reruns, then reimage (1), then 1 more validation cycle? So max 4 total? Need to be crisp.

Vanessa Ortiz: Good call. Concrete:
- Validation reruns: max 2 (same image)
- If fail after 2 reruns -> reimage once
- After reimage -> run validations once
- If fail after reimage validation -> quarantine
So max validation attempts recorded = 3 (2 + 1 after reimage).

Jamal Pierce: Can we allow a pool-level override for “don’t auto-quarantine on perf canary failures during expansion”, just mark NotReady and keep retrying? If we’re short on capacity, ops might accept a slower canary.

Nina Petrova: Careful: letting perf canary slide can push bad nodes into serving and create tail latency incidents. Better: keep the readiness gate strict, but allow ops to temporarily disable the *canary check* via feature flag (with audit trail).

Benji Okafor: Control plane can support per-pool “validation profile” reference (e.g., `strict`, `expansion-relaxed`) and we log when it changes. But for sprint scope we can add a simple config knob: `quarantine_on_step_failures=[...]` and `max_attempts`.

Sahana Iyer: Image pipeline angle: if the failure is driver parity mismatch or missing kernel module, rerunning validations won’t fix it. We should quarantine immediately or at least force reimage with a pinned stable channel.

Fiona McCarthy: From security/compliance: if agent reports image provenance mismatch OR OS hardening checks fail, do not keep retrying/reimaging indefinitely. Quarantine + ticket. Also need audit event when operators override quarantine or relax validation profile.

Harish Reddy: +1 on audit trail. Also we need a “quarantine reason” that’s actionable. Nothing worse than Quarantined: UNKNOWN.

Rafael Mendes: For paging: I’d rather we page on quarantine rate spike by pool/region, not individual nodes. Individual node failures should create tickets unless it’s a systemic step failure.

Vanessa Ortiz: Ok. Let’s enumerate immediate-quarantine triggers. Proposal:
- GPU health: ECC DBE, persistent Xid codes, GPU missing, NVML errors
- Image integrity: image channel mismatch vs pool pin, missing signed artifacts, hardening baseline fail
- Driver/toolkit mismatch: driver version != expected for model matrix
Everything else: retry -> reimage -> quarantine.

Nina Petrova: Add “fabric / NCCL errors that are deterministic” e.g. fails at same stage across retries. But that might be indistinguishable from transient. Maybe treat NCCL as retryable but limit retries.

Keiko Yamamoto: Agree. NCCL all-reduce smoke test: retryable twice, then reimage, then if still failing -> quarantine (likely fabric).

Jamal Pierce: Ops ask: when quarantined, do we auto-replace capacity? Or do we just surface it and humans request new instances?

Vanessa Ortiz: For Dedicated pools, capacity manager will request replacements when nodes never hit Ready within a window. Not in this thread, but quarantine should emit a clear signal so the automation can replace.

Benji Okafor: We’ll expose `state=Quarantined` + `failure_reason` + `step` + `attempts` in readiness API so tooling can do replacements.

Harish Reddy: Practical remediation order in runbook should be: drain (if it somehow joined), then quarantine, then reimage. Not “reimage first” because it can leave the cluster in weird state.

Sean Gallagher: Sounds like consensus:
- auto-quarantine after bounded retries
- one reimage attempt max before quarantine
- immediate quarantine for safety/security/hardware
- operator override + audit logging

Vanessa Ortiz: ✅ I’ll update quarantine policy draft with:
- max retries: 2 reruns + 1 after reimage
- immediate quarantine triggers list
- operator override + required reason
- metrics: quarantine_count by reason/step + quarantine_rate alerting (pool-level)

Rafael Mendes: Please add: if quarantine rate > X% in 30m for a pool, auto-disable quarantine (kill switch) AND page Infra on-call? Or at least flip to ticket + require manual action.

Keiko Yamamoto: I’d rather not auto-disable quarantine (safety). But we can add a kill switch that on-call can flip quickly. Default: page + stop rollout.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. We’ll use rollout feature flag to stop adding nodes if quarantine rate spikes, and page.

Sahana Iyer: I’ll add a note: driver mismatch should reference image channel in the reason string, e.g. `DRIVER_PARITY_FAIL expected=535.129.03 got=535.146.02 channel=candidate`.

Harish Reddy: Works for ops. As long as we can see reason + have a quick “reimage and retry” button in tooling later.

Vanessa Ortiz: Cool. Action items:
- VO: update policy doc + share in #eng-infra
- Benji/Jordan: confirm readiness API fields support attempts + step + reason + operator override
- Rafael: propose pool-level quarantine spike alert thresholds

Jamal Pierce: :thumbsup: please ping me when thresholds are proposed; want to sanity-check against expansion patterns.

Fiona McCarthy: And ping security for the immediate quarantine triggers list before it’s final.

Vanessa Ortiz: will do.

source.content:
Vanessa Ortiz: heads up, for burst pools we need a Terraform change that’s a bit more than “just another gpu pool”. Want to align on what infra bits we expect to land in redwood-terraform so we can start reserving capacity by region/hw class.

Vanessa Ortiz: requirements from Fleet side:
- provision shared burst pools per *region + hardware class* (ex: us-east/h100, eu-west/l40s)
- hard cap controls (fleet safety cap) + quick disable hooks
- attach monitoring/alerts (SRE asked for dashboards to be “on by default” for any pool)

Harish Reddy: +1. what I’m hearing is we need a module like `burst_pool` that wraps existing GPU pool primitives + opinionated defaults.

Keiko Yamamoto: yup. also autoscaling policy differs. baseline Dedicated pools scale around customer reserve; burst pools scale around fleet headroom + stricter max.

Jamal Pierce: can we agree on inputs? I’d like the sizing model to just output a target per (region, hw) and we feed that into TF.

Vanessa Ortiz: exactly. V1 can be manual numbers; later we can pipe from the sizing model.

Peter Holtz: from SRE side we need:
- alerts wired to oncall rotation (eng-sre) for saturation + admission timeout
- alerts to be labeled by (region, hw_class, pool)
- do *not* explode cardinality with tenant_id

Allison Grant: +1 on cardinality. metrics/alerts should be pool-level only. tenant breakdown belongs in app metrics, not infra module.

Sergio Costa: dashboards PR in observability-pack is almost ready, but it expects standard labels:
`region`, `hardware_class`, `pool_type` (should be `burst`), and `pool_name`.
If TF can force those tags/labels on the cluster/daemonset, that’s easiest.

Harish Reddy: ok, so module should:
- create nodegroup / MIG / ASG (depending on cloud) with labels
- deploy the serving runtime + burst-specific taints/tolerations
- install monitoring sidecars / scrape configs
- create alert rules + link to dashboard

Ethan Park: quick q: does TF need to know anything about admission controller endpoints? or is that in app deploy?

Keiko Yamamoto: TF shouldn’t hardcode service endpoints, but it should output pool identifiers that control plane uses (region/hw/pool_name). maybe as an output.

Vanessa Ortiz: agree. output should include pool ARN/cluster identifiers + a canonical `burst_pool_id` string.

Peter Holtz: also need a “kill switch” story. infra side: scaling to 0 + drain? control plane side: deny burst immediately. but for game day we want infra lever too.

Harish Reddy: we can add variable `enabled = true` and if false set desired=0 and apply a taint that prevents scheduling. still need to be careful about draining.

Keiko Yamamoto: draining matters. if we scale to 0 too aggressively we’ll evict active workloads and cause retries/backpressure. maybe provide:
- `disable_mode = {"soft"|"hard"}`
soft = stop new scheduling (taint) + keep current
hard = scale to 0

Allison Grant: +1. and alerts should have annotation for “disable_mode/hard” to avoid paging noise when we intentionally hard-disable.

Sergio Costa: for alerts, we can include a silencing label like `maintenance="true"` if module sets it.

Harish Reddy: what does the module interface look like? rough sketch:
```hcl
module "burst_pool" {
  source = "./modules/burst-pool"

  region         = "us-east"
  hardware_class = "h100"
  pool_name      = "burst-h100-us-east-1"

  min_nodes = 0
  max_nodes = 64
  desired_nodes = 16

  enabled      = true
  disable_mode = "soft"

  labels = {
    pool_type = "burst"
  }

  monitoring = {
    enable_alerts   = true
    enable_dash     = true
    pagerduty_route = "eng-sre"
  }
}
```

Vanessa Ortiz: looks good. add `safety_max_nodes` maybe? so even if someone sets max_nodes too high we cap.

Jamal Pierce: and a `supply_risk_tier` input? we talked about bigger buffers for constrained regions. not sure TF should care.

Vanessa Ortiz: that’s policy; I’d rather bake into sizing doc, not TF.

Keiko Yamamoto: we also need burst pool separation by runtime config (different batching defaults). can TF set env/values in Helm chart?

Harish Reddy: yes, module can call a shared helm release with `values` tuned for burst pools (e.g., more conservative concurrency). but we should keep it minimal in v1.

Peter Holtz: on the alert set, minimum viable:
- `BurstPoolSaturationHigh` (util > 0.9 for 10m)
- `BurstAdmissionControllerTimeouts` (if it’s infra-related)
- `BurstPoolQueueDepthHigh`
- `TelemetryLagHigh` (since we had that fairness/lag issue)

Allison Grant: telemetry lag is emitted by app, not node. but we can still install the rules centrally if Prometheus scrapes it.

Sergio Costa: yep. rule group can live in observability-pack and TF just ensures the namespace/labels exist. otherwise, TF-managed PrometheusRule is fine too.

Harish Reddy: preference? I’d rather avoid duplicating rules in TF. can TF just deploy dashboards + rules from observability-pack release?

Sergio Costa: that’s what we’re doing elsewhere. TF can install the `observability-pack` chart with `burst_enabled=true`.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok, and for Terraform PR we’ll need a new module + wiring in env stacks (us-east, eu-west). who owns the PR?

Harish Reddy: I can take first pass on PR in redwood-terraform. will loop you + Keiko for review.

Keiko Yamamoto: please include autoscaler settings. for burst pools we want:
- faster scale-up (shorter cooldown)
- slower scale-down (avoid thrash)
- max surge limit

Harish Reddy: ack. I’ll add `autoscaling` block.

Peter Holtz: and please ensure alerts route to eng-sre *but* also post summary to #eng-infra during provisioning, so we see when pools come online.

DeployBot: buildkite: Terraform plan generated for `burst-pool-module-wip` (no changes applied). Requested reviewers: @Vanessa Ortiz @Keiko Yamamoto @Peter Holtz

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: once the module exists, Fleet will fill in initial desired counts for us-east/h100 and eu-west/l40s.

Jamal Pierce: can you add outputs for `nodegroup_name`, `cluster_name`, `pool_labels` so capacity planning can audit what’s actually deployed?

Harish Reddy: yep.

Allison Grant: last thing: please don’t include tenant IDs in any tags. we had issues with cloud tag limits before. keep tags to region/hw/pool_type/pool_name.

Harish Reddy: got it.

Keiko Yamamoto: reminder: these pools must be constrained within region (no cross-region). so TF should prevent accidentally attaching global load balancer to burst pool.

Harish Reddy: good catch. I’ll avoid any global LB creation in module. it’ll be compute + cluster local service only.

Peter Holtz: :eyes: also add a runbook link annotation in alert rules (even if placeholder). SRE will fill it in.

Sergio Costa: I’ll paste the dashboard UID once the observability-pack PR merges.

Vanessa Ortiz: thanks all. Harish, ping when PR is up; we’ll prioritize review so we can start reserving capacity this week.

Neha Kapoor: heads up: saw pretty brutal NFS perf in the lab when running the new backup flow. Wanted to sanity check before we recommend NFS beyond "works but caveats".

Bruno Silva: oof. what numbers are we seeing + what setup?

Neha Kapoor: lab k8s (kind-ish on baremetal) w/ NFS server (single VM, gp3-backed). Backup job writes a single encrypted tarball ~28GB.
- expected: saturate 10GbE-ish (at least 200-400MB/s)
- actual: 18-35MB/s steady, plus periodic stalls (10-30s) during finalization

Sean Gallagher: stalls at the end sounds like fsync/close semantics on NFS. did we see it in logs?

Neha Kapoor: yep. installer log shows it basically parked on "finalizing artifact".

Bruno Silva: we do `sha256` + manifest write + optional read-back verify (depending flags). Were you running with verify on?

Neha Kapoor: default verify on. It reads back the tarball to compute checksum.

Sean Gallagher: so NFS is paying twice (write + full read). That’s still not 20MB/s though.

Colin O'Donnell: what mount options / NFS version?

Neha Kapoor: default from our test StorageClass:
- nfsvers=4.1
- rsize/wsize not set (so default)
- no nconnect

Miles Thornton: default rsize/wsize can be tiny depending on distro/kernel. worth explicitly setting 1MiB.

Bruno Silva: can you paste `mount` output?

Neha Kapoor: ```
$ mount | grep nfs
10.0.12.44:/exports/redwood-backups on /var/lib/kubelet/pods/.../volumes/kubernetes.io~nfs/backup type nfs4 (rw,relatime,vers=4.1,rsize=262144,wsize=262144,namlen=255,hard,proto=tcp,timeo=600,retrans=2,sec=sys,clientaddr=10.0.9.18,local_lock=none)
```

Colin O'Donnell: rsize/wsize 256K is leaving perf on the table. also relatime -> noatime might help a bit but not huge for a single file.

Rafael Mendes: do we have any fio baseline on that mount?

Neha Kapoor: did quick fio (seq write, 1 job):
- 32MB block: ~55MB/s
- 1MB block: ~40MB/s
Local disk was ~700MB/s.

Sean Gallagher: so NFS server is the bottleneck (or export options). still, we should set client mount options to best-known defaults.

Bruno Silva: agreed. for the helm chart NFS example, we can recommend mountOptions.

Fatima Noor: (on-prem side) we’ve also seen NFS hang when the server is synchronous + underlying disk is slow. customers sometimes run it on a NAS with tons of other workloads.

Neha Kapoor: I tried a few client-side tweaks and got it to a much better place.
Tested mounting directly on node:
1) baseline (above): ~35MB/s effective for backup end-to-end
2) set rsize/wsize=1048576: ~85-110MB/s
3) add nconnect=4 (kernel supports it here): ~140-180MB/s
Stalls on close were shorter but still there.

Colin O'Donnell: nconnect only works on newer kernels, but worth recommending as "if supported".

Sean Gallagher: we should also consider defaulting verify behavior for NFS. Reading back 30GB on NFS doubles runtime and pounds the server.

Bruno Silva: we can document: keep verify on for S3; for NFS, recommend `--verify=manifest` (checksum only local before upload?)

Miles Thornton: careful: if we skip read-back verification, we lose detection of server-side corruption. But realistically NFS corruption is rare; the bigger issue is we need a usable RTO.

Rafael Mendes: SRE take: make it a knob with a big warning. Default verify stays on, but docs for NFS: "consider disabling read-back verify if your NFS backend is slow; ensure periodic restore drills.".

Neha Kapoor: +1. Also recommend writing to local ephemeral disk then copying to NFS as one stream. It helps avoid NFS being in the hot path for the encryption/compression pipeline.

Bruno Silva: does it actually help if it’s still one big file?

Neha Kapoor: yes, because encryption step was CPU bursty and the write pattern was uneven. With local staging, we do steady copy to NFS, fewer pauses, and the final close/fsync stall is less noticeable.

Sean Gallagher: ok, so tuning options we should capture:
- mount options (rsize/wsize, nconnect)
- recommend dedicated export + server-side async if allowed
- recommend local staging + copy
- verify mode guidance

Colin O'Donnell: on the mount options, I’d propose:
- `vers=4.1` (or 4.2 if server supports)
- `proto=tcp`
- `hard` (don’t use soft; corrupt backups are worse than a stuck job)
- `timeo=600,retrans=2`
- `rsize=1048576,wsize=1048576`
- `noatime`
- `nconnect=4` (optional)

Bruno Silva: makes sense. do we want `actimeo` tweaks?

Neha Kapoor: I wouldn’t. It’s a single large file; attribute caching isn’t the issue. And making it too small adds metadata chatter.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Also note: k8s NFS volume plugin doesn’t always respect all mount options depending on CSI/driver. We should show both the "plain k8s" and "CSI" examples.

Bruno Silva: I’ll add an NFS example block to helm values + docs with "recommended defaults" + a comment about kernel support for nconnect.

Bruno Silva: draft snippet for docs (tell me if you hate it):

Bruno Silva: ```yaml
# values.yaml
backup:
  storage:
    backend: nfs
    nfs:
      server: 10.0.12.44
      path: /exports/redwood-backups
      # Recommended mount options for throughput / stability.
      # Note: nconnect requires Linux kernel support (>= 5.3-ish) and server support.
      mountOptions:
        - vers=4.1
        - proto=tcp
        - hard
        - timeo=600
        - retrans=2
        - rsize=1048576
        - wsize=1048576
        - noatime
        # - nconnect=4
```

Colin O'Donnell: looks good. add a sentence: "If your environment does not support these, leave unset; defaults are usually safe but slower."

Rafael Mendes: can we also add a quick troubleshooting checklist? e.g. run fio, check server load, check export options.

Neha Kapoor: I can paste what I used:
- `fio --name=seqwrite --directory=/mnt/nfs --rw=write --bs=4m --size=4g --numjobs=1 --iodepth=16 --direct=1`
- check server: `nfsiostat 1`, `nfsstat -s`, disk iowait
- watch client: `nfsstat -c`, `iostat -x 1`

Sean Gallagher: also: if backup close stalls for minutes, it’s usually server committing writes. recommend server-side `async` export if their risk tolerance allows (and they have battery-backed cache / stable storage).

Miles Thornton: +1 but we should phrase carefully. Some security folks will insist on sync.

Fatima Noor: for on-prem, we can phrase it as: "Prefer an enterprise NFS appliance or HA filer with adequate write cache; avoid single VM NFS unless you validate performance.".

Bruno Silva: ok. action items:
- I’ll update docs + helm example with mountOptions and tuning notes
- Neha can you drop the lab numbers in the Bruno edge-cases doc or a short note for the runbook appendix?

Neha Kapoor: yep will do. Also, strong recommendation: if customer chooses NFS, we should explicitly recommend periodic restore drills because perf varies wildly.

Sean Gallagher: +1. That’s the core message: NFS can be "supported" but you only know RTO after you test.

Bruno Silva: :thumbsup: I’ll post in docs thread once updated.

source.thread:
Vanessa Ortiz: pulling infra folks in — for Dedicated staged rollouts, we need to pick *which* GPU health signals count as a rollout gate (promotion blocker). ideas: ECC errors, node taints, throttling, etc. also need a data freshness requirement so gates don’t flap / lie when telemetry is stale.

Irene Choi: +1. we need something that’s (a) objective and (b) already in our fleet control loop. SRE is going to ask “what does unhealthy mean” and “how fresh is it”.

Logan Wright: from telemetry side we currently have 3-ish sources for “gpu health”:
- kube node conditions/taints (from fleet agent)
- DCGM exporter metrics (ECC, Xid, throttling reasons)
- our internal `fleet-healthd` aggregate (what scheduler uses to mark nodes unschedulable)

Caleb Johnson: DCGM is the most direct but also the noisiest depending on model/hardware gen. also some signals are counters w/ weird reset behavior on reboot.

Vanessa Ortiz: what do we trust for v1 gates? we need something we can explain to customers + is stable enough to be part of automated promotion.

Allison Grant: keep in mind: “gate” shouldn’t be 50 metrics. we’ll regret it. pick a small set with clear semantics + define staleness behavior.

Peter Holtz: SRE POV: hard block on “node is already deemed unhealthy by fleet” + “new critical hw faults appearing”. don’t block promotion on mild throttling unless it’s severe.

Mina Haddad: routing side: if we block promotion because a single host has a transient Xid blip, we’ll stall rollouts constantly. prefer gating on *rate* or *fraction of capacity* impacted.

Ravi Menon: +1. make it “% replicas healthy >= X” not “any unhealthy”. otherwise multi-region pools w/ 1 bad node will never move.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok, so: gate definition should probably operate on pool partition/replica set: e.g. “>= 99% GPUs healthy” for stage. question is: how do we compute “GPU healthy” deterministically.

Irene Choi: I’d push to use `fleet-healthd` as source of truth for “eligible”. it already accounts for node taints, drain/maintenance, and some GPU diag signals. it’s what protects customers today.

Logan Wright: fwiw `fleet-healthd` emits a per-node boolean and reason codes, but reason cardinality can be gross. we can expose a simplified rollup:
- `node_eligible=1|0`
- `in_maintenance=1|0`
- `gpu_fault_critical=1|0` (derived)

Caleb Johnson: for “critical hw faults”, I’d specifically include ECC double-bit errors (DBE) and Xid resets. for ECC single-bit (SBE) you’ll see background noise depending on load; not sure we want that as a gate.

Peter Holtz: agree: DBE is paging territory. SBE might be a ticket.

Vanessa Ortiz: do we have reliable DBE across fleet?

Caleb Johnson: yes via DCGM. metrics we have today (Prom-ish):
```
dcgm_fi_dev_ecc_dbe_agg_total
# (also sbe: dcgm_fi_dev_ecc_sbe_agg_total)
dcgm_fi_dev_xid_errors
```
DBE increments are rare but meaningful.

Allison Grant: question: do those counters reset on reboot? if so gate logic should be “delta in window” not “absolute > 0”.

Logan Wright: correct, counters can reset. policy engine should use `increase(metric[window])` semantics.

Mina Haddad: also: we need the gate to not block forever if the metric disappears (node gone, exporter down). what’s the staleness rule?

Vanessa Ortiz: yeah, data freshness. if DCGM exporter is down but node is otherwise eligible, do we fail the gate?

Peter Holtz: if we can’t observe health, safest is fail-closed (block promotion) *but* allow operator override. that’s consistent w/ other gate semantics.

Irene Choi: but fail-closed on *any* missing sample will be brutal during telemetry hiccups. can we tie it to `fleet-healthd` freshness? since that’s required for scheduling anyway.

Logan Wright: we can define:
- primary: `fleet-healthd` heartbeat freshness <= 2m
- secondary: DCGM freshness <= 5m (since exporter can lag)
If `fleet-healthd` is stale, we should treat health as unknown and block promotion.

Allison Grant: +1. we already page when fleet heartbeat is stale; it’s a real outage signal.

Caleb Johnson: note: throttling signals (nvml clock throttling / thermal) are *not* reliable for gating. lots of “throttling reason active” during normal operation. better to keep for dashboards.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok so maybe:
1) % eligible nodes (from fleet-healthd) >= threshold
2) no *new* ECC DBE in window
3) no *new* Xid errors in window
and throttling is informational.

Mina Haddad: would we gate on “% eligible nodes” computed per stage partition? bc if stage alloc is tiny (1 replica canary), % is binary.

Irene Choi: for small partitions, we need a minimum replica rule anyway (partitioning guidelines). but yes gate should be evaluated on the stage’s replica set.

Peter Holtz: for 1-replica canary, we probably *do* want binary. if that one GPU is unhealthy, canary is meaningless.

Ravi Menon: agreed. but the gate should be “eligible AND serving” not just “node not tainted”. can we use request success/latency as separate gates (already in rollout gating)?

Vanessa Ortiz: yep, latency/error gates are separate. this thread is just GPU health gate.

Logan Wright: data freshness proposal (numbers):
- fleet heartbeat max age: 120s
- dcgm metrics max age: 300s
If older than that -> gate status = UNKNOWN (and policy = block promote unless force-promote)

Allison Grant: pls make sure UI says “UNKNOWN (telemetry stale)” vs “FAILED” so folks don’t chase ghosts.

Caleb Johnson: +1 and for DBE/Xid we should define window and minimum samples. recommend 15m window for hardware fault counters so we don’t miss short spikes.

Peter Holtz: careful: 15m window means a single transient Xid will block promotion for 15m (until it ages out). that might be ok.

Mina Haddad: what about Xid severity? some Xids are benign-ish, some are “GPU fell off bus”. do we have that breakdown?

Caleb Johnson: DCGM `xid_errors` is aggregated; severity breakdown is in logs. we can’t reliably parse logs in gate eval v1.

Irene Choi: I’d still include Xid count increase as a “yellow” gate? or do we only block on DBE and rely on fleet eligibility for the rest.

Peter Holtz: if fleet marks node ineligible on bad Xids, that’s redundant. do we know if it does?

Vanessa Ortiz: fleet-healthd currently taints on repeated Xid and on GPU reset loops. single Xid may not taint. that’s why product asked for it.

Allison Grant: if we include Xid, keep threshold conservative: maybe allow 0 in window for canary stage only? else we’ll stall big rollouts due to one host log noise.

Mina Haddad: +1: make it stage-aware? e.g.
- stage <=10% traffic: Xid gate strict (0)
- stage >10%: only block if Xid increase impacts >N% replicas

Vanessa Ortiz: that’s getting complex but maybe necessary. otherwise one node in a 200-GPU pool will keep blocking.

Irene Choi: simplest: evaluate Xid gate as “fraction of replicas with xid increase >0 in window <= 1%” (rounded up). so large pools tolerate a tiny bit.

Logan Wright: doable if we have per-node metrics + stable node IDs in labels. but cardinality risk.

Allison Grant: we can do per-node internally for gate eval without exposing it broadly. dashboard templates should stay aggregated.

Caleb Johnson: labeling: use `node_id` (uuid) not hostname. hostnames churn.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok. last thing: node taints — which taints count?

Irene Choi: for v1 gate, just treat `fleet-healthd` eligible=false as fail. don’t enumerate taints in policy.

Peter Holtz: yes. keep it “eligible” as the abstraction.

Vanessa Ortiz: summary proposal (please poke holes):
- Gate A (Fleet eligibility): stage replica set has >= (1 - max_unhealthy_fraction) eligible nodes. default max_unhealthy_fraction=0 for <=10 replicas, else 1%.
- Gate B (ECC DBE): `increase(dcgm_fi_dev_ecc_dbe_agg_total[15m]) == 0` across stage replicas (any DBE => fail).
- Gate C (Xid): fraction of replicas with `increase(dcgm_fi_dev_xid_errors[15m]) > 0` <= 1% (or 0 for small stages).
- Freshness: fleet heartbeat age <= 120s, dcgm metric age <= 300s else gate=UNKNOWN and block promotion unless force-promote.
- Throttling: dashboards only, not a gate.

Mina Haddad: looks good. only add: for multi-region pools, evaluate per region and take worst region? otherwise a bad region will be masked by healthy region.

Irene Choi: yes per-region evaluation. routing already splits by region capacity, so gating should too.

Logan Wright: I’ll open a telemetry task to ensure we can compute “metric age” and surface it in gate evidence payload (so Console shows why UNKNOWN).

Allison Grant: and please add an annotation/event when gating is UNKNOWN due to staleness. helps with postmortems.

Vanessa Ortiz: 👍 I’ll write this up in partitioning guidelines + send to Paula/Jordan for the policy spec. Irene can you sanity check the max_unhealthy defaults w/ fleet constraints?

Irene Choi: yep, will do. also will confirm fleet-healthd heartbeat SLA (I think it’s 60s, so 120s stale cutoff is reasonable).

Peter Holtz: thanks. this will make oncall life better vs “random gpu is sad” gates.

Caleb Johnson: if you need example queries for DBE/Xid w/ increase() and handling counter resets, ping me.

Vanessa Ortiz: will do. closing loop once I have the writeup + thresholds.

source.messages:
Vanessa Ortiz: dropping this here for review before we lock defaults for GA. Need alignment on (1) fleet safety caps for burst pools and (2) global circuit breaker behavior. goal is: bursting never drags non-burst dedicated SLOs.

Vanessa Ortiz: current proposal (v3):
- Per-pool soft guardrail at 0.80 saturation (start increasing deny % for *new* burst requests)
- Per-pool hard CB at 0.92 saturation for >=2m OR queue_depth >= 300 for >=60s
- Global fleet CB if ANY region/hw pool trips hard CB AND neighbor impact indicators show regression (more below)

Vanessa Ortiz: numbers we need to agree on:
- saturation thresholds: 0.80 / 0.92
- queue depth: 300
- CB open duration / cooldown: 10m open, then half-open with 10% admit for 5m

Keiko Yamamoto: +1 on having both saturation and queue depth. saturation alone lags when telemetry is stale.

Keiko Yamamoto: Q: what are we defining as saturation here? (allocated_burst_gpus / burst_pool_size) or (in_use_gpus / burst_pool_size)? those behave differently w/ warm standby.

Vanessa Ortiz: good call. definition in fleet telemetry is `in_use_gpus / total_gpus` for the pool, sampled every 5s. `allocated` is more control-plane-ish and can overshoot.

Harish Reddy: for procurement: we can’t assume burst pool size is always stable. in eu-west we’ll be running thinner for a while. would prefer soft guardrail starts earlier there.

Vanessa Ortiz: we can parameterize by region/hw class. but want sane global defaults.

Peter Holtz: from routing/SRE view: we need deterministic behavior + quick fail closed. if pool is near the edge we should stop admitting burst before we see tail amplification.

Peter Holtz: can we add a second hard trigger: non-burst p95 latency delta > +15% for 3m => open CB regardless of saturation?

Sean Gallagher: +1. SLO is the thing we’re protecting. if we wait for saturation to hit 0.92 we’ve already hurt someone.

Sean Gallagher: suggested neighbor impact gate:
- `nonburst_p95_latency_ratio` (current / 7d baseline) > 1.15 for 3 out of 5 mins
- OR `nonburst_429_rate` > 0.5% for 2 mins
then auto-open CB for that pool for 15 mins.

Allison Grant: metric-wise we can do that. question is label cardinality: we can calculate per dedicated “cell” / pool, but not per-tenant without exploding.

Jordan Lee: also need to be careful with feedback loops. if latency bumps due to unrelated deploy, CB trips and we shut off burst everywhere, then customers complain.

Jordan Lee: propose we require *both* (a) pool pressure signal AND (b) neighbor impact to open the *global* CB. but allow *per-pool* CB on pool pressure alone.

Mina Haddad: +1. global CB should be rare. per-pool CB should be immediate and deterministic.

Ravi Menon: re determinism: admission controller is making decisions off fleet telemetry; if we mix latency signals (which are delayed + aggregated) we need to codify timing windows.

Ravi Menon: recommendation:
- Per-pool CB: pressure-based only (saturation/queue/telemetry lag)
- Global CB: requires pressure + neighbor impact, and opens for 10m, evaluated every 60s

Vanessa Ortiz: ok, so global CB acts like “stop bursting anywhere in region/hw class” vs per-pool is just that pool.

Peter Holtz: yeah. and when global CB is open, routing should behave like: treat burst capacity as 0, return denial reason `FLEET_SAFETY_GLOBAL_CB` (not “cap reached”). helps support.

Harish Reddy: can we keep it “region/hw class” scoped for now? i’d rather not have us-east H100 issue shut off eu-west L40S.

Vanessa Ortiz: agreed. global CB scope = region + hw class.

Vanessa Ortiz: adding third trigger being discussed in other threads: telemetry lag. we saw this in staging.

Keiko Yamamoto: yes please. if telemetry_lag spikes, admission can over-admit. hard trigger should include `telemetry_lag_seconds > 15` for >=60s.

Jordan Lee: +1. and make CB open duration longer than lag window, otherwise we oscillate.

Sean Gallagher: for paging: CB opening should page only if it stays open > 20m, otherwise it’s just doing its job. we can alert on “CB flapping” separately.

Allison Grant: numbers proposal (based on our beta graphs):
- soft deny ramp: start at 75% saturation, linear ramp to 100% deny at 90%
- hard CB: saturation >= 90% for 2m OR queue_depth >= 500 for 60s OR telemetry_lag >= 15s for 60s

Vanessa Ortiz: that’s different from 0.80/0.92 and queue 300.

Allison Grant: yeah. 300 queue is too low on H100 with continuous batching; you can hit 300 transiently during a GC pause and recover.

Peter Holtz: +1. we saw queue blips in load tests. 500 for 60s seems more meaningful.

Keiko Yamamoto: i’m ok with soft starting at 75%. earlier backpressure is safer and reduces chance of cliff.

Harish Reddy: earlier soft gate helps the thin pools too (eu-west). we can still override per region if needed.

Jordan Lee: on ramp shape: linear is fine but we should keep it deterministic given same telemetry sample. (no random). so “deny if hash(req_id) % 100 < deny_percent” is ok.

Ravi Menon: +1, deterministic sampling based on request_id / tenant_id. we should document that.

Sean Gallagher: neighbor impact threshold: i’d prefer +20% p95 (not +15%) to reduce false positives. but with requirement that pressure is also high, +15% is probably ok.

Sean Gallagher: propose neighbor gate =
- (nonburst_p95_ratio > 1.15 AND nonburst_p99_ratio > 1.20) for 3/5 mins
- OR nonburst_5xx_rate > 0.2% for 2 mins

Peter Holtz: that’s good. p95 alone can shift due to mix.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok summarizing consensus so far:
1) Saturation definition = `in_use_gpus / pool_size` sampled 5s
2) Soft guardrail: start at 0.75, ramp denies to full deny at 0.90
3) Hard per-pool CB triggers (any):
   - saturation >= 0.90 for 2 mins
   - OR queue_depth >= 500 for 60s
   - OR telemetry_lag_seconds >= 15 for 60s
4) Per-pool CB open duration 10 mins, then half-open at 10% admit for 5 mins (if still under 0.85 saturation)
5) “Global” CB scope = region + hw class, requires (pool pressure) AND (neighbor impact gate), opens 10 mins

Vanessa Ortiz: open question: should we also have an absolute fleet safety cap like “burst can never consume > X% of total dedicated GPUs in region/hw” even if pool is oversized?

Keiko Yamamoto: yes. otherwise we risk sizing mistakes. i’d set X=25% of total dedicated GPUs for that region/hw. (i.e. burst pools can’t be >0.25 * baseline dedicated).

Harish Reddy: 25% might be too high for early regions. but as a cap it’s fine; we likely run 10–15%.

Jordan Lee: careful: cap on pool size is one thing, cap on admitted burst at runtime is another. runtime cap should be based on pool_size anyway. but procurement cap is helpful.

Vanessa Ortiz: i meant runtime “fleet safety cap” = regardless of pool, don’t admit more than 20% of *total* GPUs in that region/hw to bursting at once. defense in depth.

Peter Holtz: i like that. it prevents weirdness where pool_size is accidentally set huge.

Allison Grant: implementable as another pressure signal: `burst_in_use_gpus / (dedicated_total_gpus_region_hw)`.

Sean Gallagher: set it conservative: 15% for GA, we can raise later. and make it configurable.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1 15% GA. we can revisit after we have 30d data.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok: runtime fleet safety cap default = 0.15 (15%) of total dedicated GPUs per region/hw.

FleetBot: FYI `eu-west / H100` supply risk flag = HIGH (buffer recommendation +5%). see capacity note: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/FLEET/eu-west-h100-forecast (internal)

Harish Reddy: lol bot is listening. but yeah, for HIGH risk regions we should start soft ramp at 0.70 maybe.

Vanessa Ortiz: let’s keep one global default for GA and allow region overrides via config. default ramp start 0.75; eu-west override start 0.70.

Jordan Lee: please document in policy engine as:
- `soft_start_saturation`
- `soft_full_deny_saturation`
- `hard_cb_saturation`
- `hard_cb_queue_depth`
- `hard_cb_telemetry_lag_seconds`
- `fleet_safety_cap_ratio`
so we can change without redeploy.

Vanessa Ortiz: ack. i’ll update the config doc + open a PR comment on ENG-4902.

Peter Holtz: also please make sure denial reasons distinguish:
- SOFT_DENY_POOL_PRESSURE
- HARD_CB_POOL_OPEN
- FLEET_SAFETY_CAP
- GLOBAL_CB_OPEN
support will need this.

Sean Gallagher: +1 and make CB open/close events emit to audit/event stream for postmortems.

Allison Grant: we can emit `burst_cb_state{scope=pool|region_hw, state=open|half_open|closed}` + event annotation.

Vanessa Ortiz: cool. i’m calling this settled unless objections in next 24h. i’ll paste final thresholds into the design doc and tag @Ethan/@Jordan for config wiring.

Keiko Yamamoto: :thumbsup:

Peter Holtz: :+1: and pls route the “half-open 10%” traffic using deterministic hashing so customers don’t see random flaps.

Jordan Lee: yep, can do `hash(tenant_id) < threshold` style.

Harish Reddy: thanks all. i’ll note the eu-west override in the rollout plan.

Sean Gallagher: +1, and for GA checklist we’ll add: verify CB alerts + simulate open/close in game day.

Vanessa Ortiz: done. will follow up w/ updated numbers in confluence + link here.

FleetBot: FYI `eu-west / H100` supply risk flag = HIGH (buffer recommendation +5%). see capacity note: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/FLEET/eu-west-h100-forecast (internal)

Harish Reddy: lol bot is listening. but yeah, for HIGH risk regions we should start soft ramp at 0.70 maybe.

Vanessa Ortiz: let’s keep one global default for GA and allow region overrides via config. default ramp start 0.75; eu-west override start 0.70.

Jordan Lee: please document in policy engine as:
- `soft_start_saturation`
- `soft_full_deny_saturation`
- `hard_cb_saturation`
- `hard_cb_queue_depth`
- `hard_cb_telemetry_lag_seconds`
- `fleet_safety_cap_ratio`
so we can change without redeploy.

Vanessa Ortiz: ack. i’ll update the config doc + open a PR comment on ENG-4902.

Peter Holtz: also please make sure denial reasons distinguish:
- SOFT_DENY_POOL_PRESSURE
- HARD_CB_POOL_OPEN
- FLEET_SAFETY_CAP
- GLOBAL_CB_OPEN
support will need this.

Sean Gallagher: +1 and make CB open/close events emit to audit/event stream for postmortems.

Allison Grant: we can emit `burst_cb_state{scope=pool|region_hw, state=open|half_open|closed}` + event annotation.

Vanessa Ortiz: cool. i’m calling this settled unless objections in next 24h. i’ll paste final thresholds into the design doc and tag @Ethan/@Jordan for config wiring.

Keiko Yamamoto: :thumbsup:

Peter Holtz: :+1: and pls route the “half-open 10%” traffic using deterministic hashing so customers don’t see random flaps.

Jordan Lee: yep, can do `hash(tenant_id) < threshold` style.

Harish Reddy: thanks all. i’ll note the eu-west override in the rollout plan.

Sean Gallagher: +1, and for GA checklist we’ll add: verify CB alerts + simulate open/close in game day.

Vanessa Ortiz: done. will follow up w/ updated numbers in confluence + link here.

source.messages:
Vanessa Ortiz: seeing something weird in beta pool partitioning. canary stage went live (stage=1%) and queue depth spiked hard + 429s. looks like canary partition is starving capacity.

Vanessa Ortiz: pool: `dedicated-pool/lat-emb-prod` (beta), region `us-east-1`, reservation 8xH100. stages: 1%/10%/50%/100%. canary got 1 replica.

Irene Choi: do we have a graph? is this just bursty traffic or sustained? also did they have streaming on?

Vanessa Ortiz: grafana link (internal): https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/dedicated-rollout-stage?var-pool=lat-emb-prod&from=now-1h

Vanessa Ortiz: tl;dr at 22:11 UTC, stage promoted to canary. within ~2 min:
- canary `queue_depth_p95` jumps from ~0-3 -> 220+
- canary `request_drop_rate` to 2-5%
- overall pool 429s from ~0.1% -> 1.6% (mostly canary routed)

Peter Holtz: that’s consistent w/ tiny partition + sticky routing. if the canary partition has 1 replica and we route 1% of all traffic, it should be fine *unless* we’re pinning more than intended (sessions/streams) or the traffic split isn’t actually 1%.

Mina Haddad: checking routing logs. we do stage selection at request start, but streaming requests stay pinned to the selected partition (expected). if a client does long streams, it can occupy that partition longer.

Mina Haddad: also: we have a “min 1 replica” for canary, but no guardrail around request shape.

Ravi Menon: quick thought: in Dedicated, “1% traffic” is misleading if request cost distribution is heavy-tailed. 1% of *requests* can be >>1% of *tokens*. did this customer have long context?

Vanessa Ortiz: yep. from their TAM notes, they send 16k context spikes when they re-index. and the canary partition was assigned the newest runtime build (more aggressive batching), so per-request latency might be higher at low concurrency.

Logan Wright: do we have stage-tagged `tokens_in` / `tokens_out` yet? or are we looking at request count only.

Caleb Johnson: stage tagging is partially on. i can query `tokens_out_total{stage_id=canary}` but it’s spotty (some pods missing label).

Vikram Singh: data plane note: when we carve a 1-GPU partition, it’s basically a single queue with continuous batching. if it gets one or two pathological long sequences, it can block smaller ones behind it (HOL). at small N, batching efficiency tanks too.

Emily Zhao: can we confirm how the 1 replica was selected? was it “strict partition” or “shared pool with weighted routing”? if strict, then any spillover is 429.

Mina Haddad: strict partition. routing policy picks partition key; no spillover unless fallback triggers (and fallback is disabled during rollout unless abort). so yeah, stage=canary is hard-isolated.

Rafael Mendes: :eyes: so the canary stage isolation is causing self-inflicted outage for that slice. do we have an auto-abort condition based on queue depth / saturation?

Peter Holtz: not yet for queue depth. gates are mostly error rate + p95 latency + GPU util. but with 1 replica, p95 is noisy + you can get weird partial failures.

Vanessa Ortiz: adding logs snippet. from canary pod `dp-dedicated-rt-7f4c`:
```
2025-02-06T22:13:18.991Z WARN scheduler queue_depth=241 inflight=128 max_inflight=128 stage=canary pool=lat-emb-prod
2025-02-06T22:13:20.102Z WARN admission_control drop reason="queue_full" http_status=429 stage=canary
2025-02-06T22:13:20.103Z INFO request attrs="model=llama-3.1-70b" prompt_tokens=15233 max_tokens=2048 stream=true stage=canary
2025-02-06T22:13:21.447Z WARN gpu oom_count_5m=2 stage=canary
```

Irene Choi: so canary got the worst-case requests (either by chance or by some routing stickiness). with 1 replica, it’s dead.

Ravi Menon: do we have any routing stickiness by org/user that could bias traffic? e.g., consistent hashing by customer_id means a small stage might get “hot” customers disproportionately.

Mina Haddad: yep. for Dedicated we hash on `(account_id, route_id, request_group)` to keep cache locality. if canary partition is selected first for a given hash bucket, it can get consistent load until stage changes.

Peter Holtz: that’s the smoking gun. with 1 replica canary, you can accidentally concentrate a large bucket.

Vikram Singh: +1. traffic % split assumes iid requests. consistent hashing breaks that assumption.

Vanessa Ortiz: so proposed guardrail ideas:
1) enforce min canary partition size >= 2 replicas (or >= 20% for small pools)
2) stage selection should be token-weighted or at least “cost-aware” if we’re going to use hashing
3) allow spillover to stable partition when canary is saturated (but then it’s not a strict test)

Mina Haddad: (3) is tricky for rollback semantics + audit. but we could do “soft isolation” w/ explicit policy: canary preferred, stable fallback on saturation. and still measure canary outcomes.

Rafael Mendes: for beta, we need something fast and safe. i’d take (1) + (gate on queue depth / drops) as immediate.

Logan Wright: i can add a gate signal: `queue_depth_p95` and `admission_drop_rate` per stage. but we need staleness handling.

Caleb Johnson: also we should stop promoting into a stage if sample size is tiny. “min_requests” gate would prevent promoting a 1-replica stage with 5 req/min.

Emily Zhao: infra-side: we should codify in partitioning guidelines: for pools < 10 GPUs, don’t do 1% stage at all. default stages should be replica-based not % based.

Irene Choi: aligns with the ADR discussion (replica partitions for small pools). can we change defaults for Dedicated? (1/10/50/100) doesn’t work for 8 GPUs.

Vanessa Ortiz: agree. for 8 GPUs, stages should be something like 2/4/6/8 replicas or 2/4/8.

Mina Haddad: routing can support both, but we need a rule: if pool_size < N, interpret stages as replica counts; or clamp % to minimum replica count.

Peter Holtz: propose algorithm:
- compute desired replicas = ceil(pool_size * stage_percent)
- clamp to min_replicas_per_stage (2 for canary)
- also require remaining stable replicas >= min_headroom (e.g., 2)
- if not possible, reject plan or suggest alternate stages

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. and also: if using consistent hashing, make stage assignment per request not per bucket, or add salt so buckets distribute. but that will hurt cache locality.

Ravi Menon: maybe keep hashing but hash includes stage_id when stage is < X replicas, so it reshuffles buckets as you promote? you lose some cache but avoid hot bucket.

Mina Haddad: i can add a “small partition mode” where we disable deterministic hashing and do pure weighted random for stage selection. probably ok for canary.

Incident Bot: incident created: INC-2025-02-06-219 "Dedicated rollout canary partition saturation" (severity: SEV-2) https://inc.redwood.internal/INC-2025-02-06-219

Rafael Mendes: action for now: abort rollout on that pool? canary is failing.

Vanessa Ortiz: already told oncall to hold at current stage and route back to stable (manual override). customer is ok.

Mina Haddad: i’ll paste the routing diff showing the stage selection behavior (from `routingd`):
```
// pseudo
partition = consistent_hash(account_id, route_id) % num_partitions
if rollout_enabled:
  stage = rollout_stage_for_partition(partition)
```
so yeah, if one bucket maps to canary, it’s sticky.

Peter Holtz: ok. guardrail: for canary stage, don’t map whole hash buckets. do per-request selection.

Logan Wright: also should alert on canary stage saturation separately. right now overall pool dashboards hide it.

Irene Choi: can we get a PR quickly to clamp canary to >=2 replicas + disable sticky hashing on canary?

Mina Haddad: i can do clamp in routing policy config + add `stage_selection_mode=weighted_random` for canary when replicas < 3. will open PR today.

Vanessa Ortiz: i’ll update infra partitioning guideline doc: “no 1-replica canary partitions” + “small pools use replica-based stages”.

Rafael Mendes: and SRE wants runbook note: if queue_depth spikes in canary, abort/rollback immediately; don’t wait for latency gate (it’ll be noisy).

Caleb Johnson: telemetry fix: stage tag mismatch mentioned earlier. i’ll sync w/ Logan; we need `stage_id` consistently in both router + runtime metrics.

Logan Wright: cool. i’ll add a short-term workaround query that matches either label (`stage` or `stage_id`) until we roll out the fix.

Irene Choi: summary so we don’t lose the thread:
- root cause: 1-replica canary + strict isolation + consistent hashing => hot bucket => queue depth + drops
- mitigations (fast): clamp canary min replicas=2; canary stage selection weighted-random; add gate/alerts on queue depth + drop rate; update guidelines for small pools

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: i’ll post follow-ups in the incident and link to this thread.

Mina Haddad: assigning myself routing changes; will link PR once up.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. please tag me on the PR so we can validate with a replay test before re-enabling beta.

- mitigations (fast): clamp canary min replicas=2; canary stage selection weighted-random; add gate/alerts on queue depth + drop rate; update guidelines for small pools

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: i’ll post follow-ups in the incident and link to this thread.

Mina Haddad: assigning myself routing changes; will link PR once up.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. please tag me on the PR so we can validate with a replay test before re-enabling beta.

source.text:
Vanessa Ortiz: trying to lock baseline metrics for “time-to-ready” before we build the new state machine. need alignment on: what exact timestamp = “allocated” and what exact timestamp = “ready”. also: what’s the source of truth so we don’t have 3 different numbers floating around.

Miles Thornton: +1. for SLI we need start/end events that are (a) consistent across regions/GPU types, (b) collectible today (even if messy), and (c) same semantics we’ll use post-sprint.

Aisha Bello: if we can’t get the canonical events yet, I’d rather define the SLI now and do “best-effort backfill” than pick something like terraform apply time.

Keiko Yamamoto: please not terraform apply time 🙃 that’s gonna include human delays + CI queueing + retries. also apply can be rerun.

Jamal Pierce: from capacity ops POV, “allocated” is basically when we see instance IDs created + tagged to the pool. but depending on cloud, the instance can be “created” and still stuck in pending for a while.

Vanessa Ortiz: right. my original thought was:
- start = EC2 RunInstances response time OR when instance appears in fleet inventory
- end = node is Ready in k8s
but “Ready in k8s” != “ready for inference” (driver mismatch, etc).

Rafael Mendes: for baseline (pre-gates), “ready for inference” is fuzzy. but we can split into two:
1) infra-ready: kubelet joined + NodeReady true
2) serving-ready: node added to serving pool / schedulable for inference

Harish Reddy: current manual checklist basically ends when the node shows up in the pool and can take a canary request. we do sometimes mark nodes schedulable and then find out drivers are off.

Benji Okafor: from control plane side, we *don’t* currently have a single “serving-ready” event persisted. we have:
- cluster join (from kube api)
- pool membership changes (from fleet tooling)
- some logs in provisioning orchestrator

Jordan Lee: the ADR we’re drafting uses Allocated/Provisioning/Validating/Ready. for baseline, we can define Allocated = “instance created + recorded in fleet inventory” and Ready = “node admitted to serving pool” (or “marked ready” in fleet tool).

Ethan Park: question: do we have a DB field today for “added to serving pool”? if not, baseline might need to use kube node label/taint changes as a proxy.

Allison Grant: reminder: whatever we pick needs to be doable as an SLI later (events or metrics). also please avoid per-node high-cardinality tags in the interim dashboards.

Sergio Costa: we can compute baseline from logs if we standardize a correlation key (instance_id / node_name). but if start is cloudtrail and end is kube, it’s gonna be brittle.

Vanessa Ortiz: what do we have *reliably* right now?
- EC2 instance LaunchTime
- our fleet inventory service writes a record when it discovers the instance (poller)
- kube node creation time
- kube NodeReady condition lastTransitionTime
- pool assignment is via labels like `redwood.ai/pool-id` (I think?)

Keiko Yamamoto: the poller timestamp (fleet inventory record created_at) is probably closer to “allocated” in our system than EC2 LaunchTime. LaunchTime requires AWS API + permissions and can differ by provider.

Miles Thornton: I’d prefer start = “we know about it” (inventory created_at), since that reflects our operational latency too (discovery). BUT the sprint goal is “provision-to-ready” which should start at allocation (customer-visible lead time). discovery delay might be part of that? depends on messaging.

Jamal Pierce: for Dedicated, customers feel it from the moment we accept the capacity expansion request. but we don’t have a single timestamp for that either.

Talia Benson: for sprint reporting, Irene/Neha asked for “time from capacity allocated to ready for workloads” (their words). i’d avoid “request accepted” because that’s more GTM/ops process.

Aisha Bello: can we standardize start = “cloud instance created” where available, and fall back to inventory created_at where not? (and document the fallback)

Rafael Mendes: I’d rather pick one canonical definition even if imperfect. if we do multi-source fallbacks, we’ll spend the whole sprint arguing about deltas.

Allison Grant: +1. pick one now, then in the new system emit a dedicated “allocated_at” event from the orchestrator.

Benji Okafor: proposal for baseline:
- allocated_ts = fleet inventory record created_at (when instance_id appears + has pool tags)
- ready_ts = first time kube node is NodeReady=true AND has pool label AND is not tainted `NoSchedule` (or whatever we use)
this approximates “eligible to serve”.

Harish Reddy: caveat: we sometimes remove NoSchedule before drivers fully verified. but baseline will reflect reality today (including that footgun).

Keiko Yamamoto: on labels/taints: for Dedicated pools we do:
- label `redwood.ai/dedicated-pool=<pool_id>`
- taint `redwood.ai/provisioning=true:NoSchedule` until we flip it
at least in us-east.

Sergio Costa: if that’s consistent, we can compute ready_ts from kube events. also: NodeReady condition can flap; use first time it becomes Ready *and stays* for N minutes?

Miles Thornton: for SLI, we usually define end as “entered Ready state” (first transition), not “stayed ready”. but we can add a “stability” metric later.

Rafael Mendes: for baseline, keep it simple: first transition to Ready + taint removed. if it flaps later, that’s a different SLI.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok, so sounds like:
START (“allocated”): fleet inventory created_at for instance_id with pool tags
END (“ready”): kube node has NodeReady=true AND provisioning taint removed AND pool label present
source of truth for baseline: derived query (inventory DB + kube API)

Jordan Lee: + document that this is baseline only. post-sprint, source of truth becomes Fleet Readiness Service state transitions (Allocated->Ready) persisted in control plane.

Ethan Park: works for me. we should add a note that kube timestamps are from api-server; inventory timestamps from our DB. clock skew risk is low but non-zero.

Aisha Bello: I can draft the metric definition as:
`time_to_ready_seconds = ready_ts - allocated_ts`
Dimensions: region, gpu_type, pool_id (maybe pool_id only in logs, not in metrics)

Allison Grant: please no pool_id as a metric label. region + gpu_type + step is ok. pool_id can be a filter in logs/traces.

Sergio Costa: +1. we can keep pool_id in the spreadsheet/backfill job output, but dashboard metrics should aggregate.

Jamal Pierce: can we also capture P50/P95 by GPU type (A100/H100/L4 etc) and by region? that’s what capacity wants.

Vanessa Ortiz: yep. i’ll update the baseline sheet with these definitions + note gaps. also adding action item: Keiko + Sergio to confirm taint/label names in each region.

Keiko Yamamoto: i’ll sanity check labels/taints in us-east + eu-west today and report back.

Sergio Costa: I’ll put together a quick query/script outline. if kube event scraping is gross, we can do it via the kube API server and dump timestamps.

Rafael Mendes: once we have baseline numbers, SRE can propose initial SLO thresholds (even if we don’t page on them yet).

Vanessa Ortiz: cool. locking this unless strong objections. :thumbsup:

Aisha Bello: :thumbsup: (and +1 on documenting that “ready” here is “schedulable” not “passed validations”).

Allison Grant: please paste the exact taint key + label keys into the doc once confirmed. saves us a lot of drift later.
